# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a spreadsheet. List any four built-in statistical functions of MS Excel with their syntax.**

**Model Answer:**

A **spreadsheet** is an electronic worksheet that organizes data into a grid of rows and columns, allowing users to perform arithmetic, logical, and statistical computations through formulas and functions. It is widely used in the food industry for quality control, production tracking, and nutritional data analysis.

**Four Built-in Statistical Functions:**

| Function | Syntax | Description |
|----------|--------|-------------|
| AVERAGE | `=AVERAGE(range)` | Computes the arithmetic mean of the range |
| STDEV | `=STDEV(range)` | Computes the sample standard deviation |
| MAX | `=MAX(range)` | Returns the maximum numeric value |
| COUNTIF | `=COUNTIF(range, criteria)` | Counts cells meeting a specified condition |

**Example Application in Food Industry:** To find the average protein content of 10 batches, the formula `=AVERAGE(B2:B11)` is used.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between Relative and Absolute cell referencing in spreadsheets with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Relative Reference | Absolute Reference |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Cell address that shifts when formula is copied | Cell address that remains fixed when copied |
| Symbol | `A1` (no dollar sign) | `$A$1` (dollar before both row & column) |
| Behavior | Adjusts based on new position | Locked to original position |
| Use Case | Calculating totals across rows | Applying a fixed tax/discount rate |

**Example:**

Relative: In cell `C1`, formula `=A1+B1`. When copied to `C2`, it becomes `=A2+B2`.

Absolute: In cell `C1`, formula `=A1*$B$1`. When copied to `C2`, it becomes `=A2*$B$1` (B1 is locked).

**Food Industry Application:** When computing the total cost of multiple recipes, the tax rate of 5% stored in `$B$1` is referenced as absolute, while the ingredient prices vary relatively.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: A food quality lab recorded the moisture content (%) of 8 batches of biscuits as follows: 12.5, 13.0, 12.8, 13.2, 12.6, 12.9, 13.1, 12.7. Answer the following:**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Using MS Excel, write the stepwise procedure to compute the **mean**, **median**, **standard deviation**, **variance**, **minimum**, and **maximum** of this dataset. Provide the **Excel formulas** and the **final computed values**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the IF function, classify each batch as **"PASS"** if moisture is between **12.5% and 13.0%**, otherwise **"FAIL"**. Write the formula, compute results, and construct a **bar chart** showing the number of PASS vs. FAIL batches.

### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Enter data into cells `B2` through `B9` (8 values).

**Step 2:** Identify the formula for mean:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i $$

**Step 3:** Sum all values:

$$ \sum = 12.5 + 13.0 + 12.8 + 13.2 + 12.6 + 12.9 + 13.1 + 12.7 = 102.8 $$

**Step 4:** Mean calculation:

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{102.8}{8} = 12.85 \, \% $$

**Step 5:** Excel formulas and computed results:

| Statistic | Excel Formula | Result |
|-----------|---------------|--------|
| Mean | `=AVERAGE(B2:B9)` | 12.85 |
| Median | `=MEDIAN(B2:B9)` | 12.85 |
| Standard Deviation | `=STDEV(B2:B9)` | 0.239 |
| Variance | `=VAR(B2:B9)` | 0.0571 |
| Minimum | `=MIN(B2:B9)` | 12.5 |
| Maximum | `=MAX(B2:B9)` | 13.2 |

### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Write the IF formula in cell `C2`:

$$ \texttt{=IF(AND(B2>=12.5, B2<=13.0), "PASS", "FAIL")} $$

**Step 2:** Drag the formula from `C2` to `C9` to classify all batches.

**Step 3:** Computed classification results:

| Batch | Moisture (%) | Result |
|-------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 12.5 | PASS |
| 2 | 13.0 | PASS |
| 3 | 12.8 | PASS |
| 4 | 13.2 | FAIL |
| 5 | 12.6 | PASS |
| 6 | 12.9 | PASS |
| 7 | 13.1 | FAIL |
| 8 | 12.7 | PASS |

**Step 4:** Count PASS and FAIL using `=COUNTIF(C2:C9,"PASS")` and `=COUNTIF(C2:C9,"FAIL")`.

$$ \text{PASS count} = 6, \quad \text{FAIL count} = 2 $$

**Step 5:** Bar Chart Procedure:

- Select cells containing PASS and FAIL counts.
- Navigate to `Insert > Chart > Bar Chart`.
- Add chart title: "Batch Quality Classification".
- Add X-axis label: "Category", Y-axis label: "Number of Batches".

> [!NOTE]
> **Outcome:** 6 batches PASS, 2 batches FAIL (75% pass rate). The bar chart visually communicates this proportion to management.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: A food company wants to analyze the correlation between storage temperature (°C) and shelf-life (days) of a packaged fruit juice. The recorded data is:**

| Temperature (°C) | Shelf-Life (days) |
|------------------|-------------------|
| 5 | 60 |
| 10 | 45 |
| 15 | 32 |
| 20 | 22 |
| 25 | 15 |
| 30 | 10 |
| 35 | 6 |

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the **Pearson Correlation Coefficient** using spreadsheet formulas. Show stepwise derivation and interpret the result.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Construct a **Scatter Plot** with trendline. Write the linear regression equation and predict the shelf-life at **40°C**.

### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1:** Enter Temperature data in cells `A2:A8` and Shelf-Life data in cells `B2:B8`.

**Step 2:** Compute the mean of Temperature ($\bar{x}$) and Shelf-Life ($\bar{y}$).

$$ \bar{x} = \frac{5 + 10 + 15 + 20 + 25 + 30 + 35}{7} = \frac{140}{7} = 20 \, °C $$

$$ \bar{y} = \frac{60 + 45 + 32 + 22 + 15 + 10 + 6}{7} = \frac{190}{7} \approx 27.14 \, \text{days} $$

**Step 3:** Compute deviations $(x_i - \bar{x})$, $(y_i - \bar{y})$, their products, and squares.

| $x_i$ | $y_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $y_i - \bar{y}$ | Product | $(x_i - \bar{x})^2$ | $(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ |
|-------|-------|------------------|------------------|---------|--------------------|--------------------|
| 5 | 60 | -15 | 32.86 | -492.86 | 225 | 1079.43 |
| 10 | 45 | -10 | 17.86 | -178.57 | 100 | 318.86 |
| 15 | 32 | -5 | 4.86 | -24.29 | 25 | 23.59 |
| 20 | 22 | 0 | -5.14 | 0.00 | 0 | 26.45 |
| 25 | 15 | 5 | -12.14 | -60.71 | 25 | 147.45 |
| 30 | 10 | 10 | -17.14 | -171.43 | 100 | 293.88 |
| 35 | 6 | 15 | -21.14 | -317.14 | 225 | 446.88 |

**Step 4:** Sum the products and squared deviations.

$$ \sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y}) = -1244.99 $$

$$ \sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 = 700 $$

$$ \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2 = 2336.55 $$

**Step 5:** Apply Pearson Correlation Coefficient formula.

$$ r = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sqrt{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2 \cdot \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}} $$

$$ r = \frac{-1244.99}{\sqrt{700 \times 2336.55}} $$

$$ r = \frac{-1244.99}{\sqrt{1,635,585}} $$

$$ r = \frac{-1244.99}{1278.9} $$

$$ r \approx -0.973 $$

**Step 6:** Excel verification formula:

$$ \texttt{=CORREL(A2:A8, B2:B8)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \texttt{Result: -0.973} $$

**Step 7:** Interpretation:

$$ r \approx -0.97 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Strong negative correlation} $$

**Conclusion:** As storage temperature increases, shelf-life decreases sharply. This is consistent with **Arrhenius kinetics** in food chemistry.

### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1:** Insert > Chart > Scatter Plot (X-Y plot) with Temperature on X-axis and Shelf-Life on Y-axis.

**Step 2:** Right-click data point > "Add Trendline" > Select "Linear" > Check "Display Equation on Chart" and "Display R-squared value".

**Step 3:** Compute slope (m) and intercept (b) using Excel formulas:

$$ \texttt{=SLOPE(B2:B8, A2:A8)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad m \approx -1.78 $$

$$ \texttt{=INTERCEPT(B2:B8, A2:A8)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad b \approx 62.71 $$

**Step 4:** Linear regression equation:

$$ y = mx + b $$

$$ y = -1.78x + 62.71 $$

**Step 5:** Predict shelf-life at 40°C:

$$ y = -1.78(40) + 62.71 $$

$$ y = -71.20 + 62.71 $$

$$ y \approx -8.49 \, \text{days} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Interpretation:** A negative shelf-life is physically impossible! This indicates that the linear model **breaks down beyond 35°C** and that **exponential decay (Arrhenius model)** would be more accurate at higher temperatures. In a real exam, this nuance earns extra marks!

**Step 6:** R-squared value:

$$ R^2 = (-0.973)^2 \approx 0.947 $$

This indicates **94.7%** of the variance in shelf-life is explained by temperature variation — an excellent model fit for the observed range.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in KTU 2024 Evaluations:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `=` sign** — A formula without `=` is treated as text. (Penalty: 1 mark per formula).
> 2. **Confusing STDEV vs. STDEVP** — STDEV uses $(n-1)$ (sample), STDEVP uses $n$ (population). Read the question carefully!
> 3. **Using `TRUE` in VLOOKUP** — This requires sorted data and returns approximate matches, causing silent errors in food databases. Always use `FALSE`.
> 4. **Not labeling chart axes** — Charts without X-axis and Y-axis labels lose 1 mark in KTU grading.
> 5. **Skipping units** in answers — Always mention "%", "°C", "days", "mg/100g" etc. Units missing = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 6. **Forgetting to lock absolute references** with `$` when copying formulas — leads to wrong answers in VLOOKUP/cost calculations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 1, Topic: Spreadsheets**

- **Cell Address Format:** Column Letter + Row Number (e.g., `B5`). Use `$` for absolute reference.
- **Formula Bar:** Always begins with `=`. Press `Enter` to evaluate.
- **Mean Formula:** $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum x_i}{n}$ → Excel: `=AVERAGE()`.
- **Median:** Robust to outliers → Excel: `=MEDIAN()`.
- **Standard Deviation (Sample):** $s = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum(x_i - \bar{x})^2}{n-1}}$ → Excel: `=STDEV()`.
- **Variance:** $s^2$ → Excel: `=VAR()`.
- **Correlation:** $r \in [-1, +1]$ → Excel: `=CORREL()`.
- **IF Function:** `=IF(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false)`.
- **AND/OR/NOT:** Combine multiple conditions.
- **VLOOKUP:** `=VLOOKUP(lookup, table, col, FALSE)` — always use FALSE for exact match.
- **COUNTIF/COUNTIFS:** Conditional counting; widely used in quality grading.
- **Pivot Tables:** Summarize large food datasets by category (batch, supplier, product).
- **Charts:** Bar (comparison), Line (trend over time), Pie (composition), Scatter (correlation).
- **Conditional Formatting:** Visually highlight values above/below thresholds (e.g., red for FAIL).
- **Data Validation:** Dropdown lists to prevent invalid food category entries.
- **Sorting & Filtering:** Organize data by batch number, date, or quality grade.
- **What-If Analysis:** Goal Seek, Scenario Manager, Data Tables for predictive modeling.
- **Food Industry Applications:** SPC charts, FSSAI compliance, sensory panel analysis, inventory management, shelf-life studies, recipe cost analysis.
- **Common Mistakes:** Missing `=`, wrong range, no axis labels, ignoring units, using TRUE in VLOOKUP.
- **Industry Standards Supported:** HACCP, ISO 22000, FSSAI, Codex Alimentarius data reporting.

> **Final KTU Tip:** When asked "use a spreadsheet to solve," always present your answer in three layers: **(1) Data Layout → (2) Formula Used → (3) Computed Result**. This structure scores maximum marks in KTU 2024 ESE evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Charts, Using Macros, Add-ins, and Solver for Engineering Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT751 - ICT APPLICATIONS IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Creating Charts, Using Macros, Add-ins, and Solver for Engineering Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Excel-Based Engineering Tools

In the modern food processing industry, **Information and Communication Technology (ICT)** drives decision-making across quality control, supply chain optimization, process scheduling, and recipe standardization. Microsoft Excel, when extended with **Charts, Macros, Add-ins, and Solver**, becomes a powerful engineering workstation that can model, visualize, and optimize complex food industry processes without requiring specialized statistical software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Highlight:** This topic forms the computational backbone of the course. Students are expected to demonstrate working knowledge of (a) data visualization through charts, (b) automation through macros, (c) functional extension through add-ins, and (d) optimization through Solver for linear and non-linear food engineering problems.

---

## 1.2 Creating Charts — Formal Definition

A **chart** in Excel is a graphical representation of worksheet data that translates numerical values into visual patterns using coordinate axes, geometric markers, and color-coded legends. Charts convert raw tabular data — such as moisture content vs. drying time, temperature vs. enzyme activity, or shelf-life vs. storage humidity — into immediately interpretable trends.

### Key Chart Components

- **Category Axis (X-axis):** Independent variable (e.g., time, temperature, concentration).
- **Value Axis (Y-axis):** Dependent variable (e.g., mass loss, microbial count, moisture content).
- **Data Series:** A set of related data points forming one curve or bar group.
- **Legend:** Identifier showing which series corresponds to which dataset.
- **Plot Area:** The interior region where data is rendered.
- **Chart Title & Axis Titles:** Descriptive labels for clarity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Food Industry Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a bakery owner recording how bread volume changes with fermentation time across five different yeast concentrations. A table shows numbers like 250 mL, 320 mL, 410 mL... but you cannot *see* the trend instantly. A chart is like a picture-frame on the wall — it instantly shows that volume rises with fermentation time, peaks, then declines. The chart *translates numbers into insight*.

### Common Chart Types in Food Engineering Applications

| Chart Type | Best Used For | Typical Food Engineering Use Case |
|------------|--------------|-----------------------------------|
| Column / Bar Chart | Comparing discrete categories | Comparing vitamin retention across cooking methods |
| Line Chart | Trends over continuous variable | Moisture loss during drying over time |
| Pie Chart | Parts of a whole | Composition of milk (fat, protein, lactose, water) |
| Scatter (XY) Chart | Correlation between two variables | pH vs. titratable acidity |
| Area Chart | Cumulative volume | Daily production output across a month |
| Radar Chart | Multi-variable comparison | Sensory evaluation across 5 attributes |
| Histogram | Frequency distribution | Particle size distribution of flour |

---

## 1.3 Using Macros — Formal Definition

A **macro** is a sequence of recorded or programmatically written commands that Excel executes automatically to perform repetitive tasks. Macros are powered by **Visual Basic for Applications (VBA)**, Excel's built-in programming language. Macros eliminate manual repetition and enforce consistency in data processing.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a macro like a *recipe card* in a commercial kitchen. Instead of telling each new chef verbally how to prepare the marinade step-by-step, you write the recipe once on a laminated card. Any chef who follows that card produces the identical result every time. The macro is the "recipe card" — written once, executed repeatedly, identical result every time.

### Recording vs. Writing Macros

- **Macro Recorder:** Captures mouse clicks and keystrokes. Best for simple, repetitive formatting tasks (e.g., formatting a daily QC report with the same headers, borders, and color codes).
- **VBA Editor (Alt + F11):** Allows full programmatic control. Best for conditional logic, loops, user-defined functions, and integration with other Office applications.

### Macro Security Levels

| Security Level | Behavior | Suitable For |
|----------------|----------|--------------|
| Disable all macros | No macros run | High-security environments |
| Disable with notification | Prompts user before running | Default in food industry PCs |
| Disable except digitally signed | Only trusted macros run | Production environments |
| Enable all macros | All macros run freely | Development/testing only |

---

## 1.4 Add-ins — Formal Definition

An **add-in** is a supplemental program that extends Excel's built-in functionality by adding new commands, functions, or features. Add-ins are typically distributed as `.xlam` (Excel Add-in) files and can be loaded/unloaded via *File → Options → Add-ins*.

### Types of Add-ins Relevant to Food Industry

- **Excel Built-in Add-ins:** Analysis ToolPak (regression, ANOVA, t-tests, Fourier analysis), Solver Add-in.
- **Third-Party Add-ins:** Statistical process control (SPC) toolkits, fuzzy logic toolboxes, food formulation solvers.
- **Custom Add-ins:** Developed in-house by food companies for proprietary calculations like °Brix correction, water activity prediction, or shelf-life modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** If Excel is a basic kitchen knife, then an add-in is a specialized attachment — a bread-slicing guide, a julienne blade, or a meat tenderizer. The core tool (knife/Excel) remains the same, but the add-in *specializes* it for a specific job (slicing bread/complex statistics).

---

## 1.5 Solver — Formal Definition

**Solver** is an Excel add-in (must be loaded manually) that finds an **optimal value** for a target cell (objective) by changing a set of input cells (decision variables), subject to a defined set of constraints. Solver uses advanced numerical algorithms (Simplex LP, GRG Nonlinear, Evolutionary) to solve optimization problems.

### Solver Terminology

| Term | Meaning | Food Industry Example |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| **Objective Cell** | The cell to be maximized, minimized, or set to a target value | Minimize cost of feed formulation |
| **Decision Variable Cells** | Cells Solver is allowed to change | Quantity (kg) of each ingredient |
| **Constraints** | Limits on variables or formulas | Protein content ≥ 18%, Cost ≤ ₹50/kg |
| **Solving Method** | Algorithm used | Simplex LP for linear problems |

### Solver Methods

- **Simplex LP:** Used when objective and constraints are *linear*. Best for blend optimization, linear recipe formulation.
- **GRG (Generalized Reduced Gradient) Nonlinear:** Used when objective or constraints are *non-linear*. Best for enzyme kinetics, exponential shelf-life decay.
- **Evolutionary:** Used for non-smooth, non-differentiable problems. Best for multi-modal sensory optimization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Solver is **NOT** built into Excel by default. Students must explicitly load it via *File → Options → Add-ins → Manage Excel Add-ins → Solver Add-in*. This is a frequently tested practical step.

---

## 1.6 Summary of the Four Tools

> [!NOTE]
> **Integrated View:** These four tools form a *pipeline* in food industry ICT work:
> 1. **Charts** → *Visualize* data (what happened?)
> 2. **Macros** → *Automate* repetitive tasks (do it faster)
> 3. **Add-ins** → *Extend* functionality (do more)
> 4. **Solver** → *Optimize* decisions (what should we do?)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Chart Construction — Stepwise Theory

The chart engine in Excel internally treats data as a Cartesian coordinate system. For each data point $(x_i, y_i)$, the engine maps the $x_i$ to the category axis and $y_i$ to the value axis using linear scaling.

### Mathematical Foundation of Axis Scaling

The position of a value on the axis is computed using the formula:

$$v_{\text{display}} = v_{\min} + \frac{(v_{\text{actual}} - v_{\min,\text{data}})}{(v_{\max,\text{data}} - v_{\min,\text{data}})} \cdot (v_{\max} - v_{\min})$$

where:
- $v_{\text{display}}$ is the pixel/position-based plotted value
- $v_{\text{actual}}$ is the source data value
- $v_{\min,\text{data}}$, $v_{\max,\text{data}}$ are the minimum and maximum of the dataset
- $v_{\min}$, $v_{\max}$ are the axis limits

This linear interpolation ensures proportional visual representation.

### Trendlines and Regression Overlay

Excel allows fitting trendlines using least-squares regression. For a linear trendline $y = mx + c$:

$$m = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$$

$$c = \bar{y} - m \bar{x}$$

These coefficients are *theoretically equivalent* to those derived by hand; Excel computes them silently. The coefficient of determination $R^2$ is reported automatically:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In drying kinetics of food, plotting $\ln(MR)$ vs. time yields a linear trendline whose slope equals $-k$ (drying constant). Excel's trendline feature can extract this $k$ value directly without manual calculation — a huge time-saver in research.

---

## 2.2 Macro Architecture — Theoretical Breakdown

A VBA macro has a hierarchical structure:

1. **Module:** A container for VBA code within the workbook.
2. **Procedure (Sub):** An executable block of code.
3. **Function:** A procedure that returns a value.
4. **Event Handler:** A procedure triggered by user actions (e.g., `Worksheet_Change`).

### Anatomy of a Macro

```vba
Sub FormatDailyQCReport()
    ' Comment: Applies standard formatting to QC report
    Range("A1").Value = "Date"
    Range("A1").Font.Bold = True
    Range("A1:A31").NumberFormat = "dd-mmm-yyyy"
    Columns("A:F").AutoFit
End Sub
```

### Key VBA Concepts for Food Industry

| VBA Object | Property / Method | Use Case |
|------------|------------------|----------|
| `Workbook` | `.Open`, `.SaveAs`, `.Close` | Automated daily report handling |
| `Worksheet` | `.Cells`, `.Range` | Data manipulation |
| `Range` | `.Value`, `.Formula`, `.Interior.Color` | Cell-level formatting |
| `Application` | `.ScreenUpdating = False` | Speed optimization |
| `MsgBox` | User dialog | Alerts for out-of-spec values |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`.ScreenUpdating = False`** is a critical performance technique. By disabling screen refresh during macro execution, Excel runs **5–10× faster**, which is essential when processing large sensory or nutritional datasets.

---

## 2.3 Add-in Theory

Add-ins operate at the application level. Once loaded, they integrate seamlessly with Excel's ribbon and function library. The technical mechanism is:

1. Excel reads the add-in's `.xlam` file at startup (or on-demand load).
2. The add-in registers its custom functions (UDFs) and RibbonX commands.
3. These UDFs become globally callable like built-in functions (`=MyCustomFunction(...)`).

### Analysis ToolPak — Key Functions for Food Science

| Tool | Statistical Purpose | Food Industry Application |
|------|--------------------|-----------------------------|
| Descriptive Statistics | Mean, median, std dev, skewness | Summarizing batch quality data |
| Histogram | Frequency binning | Particle size analysis |
| Regression | Linear / multiple regression | Modeling drying kinetics |
| ANOVA | One-way, two-way variance analysis | Comparing 3 storage conditions |
| t-Test (paired, two-sample) | Hypothesis testing | Comparing control vs. treated |
| Fourier Analysis | Frequency decomposition | Detecting cyclic patterns in shelf-life data |
| Random Number Generation | Monte Carlo simulation | Simulating batch variability |
| Sampling | Random sample extraction | Statistical quality sampling |

---

## 2.4 Solver — Mathematical Foundation

Solver solves **constrained optimization problems** of the form:

$$\text{Find } \mathbf{x} = (x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n) \text{ that optimizes } f(\mathbf{x})$$

subject to:

$$g_j(\mathbf{x}) \leq b_j, \quad j = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$
$$x_i \geq 0 \quad \text{(or other bounds)}$$

### Linear Programming (LP) Form

When $f$ and all $g_j$ are linear:

$$\text{Minimize } f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i x_i$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_i \leq b_j, \quad \forall j$$

with $x_i \geq 0$ for all $i$.

**Simplex Method** (Dantzig, 1947) solves LP by traversing vertices of the feasible polytope to find the optimal vertex.

### Nonlinear Programming (NLP) Form

When $f$ or any $g_j$ is non-linear, GRG Nonlinear is invoked. It uses gradient-based iteration:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = \mathbf{x}_k - \alpha_k \nabla f(\mathbf{x}_k)$$

where $\alpha_k$ is the step size chosen by line search.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this cheat sheet — these are the most frequently tested formulas in KTU Module 1.**

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Linear Regression Slope | $m = \dfrac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}$ | dimensionless ratio |
| Linear Regression Intercept | $c = \bar{y} - m\bar{x}$ | units of $y$ |
| Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \dfrac{\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2}$ | $0 \leq R^2 \leq 1$ |
| Axis Scaling | $v_{\text{display}} = v_{\min} + \dfrac{(v_{\text{actual}} - v_{\min,\text{data}})}{(v_{\max,\text{data}} - v_{\min,\text{data}})} \cdot (v_{\max} - v_{\min})$ | linear interpolation |
| LP Objective (Min) | $f(\mathbf{x}) = \sum c_i x_i$ | minimize cost / maximize profit |
| LP Constraint | $\sum a_{ij} x_i \leq b_j$ | resource / quality constraint |
| Non-negativity | $x_i \geq 0$ | always in physical contexts |
| Simplex Iteration Vertex Count | bounded by $\binom{n+m}{m}$ | worst-case complexity |
| Macro Speed Gain | $\approx 5\times$ to $10\times$ with `ScreenUpdating = False` | empirical |
| Default Solver Tolerance | $0.000001$ (1 part per million) | configurable |
| Solver Max Subproblems | $\leq 200$ (Evolutionary default) | for non-smooth problems |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real food processing company:

- **Charts** are used in HACCP dashboards to plot critical control point (CCP) temperatures over time.
- **Macros** automate the daily generation of nutritional labels from raw recipe databases.
- **Add-ins** like the Analysis ToolPak perform statistical quality control on incoming raw material batches.
- **Solver** optimizes least-cost feed formulations for livestock feed mills, or maximizes profit in product mix decisions across multiple SKUs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these tools matter:** Without them, a food engineer would manually calculate each statistical metric, re-plot every chart, and run trial-and-error optimizations. With these tools, an entire weekly QA report can be generated in minutes and the optimum formulation found in seconds — directly impacting profitability and food safety compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Linear Regression in Excel's Trendline

Excel's linear trendline internally performs Ordinary Least Squares (OLS). Let us derive the slope and intercept formulas step by step, exactly as Excel computes them.

### Step 1 — Define the Objective

We want to find $m$ and $c$ such that the line $y = mx + c$ minimizes the **Sum of Squared Errors (SSE)**:

$$SSE(m, c) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - mx_i - c)^2$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate with Respect to $m$

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial m} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i (y_i - mx_i - c) = 0$$

### Step 3 — Differentiate with Respect to $c$

$$\frac{\partial SSE}{\partial c} = -2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (y_i - mx_i - c) = 0$$

### Step 4 — Solve the System

From Step 3:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i = m \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i + n c$$

$$c = \bar{y} - m \bar{x}$$

Substituting into Step 2:

$$\sum x_i y_i = m \sum x_i^2 + c \sum x_i = m \sum x_i^2 + (\bar{y} - m\bar{x}) \sum x_i$$

$$\sum x_i y_i = m \sum x_i^2 + n\bar{y}\bar{x} - m\bar{x} \cdot n\bar{x}$$

$$m \left(\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2\right) = \sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}$$

$$\boxed{m = \frac{\sum x_i y_i - n\bar{x}\bar{y}}{\sum x_i^2 - n\bar{x}^2} = \frac{n\sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n\sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2}}$$

### Step 5 — Compute $R^2$

$$SS_{tot} = \sum (y_i - \bar{y})^2$$

$$SS_{res} = \sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$$

$$\boxed{R^2 = 1 - \frac{SS_{res}}{SS_{tot}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** These derived formulas are EXACTLY what Excel displays when you add a linear trendline. The displayed equation `y = mx + b` and the $R^2$ value come from these formulas. Students should understand that "trendline" is not magic — it is OLS regression done silently.

---

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Linear Programming via Simplex — Conceptual Walkthrough

Consider a feed-mill problem: a feed mix must contain at least **18% protein** and at most **8% fiber**, using two ingredients, A (cost ₹20/kg, 20% protein, 5% fiber) and B (cost ₹35/kg, 10% protein, 15% fiber). Minimize cost per kg.

### Step 1 — Formulate the LP

$$\text{Minimize } Z = 20 x_A + 35 x_B$$

subject to:

$$0.20 x_A + 0.10 x_B \geq 0.18 (x_A + x_B)$$
$$0.05 x_A + 0.15 x_B \leq 0.08 (x_A + x_B)$$
$$x_A + x_B = 1$$
$$x_A, x_B \geq 0$$

### Step 2 — Convert to Standard Form

Protein constraint (rearrange):

$$0.20 x_A + 0.10 x_B - 0.18 x_A - 0.18 x_B \geq 0$$
$$0.02 x_A - 0.08 x_B \geq 0$$

Fiber constraint:

$$0.05 x_A + 0.15 x_B - 0.08 x_A - 0.08 x_B \leq 0$$
$$-0.03 x_A + 0.07 x_B \leq 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve by Substitution (since $x_B = 1 - x_A$)

Substitute $x_B = 1 - x_A$ into the protein constraint:

$$0.02 x_A - 0.08 (1 - x_A) \geq 0$$
$$0.02 x_A - 0.08 + 0.08 x_A \geq 0$$
$$0.10 x_A \geq 0.08$$
$$x_A \geq 0.80$$

Substitute into fiber constraint:

$$-0.03 x_A + 0.07(1 - x_A) \leq 0$$
$$-0.03 x_A + 0.07 - 0.07 x_A \leq 0$$
$$-0.10 x_A + 0.07 \leq 0$$
$$x_A \geq 0.70$$

### Step 4 — Determine Feasible Region

The binding constraint is $x_A \geq 0.80$. Combined with $x_A + x_B = 1$ and non-negativity:

$$0.80 \leq x_A \leq 1$$
$$0 \leq x_B \leq 0.20$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Objective at Endpoints

At $x_A = 0.80, x_B = 0.20$:

$$Z = 20(0.80) + 35(0.20) = 16 + 7 = ₹23.00/\text{kg}$$

At $x_A = 1.00, x_B = 0.00$:

$$Z = 20(1.00) + 35(0.00) = ₹20.00/\text{kg}$$

### Step 6 — Optimal Solution

$$\boxed{x_A = 1.00, \quad x_B = 0.00, \quad Z_{\min} = ₹20/\text{kg}}$$

Pure ingredient A is optimal (it has the required protein and acceptable fiber on its own). This is the answer Excel Solver would also return.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** Although the example above was solved manually for clarity, in practice the same problem with 10 ingredients and 20 constraints would be solved in milliseconds by Solver. The student's job is to *set up* the spreadsheet correctly and let Solver find the optimum.

---

## 3.3 Detailed Walkthrough: Solver Setup Procedure (Step-by-Step)

### Procedure for Setting Up Solver in Excel

1. **Lay out the data** in a worksheet. Place decision variables in named cells (e.g., `B2:B5` for 4 ingredients).
2. **Create the objective cell** (e.g., `D10`) using a SUMPRODUCT formula:
   `=SUMPRODUCT(B2:B5, C2:C5)` where `C2:C5` are unit costs.
3. **Create constraint cells** (e.g., `D12` for protein, `D13` for fat) using SUMPRODUCT against nutritional profiles.
4. **Open Solver:** *Data tab → Solver* (after loading the add-in).
5. **Set Objective:** Select `D10`, choose "Min".
6. **By Changing Variable Cells:** Select `B2:B5`.
7. **Add Constraints:** e.g., `D12 >= 0.18`, `D13 <= 0.30`, `B2:B5 >= 0`, `B2:B5 <= 1`.
8. **Choose Solving Method:** Simplex LP (for linear problems).
9. **Click Solve.** Solver returns the optimal values and generates an Answer Report.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Error to Avoid:** Forgetting to check "Make Unconstrained Variables Non-Negative" in the Solver Options dialog. This is a 1-mark trap in KTU practical exams.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix to compare the four tools before answering exam questions on "Which tool would you use for..."**

| Aspect | Charts | Macros | Add-ins | Solver |
|--------|--------|--------|---------|--------|
| Primary Purpose | Visualization | Automation | Extension | Optimization |
| Requires Add-in Load? | No | No | Varies | Yes |
| Linear Algebra Used? | Axis scaling | No | Varies | Yes (Simplex) |
| User Programming? | Not required | VBA required | Not required (loading only) | Not required (formulation only) |
| Typical Output | Graphical | Side effects | New functions | Numeric optimum |
| Food Industry Use | QC dashboards | Daily reports | Statistical tests | Least-cost formulation |
| Output Type | Visual | Side-effect | Functional | Numerical |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Regression via Chart Trendline

### Problem

The following data shows moisture content (%) of a food product during drying:

| Time (h) | Moisture (%) |
|----------|--------------|
| 0 | 80 |
| 1 | 65 |
| 2 | 53 |
| 3 | 44 |
| 4 | 37 |
| 5 | 32 |

Fit a linear trendline $y = mx + c$ and compute $R^2$.

### Step 1 — Compute Sums

$$n = 6, \quad \sum x_i = 15, \quad \sum y_i = 311$$
$$\sum x_i^2 = 55, \quad \sum y_i^2 = 18423, \quad \sum x_i y_i = 552$$

### Step 2 — Compute Slope

$$m = \frac{n \sum x_i y_i - \sum x_i \sum y_i}{n \sum x_i^2 - (\sum x_i)^2} = \frac{6(552) - (15)(311)}{6(55) - (15)^2}$$

$$m = \frac{3312 - 4665}{330 - 225} = \frac{-1353}{105} = -12.886$$

### Step 3 — Compute Intercept

$$\bar{x} = 2.5, \quad \bar{y} = 51.833$$

$$c = 51.833 - (-12.886)(2.5) = 51.833 + 32.214 = 84.048$$

### Step 4 — Final Equation

$$\boxed{y = -12.886 x + 84.048}$$

### Step 5 — Compute $R^2$

Compute predicted values and SSE:

$$\hat{y}_1 = 84.048, \quad \hat{y}_2 = 71.162, \quad \hat{y}_3 = 58.276, \quad \hat{y}_4 = 45.390, \quad \hat{y}_5 = 32.504, \quad \hat{y}_6 = 19.618$$

$$SS_{res} = (80 - 84.048)^2 + (65 - 71.162)^2 + (53 - 58.276)^2 + (44 - 45.390)^2 + (37 - 32.504)^2 + (32 - 19.618)^2$$

$$SS_{res} = 16.38 + 38.02 + 27.86 + 1.93 + 20.21 + 153.41 = 257.81$$

$$SS_{tot} = \sum (y_i - 51.833)^2 = (28.167)^2 + (13.167)^2 + (1.167)^2 + (-7.833)^2 + (-14.833)^2 + (-19.833)^2$$

$$SS_{tot} = 793.4 + 173.4 + 1.36 + 61.36 + 220.0 + 393.3 = 1642.82$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{257.81}{1642.82} = 1 - 0.157 = 0.843$$

$$\boxed{R^2 \approx 0.843}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The linear trendline explains ~84.3% of the variance. For higher accuracy, an exponential decay model is typically more appropriate for drying kinetics — illustrating that linear regression is a *first approximation* in food engineering.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Chart Creation Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the standard procedure for creating a chart in Excel for food industry data visualization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step1 Identify Data Range"] --> B["Step2 Select Cells B2 to D10"]
    B --> C["Step3 Click Insert Tab"]
    C --> D["Step4 Choose Chart Type e.g. Line"]
    D --> E["Step5 Chart Appears on Sheet"]
    E --> F["Step6 Add Chart Title"]
    F --> G["Step7 Label X and Y Axes"]
    G --> H["Step8 Add Legend"]
    H --> I["Step9 Add Trendline if needed"]
    I --> J["Step10 Move or Resize Chart"]
    J --> K["Step11 Finalize for Report"]
```

## 4.2 Macro Recording and Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["User Opens Developer Tab"] --> B["Click Record Macro"]
    B --> C["Provide Macro Name and Shortcut"]
    C --> D["Perform Actions on Sheet"]
    D --> E["Stop Recording"]
    E --> F["Macro Stored in VBA Module"]
    F --> G["Run via Shortcut or Button"]
    G --> H["Excel Replays Actions"]
    H --> I["Same Result Every Time"]
```

## 4.3 Add-in Loading Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step1 Click File Tab"] --> B["Step2 Click Options"]
    B --> C["Step3 Select Add-ins Category"]
    C --> D["Step4 Set Manage to Excel Add-ins"]

```

## 4.4 Solver Operational Block Diagram

This block diagram shows Solver's internal architecture and data flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP["Inputs Objective Cell Decision Variables Constraints"] --> FWD["Forward Pass"]
    FWD --> EVAL["Evaluate Objective Function"]
    EVAL --> CHK["Check Constraint Satisfaction"]
    CHK -->|Infeasible| ADJ["Adjust Decision Variables"]
    ADJ --> FWD
    CHK -->|Feasible| OPT{"Is Objective Optimal"}
    OPT -->|No| ALG["Apply Simplex or GRG Step"]
    ALG --> FWD
    OPT -->|Yes| OUT["Outputs Optimal Variable Values Answer Report Sensitivity Report"]
```

## 4.5 Integrated Food Industry ICT Workflow

This diagram shows how the four tools integrate in a complete food industry ICT pipeline.

flowchart LR

flowchart TD
