$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{(1030\ \text{kg/m}^3)(1.2\ \text{m/s})(0.05\ \text{m})}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}} \\
&= \frac{1030 \times 1.2 \times 0.05}{0.002} \\
&= \frac{61.8}{0.002} \\
&= 30{,}900
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the critical Reynolds number:**

For pipe flow, transition occurs at $Re_{crit} \approx 2300$.

$$Re = 30{,}900 \gg 2300$$

**Conclusion:** The flow is **fully turbulent**, which is desired in pasteurization to ensure good heat transfer and uniform mixing, but it requires a turbulence model (e.g., $k$-$\varepsilon$) in CFD.

### 3.2 Derivation: Power-Law Viscosity for Tomato Ketchup

Ketchup follows the **Ostwald-de Waele power-law** model:

$$\mu(\dot{\gamma}) = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

Two viscosity measurements at different shear rates are:
- $\dot{\gamma}_1 = 10\ \text{s}^{-1}$, $\mu_1 = 12\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $\dot{\gamma}_2 = 100\ \text{s}^{-1}$, $\mu_2 = 4\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

Determine $K$ and $n$.

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm of both sides of the power-law:**

$$\ln \mu = \ln K + (n-1) \ln \dot{\gamma}$$

This is linear in form: $y = a + bx$ where $b = n-1$.

**Step 2 — Write the two log-transformed equations:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(12) &= \ln K + (n-1) \ln(10) \\
\ln(4) &= \ln K + (n-1) \ln(100)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Subtract equation 1 from equation 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(4) - \ln(12) &= (n-1)\left[\ln(100) - \ln(10)\right] \\
\ln\!\left(\tfrac{4}{12}\right) &= (n-1)\ln\!\left(\tfrac{100}{10}\right) \\
\ln(0.3333) &= (n-1)\ln(10) \\
-1.0986 &= (n-1)(2.3026) \\
n - 1 &= -0.4771 \\
n &= 0.5229
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $K$ using equation 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(12) &= \ln K + (-0.4771)(\ln 10) \\
2.4849 &= \ln K - 1.0986 \\
\ln K &= 3.5835 \\
K &= e^{3.5835} \approx 36.0\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since $n < 1$, the ketchup is **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)**. As you shake the bottle harder, viscosity drops — this is precisely the property exploited in squeeze bottles.

### 3.3 Derivation: F-value for Sterilization Lethality

The **F-value** quantifies the equivalent sterilization time at a reference temperature:

$$F = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} 10^{(T(t) - T_{ref})/z}\, dt$$

For a simplified retort process where $T(t) = T_{ref} + A(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$, with $A = 20\ \text{K}$, $\tau = 30\ \text{min}$, $z = 10\ \text{K}$, $T_{ref} = 121.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$, total time = 60 min:

**Step 1 — Substitute into the F-value integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= \int_{0}^{60} 10^{\,\bigl[(T_{ref}+A(1-e^{-t/\tau}) - T_{ref}\bigr]/z}\, dt \\
&= \int_{0}^{60} 10^{\,A(1-e^{-t/\tau})/z}\, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — With $A/z = 20/10 = 2$, so:**

$$F = \int_{0}^{60} 10^{2(1-e^{-t/30})}\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Numerical evaluation via substitution $u = 1 - e^{-t/30}$:**

At $t = 0$: $u = 0$; at $t = 60$: $u = 1 - e^{-2} = 0.8647$.

Using Simpson's rule or direct numerical integration (table of values):

| $t$ (min) | $u$ | $10^{2u}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0000 | 1.00 |
| 10 | 0.2835 | 3.80 |
| 20 | 0.4866 | 30.62 |
| 30 | 0.6321 | 246.6 |
| 40 | 0.7364 | 1090 |
| 50 | 0.8082 | 3229 |
| 60 | 0.8561 | 7185 |

Numerical integration (trapezoidal) gives $F \approx 1.18 \times 10^{5}\ \text{min}$ — an extremely high lethality suitable for low-acid canned food sterilization.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Which Solver/Model for Which Food Process?

| Food Process | Dominant Physics | Recommended CFD Model | Software Typically Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Milk pasteurization (pipe) | Turbulent flow + heat transfer | $k$-$\varepsilon$ RANS, energy equation | ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL |
| Spray drying of milk powder | Multiphase (droplet + gas), evaporation | Eulerian-Lagrangian, species transport | ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM |
| Baking of bread/biscuit | Heat + mass transfer + porous media | Conjugate heat transfer, Darcy flow | COMSOL, ANSYS |
| Sterilization in retort pouch | Conduction + natural convection | Laminar or low-Re $k$-$\varepsilon$ | ANSYS Fluent |
| Mixing tank (yogurt, sauce) | Turbulent, non-Newtonian, rotating frame | MRF or sliding mesh, power-law | ANSYS Fluent, STAR-CCM+ |
| Cold storage / refrigeration | Natural convection, radiation | Boussinesq + surface-to-surface radiation | ANSYS Fluent |
| CIP (Clean-in-Place) cleaning | Turbulent, chemical species | Species transport, $k$-$\varepsilon$ | OpenFOAM, ANSYS Fluent |
| Extrusion cooking | Viscous heating, non-Newtonian | Power-law with viscous dissipation | POLYFLOW (Ansys), OpenFOAM |
| Aseptic packaging | Two-phase flow, sterile barrier | VOF (Volume of Fluid) | ANSYS Fluent, STAR-CCM+ |
| Brewing fermentation | Multiphase, reaction kinetics | Eulerian multiphase + custom reaction | OpenFOAM, COMSOL |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CFD Workflow in the Food Industry — End-to-End Pipeline

The diagram below traces the full digital workflow from the moment a food engineer conceives a new processing line to the moment the optimized design is shipped to production.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Food Process Requirement Definition] --> stepB[CAD Geometry Creation of Equipment]
    stepB --> stepC[Domain Discretization Meshing]
    stepC --> stepD{Material Type Selection}
    stepD --> stepE1[Newtonian Fluid rho mu cp k]
    stepD --> stepE2[Non-Newtonian Power Law K n]
    stepD --> stepE3[Bingham Plastic tau0 mu_p]
    stepE1 --> stepF[Boundary Conditions Setup]
    stepE2 --> stepF
    stepE3 --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG[Solver Configuration]
    stepG --> stepH[Turbulence Model Selection k epsilon or k omega]
    stepH --> stepI[Iterative Numerical Solution]
    stepI --> stepJ[Convergence Check Residuals less than 1e-6]
    stepJ --> stepK{Converged}
    stepK --> stepL[Post Processing Velocity Temperature Pressure]
    stepK --> stepM[Refine Mesh and Re Solve]
    stepM --> stepI
    stepL --> stepN[Validation Against Experiments LDV PIV Thermocouples]
    stepN --> stepO{Valid}
    stepO --> stepP[Design Optimization and Scale Up]
    stepO --> stepQ[Adjust Model and Re Simulate]
    stepQ --> stepF
    stepP --> stepR[Industrial Implementation]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — CFD Software Stack for Food Applications

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    layer1[Layer 1 Hardware Multicore CPU GPU Cluster] --> layer2[Layer 2 Operating System Linux Windows HPC]
    layer2 --> layer3[Layer 3 Pre Processor Geometry CAD Meshing ICEM GAMG SnappyHexMesh]
    layer3 --> layer4[Layer 4 Solver Core Finite Volume or Finite Element]
    layer4 --> layer5[Layer 5 Physical Models Turbulence Reaction Multiphase Radiation Porous]
    layer5 --> layer6[Layer 6 Post Processor Visualization CFD Post ParaView Tecplot]
    layer6 --> layer7[Layer 7 Food Industry Application Pasteurization Spray Drying Baking Mixing Sterilization CIP]
```

### 4.3 Typical Geometry & Mesh for a Mixing Tank (Schematic Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vesselA[Tank Wall Stationary Zone] --> interfaceA[Interface Surface]
    interfaceA --> rotorA[Impeller Rotating Zone MRF]
    rotorA --> baffleA[Wall Baffles]
    baffleA --> meshA[Mesh Tetrahedral or Polyhedral Cells]
    meshA --> bcA[Boundary Conditions Inlet Outlet Wall Impeller Shaft]
```

### 4.4 Cold-Spot Identification Workflow in Retort Sterilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pouchA[Pouch Geometry STL Import] --> meshB[Tetrahedral Mesh]
    meshB --> solveB[Solve Energy Equation Conduction plus Natural Convection]
    solveB --> fieldB[Temperature Field T x y z t]
    fieldB --> lethB[Compute Local F value F x y z]
    lethB --> coldB[Identify Cold Spot Minimum F]
    coldB --> verifyB[Compare with Target F greater than 6 min]
    verifyB --> acceptB{Pass}
    acceptB --> yesB[Approved Industrial Process]
    acceptB --> noB[Redesign Process Time or Temperature]
    noB --> pouchA
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define CFD. List any four food-industry applications where CFD is used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) is the numerical solution of the governing equations of fluid flow (Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy) using computational algorithms to simulate and visualize fluid behavior.

**Four applications (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Optimization of pasteurization pipelines** — predicting temperature uniformity and residence time.
2. **Spray dryer design** — droplet-air interaction, evaporation rate, particle trajectory.
3. **Sterilization/retort validation** — locating the cold spot in pouches and cans.
4. **Mixing tank design** — predicting flow patterns, dead zones, and power consumption for non-Newtonian food (yogurt, ketchup).

*(Also valid: baking ovens, CIP cleaning, brewing, extrusion, cold storage.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Why are most food materials modeled as non-Newtonian fluids in CFD simulations? Give one example of a non-Newtonian model and its governing equation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Reason (1.5 marks):** Most food materials (sauces, dairy, dough, chocolate) contain suspended particles, fats, polymers, or emulsions whose viscosity depends on the applied shear rate — they are **not** constant-viscosity fluids. Modeling them as Newtonian produces inaccurate velocity and temperature predictions, especially in mixing and pumping operations.

**Model and equation (1.5 marks):**

The **Ostwald-de Waele power-law model** is widely used:

$$\mu = K \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$$

Where $K$ is the consistency index, $n$ is the flow behavior index, and $\dot{\gamma}$ is the shear rate. For shear-thinning food like ketchup, $n < 1$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 marks):** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]*

#### (a) Derive the steady-state continuity equation for an incompressible fluid in 3D Cartesian coordinates. Explain its physical significance with an example from food processing. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Explain the standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ turbulence model used in food-industry CFD simulations. List the two governing transport equations with all terms explained. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question A:**

#### (a) Derivation of Continuity Equation:

**Statement of the conservation principle (1 mark):**
For any control volume, the rate of mass entering minus the rate of mass leaving equals the rate of accumulation of mass inside.

**Derivation for an infinitesimal control volume $dV = dx\,dy\,dz$ (4 marks):**

Let $\rho$ be density and $\vec{V} = (u,v,w)$ the velocity.

Mass flux in along x-direction at face $x$: $\rho u \big\vert_{x}$
Mass flux out along x-direction at face $x+dx$: $\rho u \big\vert_{x+dx} = \rho u \big\vert_{x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx$

Net outflow along x: $\dfrac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz$

Similarly for y and z. Total net outflow = sum of three components.

Conservation requires:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dx\,dy\,dz + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y}\,dx\,dy\,dz + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\,dx\,dy\,dz = 0$$

Dividing by $dx\,dy\,dz$:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Incompressible form (1 mark):** For $\rho = \text{constant}$ in space and time:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$$

**Physical significance with food example (1 mark):**
In a **milk pasteurizer pipe**, this equation guarantees mass conservation — the volumetric flow rate entering per second must equal the volumetric flow rate leaving. If the simulation predicts mass creation or loss, the numerical solution has diverged and must be re-initialized.

---

#### (b) The $k$-$\varepsilon$ Turbulence Model:

**Why turbulence modeling is needed (2 marks):**
Most food-industry flows (mixing tanks, spray dryers, plate heat exchangers) are turbulent with $Re > 10^4$. Directly resolving all eddies (DNS) requires $N \sim Re^{9/4}$ grid points — for $Re = 10^5$ this is $\sim 10^{11}$ cells, infeasible. Hence we use **RANS** with a turbulence closure model.

The **$k$-$\varepsilon$ model** introduces two extra variables:
- $k$ = turbulent kinetic energy (m²/s²)
- $\varepsilon$ = rate of dissipation of $k$ (m²/s³)

**Transport equation for $k$ (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial (\rho k)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho k u_j)}{\partial x_j} = P_k - \rho \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right]$$

Term-by-term:
- LHS = advection of $k$ by mean flow
- $P_k$ = production of turbulence by mean shear
- $\rho \varepsilon$ = dissipation of turbulence
- Last term = diffusion (molecular + turbulent)

**Transport equation for $\varepsilon$ (2 marks):**

$$\frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon u_j)}{\partial x_j} = C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\frac{\rho \varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right]$$

With $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \dfrac{k^2}{\varepsilon}$.

**Significance (1 mark):** In a **stirred yogurt tank**, the $k$-$\varepsilon$ model predicts turbulent eddy dissipation, which directly determines mixing time, blend uniformity, and power number of the impeller — all critical scale-up parameters.

---

### **Question B (14 marks):** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]*

#### (a) What is the Reynolds number? Calculate it for tomato ketchup flowing in a pipe of diameter $0.04\ \text{m}$ at mean velocity $1.0\ \text{m/s}$. Take $\rho = 1100\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.5\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$. State whether the flow is laminar or turbulent. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) With a neat flowchart, describe the step-by-step CFD workflow applied to optimize a bakery oven for uniform biscuit browning. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Answer for Question B:**

#### (a) Reynolds Number Calculation:

**Definition (2 marks):**
Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

- $Re < 2300$: laminar flow
- $Re > 4000$: turbulent flow
- $2300 \le Re \le 4000$: transitional

**Substitution (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Re &= \frac{(1100)(1.0)(0.04)}{0.5} \\
&= \frac{44}{0.5} \\
&= 88
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (2 marks):**
Since $Re = 88 \ll 2300$, the flow is **highly laminar**. In a ketchup filling line, this is desirable because turbulent flow would create splashing and inconsistent volumetric filling of bottles. For CFD, this means we do **not** need a turbulence model — the laminar Navier-Stokes equations suffice, drastically reducing computational cost.

---

#### (b) CFD Workflow for Bakery Oven Optimization:

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The goal is to predict the temperature field on the biscuit surface as a function of oven geometry, fan placement, and heating element position, so that browning is uniform across all trays.

**Step-by-step workflow (6 marks):**

1. **Geometry (1 mark):** Import the oven cavity as a 3D CAD model (STL/STEP). Include heating elements, fans, trays, biscuits as separate solid bodies.

2. **Meshing (1 mark):** Generate a tetrahedral or polyhedral mesh, with refinement near heating elements and biscuit surfaces where temperature gradients are steep.

3. **Physics setup (1.5 marks):**
   - **Fluid domain:** Air (ideal gas, Boussinesq for buoyancy).
   - **Solid domain:** Biscuit (porous media, effective thermal conductivity).
   - **Radiation model:** Surface-to-surface (S2S) for heating element walls.
   - **Boundary conditions:** Inlet from fan (velocity), outlet (pressure), heating elements (constant heat flux), biscuit surface (coupled convective + radiative).

4. **Turbulence model (0.5 marks):** $k$-$\varepsilon$ Realizable for the forced convection inside the oven.

5. **Solve (0.5 marks):** Run the coupled flow-thermal-radiation simulation until residuals < $10^{-6}$ and energy balance closes to within 1%.

6. **Post-processing (1 mark):** Plot temperature contours on the biscuit surface. Identify hot spots (>200 °C, burnt) and cold spots (<180 °C, under-baked). Adjust fan speed, baffle angle, or heating element wattage and re-run.

7. **Validation (0.5 marks):** Bake real biscuits with thermocouples embedded, compare with simulated temperature history. If error > 5%, refine mesh or adjust boundary conditions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Writing Navier-Stokes without explaining the term:** Examiners award marks for *naming* each term (inertia, pressure, viscous, body force). Writing the bare equation = 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Forgetting "incompressible" or "Newtonian" assumptions:** Always state the assumptions *before* writing the continuity or Navier-Stokes equation. Students who skip this lose 1–2 marks routinely.
> 3. **Confusing $k$ (turbulent kinetic energy) with thermal conductivity $k$:** Use different symbols in the answer (e.g., $k_t$ for thermal, $k$ for TKE). Examiners interpret this as conceptual confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention empirical constants in $k$-$\varepsilon$:** $C_\mu$, $C_{1\varepsilon}$, $C_{2\varepsilon}$, $\sigma_k$, $\sigma_\varepsilon$ — examiners expect at least the names.
> 5. **Omitting the "why" in the CFD workflow:** Just listing steps is 4 marks; explaining *why* each step matters earns full marks.
> 6. **Not converting units consistently:** Mixing Pa·s with cP or m with mm in a calculation = automatic 1-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — CFD in Food Industry**

- ✅ CFD = **numerical** solution of **Navier-Stokes + continuity + energy** equations using computers.
- ✅ **Three governing laws:** conservation of mass, momentum, and energy.
- ✅ **Incompressible** assumption valid for liquids (milk, juice, oil) below Mach 0.3.
- ✅ **Continuity equation (incompressible):** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- ✅ **Navier-Stokes:** vector form on one line, expanded in $x,y,z$ components for solving.
- ✅ **Most food fluids are non-Newtonian** — power-law ($n<1$ shear-thinning) is the most common model.
- ✅ **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho v L / \mu$; threshold $\approx 2300$ for pipe flow.
- ✅ **Turbulence:** modeled (not resolved) using **$k$-$\varepsilon$** (workhorse) or **$k$-$\omega$ SST** (near-wall accuracy).
- ✅ **Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ model uses 5 empirical constants** ($C_\mu$, $C_{1\varepsilon}$, $C_{2\varepsilon}$, $\sigma_k$, $\sigma_\varepsilon$).
- ✅ **CFD workflow:** Geometry → Mesh → Material → BCs → Solver → Iterate → Post-process → Validate → Optimize.
- ✅ **Key food-industry applications:** pasteurization, spray drying, baking, sterilization, mixing, CIP, brewing, extrusion, aseptic packaging, cold storage.
- ✅ **Sterilization F-value** uses $z = 10\ \text{K}$ for *Clostridium botulinum* typically, with $T_{ref} = 121.1\ ^\circ\text{C}$.
- ✅ **Validation is non-negotiable** — a CFD result without experimental comparison is just a colored picture.
- ✅ **Advantages:** cost reduction, faster development, full-field data, safety for extreme conditions.
- ✅ **Limitations:** garbage-in-garbage-out (GIGO), requires rheological data, expensive HPC, validation difficulty.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Overview of CFD Software Applications in Food and Beverage Industry**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)** is a branch of fluid mechanics that uses numerical methods and algorithms (primarily the **Navier–Stokes equations**) to solve and analyze problems involving fluid flows, heat transfer, mass transfer, and related physical phenomena on a computer.

In the food industry, CFD helps engineers **simulate** the behavior of fluids (milk, juices, oils, steam, air) and heat/mass transfer inside processing equipment — *before* building a physical prototype.

---

## 1.2 What are GAMBIT and FLUENT?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GAMBIT** is a **pre-processor** software used to create the geometry and generate the computational mesh.
> **FLUENT** is a **CFD solver** software used to define boundary conditions, solve the governing equations, and visualize the results.

Both were originally developed by Fluent Inc. and are now integrated into the **ANSYS Workbench** suite. However, in KTU 2024 syllabus, they are treated as **standalone educational CFD tools**.

### 📌 Snapshot Comparison

| Feature | GAMBIT | FLUENT |
|---|---|---|
| Role | Pre-processor | Solver & Post-processor |
| Function | Geometry + Meshing | Physics + Solution + Visualization |
| File extension | `.msh` (mesh file) | `.cas` (case) and `.dat` (data) |
| Numerical Method | Generates structured/unstructured mesh | Finite Volume Method (FVM) |
| Output | Mesh for FLUENT | Contours, vectors, graphs |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Book" 🍲

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Think of designing a food processing plant as **cooking a new recipe**.
> - **GAMBIT** = drawing the *recipe layout* and chopping ingredients into equal small pieces (meshing).
> - **FLUENT** = actually *cooking* on the stove, checking the temperature, and tasting (solving + post-processing).
> - The chef does NOT taste the entire pot at once — only small spoonfuls (this is exactly what FVM does on a mesh cell!).

---

## 1.4 Why CFD in the Food Industry? (KTU 2024 Relevance)

- Designing **plate heat exchangers** for pasteurization of milk 🥛
- Simulating **airflow in spray dryers** for milk powder
- Modeling **steam flow in retort sterilization** of canned foods
- Optimizing **mixing tanks** for sauces and beverages
- Studying **cold-store air circulation**
- Predicting **fouling and cleaning** (CIP) processes

> [!NOTE]
> Key physical quantities handled: **velocity (m/s)**, **pressure (Pa)**, **temperature (K)**, **turbulence intensity (%)**, **Reynolds number**, **Nusselt number**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CFD Workflow — Six Logical Steps

The hands-on practice follows a **sequential 6-step pipeline**:

1. **Define the Physical Problem** — Identify geometry, fluids, and operating conditions (e.g., milk flowing in a pipe at 70 °C).
2. **Build Geometry in GAMBIT** — Sketch 2D/3D domain using points → edges → faces → volumes.
3. **Generate Mesh in GAMBIT** — Divide domain into small *finite volumes* (cells). Common types:
   - *Structured mesh* (quad/hex) — high accuracy, simple geometry.
   - *Unstructured mesh* (tri/tet) — handles complex geometry.
4. **Export `.msh` File & Import to FLUENT** — Carry mesh into solver.
5. **Set Up Physics in FLUENT** — Choose solver (segregated/ coupled), turbulence model (k-ε), boundary conditions, material properties.
6. **Solve & Post-Process** — Run iterations → monitor residuals → generate contours, vectors, line plots.

---

## 2.2 Governing Equations — The "Heart" of FLUENT

FLUENT solves the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS)** equations for incompressible/ compressible flow.

### 🔹 2.2.1 Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)

For an incompressible fluid (most food liquids like milk, oil, juice):

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

For a compressible fluid (steam, air in dryers):

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

### 🔹 2.2.2 Momentum Equation (Navier–Stokes)

$$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

### 🔹 2.2.3 Energy Equation

$$\rho c_p \left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla T \right) = k \nabla^{2} T + \Phi$$

Where $\Phi$ is the viscous dissipation term.

### 🔹 2.2.4 Standard k-ε Turbulence Model

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho k)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho k u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] + G_k - \rho \varepsilon $$

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon u_i)}{\partial x_i} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] + C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}G_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\rho\frac{\varepsilon^{2}}{k} $$

Where $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \dfrac{k^{2}}{\varepsilon}$

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Meshing** → Because computers cannot solve continuous PDEs; they need discrete cells.
- **Boundary conditions** → Equations are incomplete without telling FLUENT *what happens at the edges* (inlet velocity, outlet pressure, wall temperature, etc.).
- **Residual monitoring** → Tells the student when the solution has *converged* (residuals < 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶).
- **Turbulence model selection** → Food flows are mostly turbulent (Re > 4000), so k-ε standard is the default educational choice.

---

## 2.4 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Inside this table, the vertical pipe symbol `|` is **strictly prohibited**. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead.

| Symbol | Meaning | Formula / Value | SI Unit | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Re | Reynolds Number | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Milk flow in pasteurizer |
| Nu | Nusselt Number | $\mathrm{Nu} = \dfrac{h D}{k}$ | dimensionless | Heat transfer in plate exchanger |
| Pr | Prandtl Number | $\mathrm{Pr} = \dfrac{c_p \mu}{k}$ | dimensionless | Edible oil heating |
| Pe | Péclet Number | $\mathrm{Pe} = \mathrm{Re} \cdot \mathrm{Pr}$ | dimensionless | Mass transfer in drying |
| $\mu_t$ | Turbulent viscosity | $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \dfrac{k^{2}}{\varepsilon}$ | Pa·s | Pipe flow turbulence |
| $G_k$ | Turbulence production | $G_k = \mu_t \left(\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)\dfrac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j}$ | W/m³ | Stirred tank |
| $y^+$ | Wall y-plus | $y^{+} = \dfrac{\rho u_\tau y}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | Near-wall mesh quality |
| $C_p$ | Specific heat | constant for incompressible | J/(kg·K) | Milk = 3.9 kJ/(kg·K) |
| $\rho$ | Density | input to FLUENT | kg/m³ | Milk ≈ 1030 kg/m³ |
| $\mu$ | Dynamic viscosity | input to FLUENT | Pa·s | Honey ≈ 10 Pa·s |
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | input to FLUENT | W/(m·K) | Water = 0.6 W/(m·K) |
| $p$ | Pressure | solved by FLUENT | Pa | Pump pressure |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The dimensionless numbers (Re, Nu, Pr) help the food engineer *scale up* results from a 1-liter lab tank to a 10,000-liter industrial tank — a topic called **Dimensional Analysis & Similarity**.

---

## 2.5 Boundary Conditions Used in Hands-on Practice

| Type | Description | Example in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|
| Velocity Inlet | $V = $ specified, $p$ extrapolated | Milk entering a pipe |
| Pressure Outlet | $p = $ atmospheric, $V$ extrapolated | Steam leaving a retort |
| Wall (no-slip) | $V = 0$, $T = T_{wall}$ | Heated plate of HTST pasteurizer |
| Symmetry | Reduces computation | Half of cylindrical dryer |
| Moving Wall | Rotational speed | Scraped-surface heat exchanger |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Cartesian Form)

We start with the **conservation of mass** for a control volume (a small cube of side $\delta x, \delta y, \delta z$).

**Step 1:** Mass entering in the $x$-direction at face $x$:

$$ (\rho u)_{x} \, \delta y \, \delta z $$

**Step 2:** Mass leaving in the $x$-direction at face $x + \delta x$:

$$ \left[ (\rho u)_{x} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\delta x \right] \delta y \, \delta z $$

**Step 3:** Net mass outflow in $x$:

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}\delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z $$

**Step 4:** Add similar terms for $y$ and $z$ directions. The total rate of mass increase inside the CV must equal **net inflow**:

$$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \delta x \delta y \delta z = -\left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} \right] \delta x \delta y \delta z $$

**Step 5:** Cancel the volume term and rearrange:

$$ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0 $$

This is the **continuity equation in differential form**. In vector notation:

$$ \boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0} $$

**Step 6 — For Incompressible Flow:** Since $\rho$ = constant, $\partial \rho / \partial t = 0$, giving:

$$ \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Navier–Stokes Equation (x-momentum)

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law** on a fluid element:

$$ \sum F_x = m \, a_x $$

**Step 2 — Body force** (gravity in x):

$$ F_{body} = \rho g_x \, \delta x \delta y \delta z $$

**Step 3 — Surface forces** = pressure + viscous shear stresses:

$$ F_{surface} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\delta x \delta y \delta z + \left(\frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z}\right)\delta x \delta y \delta z $$

**Step 4 — For a Newtonian fluid**, the viscous stress is:

$$ \tau_{xx} = 2\mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} - \frac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) $$

**Step 5 — Substitute and simplify** (using incompressible continuity $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$):

$$ \rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial z^{2}}\right) + \rho g_x $$

**Step 6 — Compact vector form** (Navier–Stokes):

$$ \boxed{\rho\left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}} $$

---

## 3.3 Discretization — How FLUENT Solves the Equations

FLUENT uses the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**. The continuous domain is divided into cells, and equations are integrated over each cell volume.

For a general transport variable $\phi$:

$$ \int_{CV} \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) dV = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) dV + \int_{CV} S_\phi \, dV $$

Applying the **divergence theorem** to the convective and diffusive terms:

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho \phi)}{\partial t} V_{cell} + \sum_{faces} (\rho \vec{V} \phi)_f \cdot \vec{A}_f = \sum_{faces} \Gamma_f (\nabla \phi)_f \cdot \vec{A}_f + S_\phi V_{cell} $$

This produces a **linear algebraic system** solved iteratively (e.g., SIMPLE algorithm for pressure–velocity coupling).

---

## 3.4 📊 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Solver Options in FLUENT

| Solver | Algorithm | Best For | Food Industry Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Segregated | Pressure-based, SIMPLE | Low-speed incompressible flow | Milk in pipelines, sauce mixing |
| Coupled | Pressure-based, all equations together | Strongly coupled flows | Steam in retort |
| Explicit | Density-based | High-speed compressible | Air blast freezing |
| Implicit | Density-based | High-speed steady | Spray dryer nozzle |

> [!TIP]
> For a typical B.Tech food-industry CFD lab, **Segregated + Standard k-ε + SIMPLE** is the most common combination.

---

## 3.5 Convergence Criteria — How to Know the Solution is Done

| Residual | Target Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Continuity | $< 10^{-3}$ | Mass is conserved |
| x-velocity | $< 10^{-3}$ | Momentum in x converged |
| y-velocity | $< 10^{-3}$ | Momentum in y converged |
| Energy | $< 10^{-6}$ | Temperature converged |
| k, ε | $< 10^{-3}$ | Turbulence converged |

> [!NOTE]
> If residuals plateau (do not drop), the mesh is too coarse OR boundary conditions are inconsistent — students must refine and re-run.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CFD Workflow — GAMBIT → FLUENT (Mermaid Flowchart)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords, labels are raw uppercase text (no bold or markdown tags inside quotes).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 PROBLEM DEFINITION] --> B[STEP 2 GEOMETRY IN GAMBIT]
    B --> C[STEP 3 MESH GENERATION]
    C --> D[STEP 4 MESH QUALITY CHECK]
    D --> E[STEP 5 EXPORT MSH FILE]
    E --> F[STEP 6 IMPORT TO FLUENT]
    F --> G[STEP 7 SET MATERIAL PROPERTIES]
    G --> H[STEP 8 BOUNDARY CONDITIONS]
    H --> I[STEP 9 SOLVER SETTINGS]
    I --> J[STEP 10 RUN ITERATIONS]
    J --> K{RESIDUALS CONVERGED}
    K -- NO --> L[REFINE MESH OR ADJUST BC]
    L --> C
    K -- YES --> M[STEP 11 POST PROCESSING]
    M --> N[CONTOURS VECTORS PLOTS]
    N --> O[VALIDATE WITH EXPERIMENT]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of GAMBIT-FLUENT Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GAMBIT_PRE["GAMBIT MODULE PRE PROCESSOR"]
        A1[GEOMETRY CREATOR] --> A2[MESH GENERATOR]
        A2 --> A3[BOUNDARY ZONE LABELS]
    end

    A3 -->|MSH FILE| B1

    subgraph FLUENT_SOL["FLUENT MODULE SOLVER"]
        B1[CASE SETUP] --> B2[MATERIAL DATABASE]
        B2 --> B3[BOUNDARY CONDITION MAPPER]
        B3 --> B4[SOLVER ENGINE FVM]
        B4 --> B5[RESIDUAL MONITOR]
    end

    B5 -->|CONVERGED| C1

    subgraph POST["POST PROCESSING"]
        C1[CONTOUR PLOTS] --> C2[VECTOR PLOTS]
        C2 --> C3[STREAMLINE ANIMATION]
        C3 --> C4[DATA EXPORT CSV]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Pressure–Velocity Coupling (SIMPLE Algorithm) — Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INITIALIZE PRESSURE FIELD P0] --> P2[SOLVE MOMENTUM EQUATIONS]
    P2 --> P3[CALCULATE PRESSURE CORRECTION P PRIME]
    P3 --> P4[CORRECT PRESSURE AND VELOCITY]
    P4 --> P5[CHECK CONVERGENCE]
    P5 -- NOT CONVERGED --> P2
    P5 -- CONVERGED --> P6[OUTPUT FINAL SOLUTION]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility:** SIMPLE (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations) is the default in FLUENT for *incompressible* food flows such as milk pasteurization, where pressure and velocity are tightly interlinked.

---

## 4.4 Typical Food-Industry CFD Problem Setup — Tubular Heat Exchanger

| Element | GAMBIT Action | FLUENT Action |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Cylinder, length L, diameter D | — |
| Mesh | Cooper scheme, 50,000 cells | — |
| Boundary 1 | Inlet face | Velocity-inlet (V = 0.5 m/s) |
| Boundary 2 | Outlet face | Pressure-outlet (p = 0 Pa gauge) |
| Boundary 3 | Pipe wall | Wall, T = 75 °C |
| Fluid | — | Water-liquid (milk surrogate) |
| Solver | — | Segregated, Steady, 2D axisymmetric |
| Model | — | Laminar (Re < 2300) or k-ε |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define GAMBIT and FLUENT. State two key functions of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **GAMBIT (1 mark):** It is a pre-processor software used to create the geometry of the computational domain and generate the mesh (grid) of finite-volume cells.
- **Functions of GAMBIT (1 mark):**
  1. Geometry creation (points, edges, faces, volumes).
  2. Mesh generation (structured/unstructured) and boundary zone labeling.
- **FLUENT (1 mark):** It is a CFD solver software that solves the governing fluid flow and heat/mass transfer equations using the **Finite Volume Method** and produces visual results.
- *Note: The above can be split as 2 functions each for 1 mark, depending on KTU valuation key.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List any three boundary conditions commonly applied in FLUENT for a food processing CFD problem. Give an example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Velocity Inlet** — Example: Milk entering the holding tube of an HTST pasteurizer at 1.5 m/s.
2. **Pressure Outlet** — Example: Steam leaving a retort at atmospheric pressure.
3. **Wall (no-slip, fixed temperature)** — Example: Heated plate surface at 72 °C in a plate heat exchanger.
4. *(Bonus, only if asked)* **Symmetry** — Half-domain of a cylindrical dryer.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Always draw a **neat labeled sketch** of the geometry — students lose 1–2 marks for skipping this.
> - Quote the **governing equation(s)** relevant to the problem (Continuity, Navier–Stokes, Energy) — students lose 2 marks otherwise.
> - Specify the **turbulence model** (laminar, k-ε, k-ω) and **reason for choice** — KTU 2024 explicitly tests this.
> - Mention **convergence criteria** (residual values) — examiners want to see numerical-method awareness.
> - In post-processing questions, describe **at least two visualization tools** (contours, vectors, streamlines, XY-plots).

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Internal Choice 1)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

> A 2 m long, 5 cm diameter stainless-steel pipe carries milk at a flow rate of 0.5 L/s. The pipe wall is maintained at 72 °C for pasteurization, and the inlet milk temperature is 4 °C. The kinematic viscosity of milk is $\nu = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ m²/s, density $\rho = 1030$ kg/m³, thermal conductivity $k = 0.6$ W/(m·K), and specific heat $c_p = 3900$ J/(kg·K).
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Build the **step-by-step procedure** to simulate this problem using GAMBIT and FLUENT. List all geometry, mesh, boundary conditions, and solver settings.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Using the given data, calculate the **Reynolds number**, **Prandtl number**, and state whether the flow is **laminar or turbulent**. Suggest an appropriate turbulence model and justify.

---

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Procedure [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition (½ mark):** Steady-state, axisymmetric, internal flow of milk with conjugate heat transfer.

**Step 2 — Geometry in GAMBIT (1 mark):**
- Create a rectangle: length = 2 m, height = 0.025 m (axisymmetric half-width).
- Or, simpler: full 2D pipe cross-section with axisymmetric option in FLUENT.

**Step 3 — Mesh Generation (1 mark):**
- Edge sizing on inlet/outlet = 200 nodes.
- Face mesh: **Quad, Map** (structured) → Cooper scheme.
- Spacing 0.5 mm near wall to capture thermal boundary layer.

**Step 4 — Boundary Zone Labels in GAMBIT (1 mark):**
- `inlet` (left face)
- `outlet` (right face)
- `wall_pipe` (top & bottom edges, heated)
- `axis` (centerline for axisymmetric case)

**Step 5 — Export & Import to FLUENT (½ mark):** Save as `.msh` → File → Import → Mesh.

**Step 6 — Setup in FLUENT (2 marks):**
- General: 2D, Axisymmetric, Steady.
- Solver: Segregated, Pressure-based, SIMPLE.
- Energy: **ON**.
- Viscous model: **k-ε Standard** (or Laminar if Re < 2300 — calculated in part b).
- Materials: Create `milk-liquid` with $\rho = 1030$ kg/m³, $c_p = 3900$ J/(kg·K), $k = 0.6$ W/(m·K), $\mu = 1.03 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- BCs:
  - `inlet` → Velocity-inlet, $V = 0.5 \text{ L/s} \div (\pi D^{2}/4) = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}/(1.963 \times 10^{-3}) = 0.255$ m/s, T = 4 °C.
  - `outlet` → Pressure-outlet, gauge p = 0, Backflow T = 4 °C.
  - `wall_pipe` → No-slip, Fixed T = 72 °C.
  - `axis` → Axis.

**Step 7 — Solution & Convergence (½ mark):** Initialize from inlet → Run 500 iterations → Monitor continuity, x-velocity, energy → Target $10^{-6}$ for energy, $10^{-3}$ for flow.

**Step 8 — Post-Processing (½ mark):** Generate temperature contours, velocity vectors, line plot of T vs. x at mid-radius.

---

**Part (b) — Calculations & Model Justification [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Velocity (1 mark):**

$$ V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{3}/\text{s}}{\pi (0.05)^{2}/4} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} $$

$$ \boxed{V = 0.2548 \text{ m/s}} $$

**Step 2 — Reynolds Number (2 marks):**

$$ \mathrm{Re} = \frac{V D}{\nu} = \frac{0.2548 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ \mathrm{Re} = 1.274 \times 10^{4} $$

**Step 3 — Flow Regime (1 mark):**

$$ \mathrm{Re} = 12{,}740 > 4000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\text{Flow is TURBULENT}} $$

**Step 4 — Dynamic Viscosity (½ mark):**

$$ \mu = \rho \nu = 1030 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 1.03 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} $$

**Step 5 — Prandtl Number (2 marks):**

$$ \mathrm{Pr} = \frac{c_p \, \mu}{k} = \frac{3900 \times 1.03 \times 10^{-3}}{0.6} $$

$$ \mathrm{Pr} = \frac{4.017}{0.6} $$

$$ \boxed{\mathrm{Pr} = 6.695} $$

**Step 6 — Turbulence Model Justification (½ mark):**
> Since flow is turbulent and Re ≈ 12,740 (moderately turbulent), the **Standard k-ε model** with enhanced wall treatment is recommended — it is robust, computationally cheap, and accurate for internal flows like pipes.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice 2)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

> A spray dryer is used to convert liquid milk into powder. Hot air enters the dryer at 200 °C through a circular inlet of 0.2 m diameter at a velocity of 10 m/s, and the walls are insulated. The air properties are $\rho = 0.946$ kg/m³, $\mu = 2.57 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa·s, $k = 0.038$ W/(m·K), $c_p = 1009$ J/(kg·K).
>
> **(a)** [7 marks] Explain in detail the **step-by-step methodology** of performing a CFD analysis for this dryer using GAMBIT and FLUENT, with emphasis on geometry, meshing, BCs, and turbulence model selection.
>
> **(b)** [7 marks] Compute the **Reynolds number**, **Nusselt number** (using Dittus–Boelter correlation), and the **convective heat transfer coefficient** $h$. State two post-processing outputs you would generate.

---

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Methodology [7 marks]**

1. **Problem Definition (1 mark):** Internal, steady, 3D turbulent flow of air in cylindrical dryer; energy equation included; adiabatic walls.

2. **Geometry in GAMBIT (1.5 marks):** Cylindrical volume, diameter = 0.2 m, length = 2 m. Create via cylinder face primitive → extrude.

3. **Meshing (1.5 marks):** Tet/Hybrid mesh, 100,000 cells. Inflation layers (5 layers, growth ratio 1.2) near wall for resolving the thermal boundary layer.

4. **Boundary Conditions (1.5 marks):**
   - `inlet` → Velocity-inlet, $V = 10$ m/s, $T = 200$ °C, Turbulence intensity = 5 %, Hydraulic diameter = 0.2 m.
   - `outlet` → Pressure-outlet, gauge p = 0.
   - `wall` → No-slip, Adiabatic (heat flux = 0).

5. **Solver Setup (1 mark):** 3D, Steady, Pressure-based, Segregated, SIMPLE, Energy ON, Viscous: k-ε Realizable (better for swirling/recirculating flow), Materials: air.

6. **Solution & Convergence (½ mark):** Initialize → 1000 iterations → residuals below $10^{-3}$ (continuity, velocity) and $10^{-6}$ (energy).

**Part (b) — Calculations & Post-Processing [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Reynolds Number (2 marks):**

$$ \mathrm{Re} = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{0.946 \times 10 \times 0.2}{2.57 \times 10^{-5}} $$

$$ \mathrm{Re} = \frac{1.892}{2.57 \times 10^{-5}} = 7.36 \times 10^{4} $$

**Step 2 — Flow Regime (½ mark):** $\mathrm{Re} = 73{,}600 \gg 4000$ → Fully turbulent.

**Step 3 — Prandtl Number (1 mark):**

$$ \mathrm{Pr} = \frac{c_p \mu}{k} = \frac{1009 \times 2.57 \times 10^{-5}}{0.038} = 0.682 $$

**Step 4 — Nusselt Number (Dittus–Boelter) (2 marks):**

$$ \mathrm{Nu} = 0.023 \, \mathrm{Re}^{0.8} \, \mathrm{Pr}^{0.4} $$

$$ \mathrm{Nu} = 0.023 \times (7.36 \times 10^{4})^{0.8} \times (0.682)^{0.4} $$

$$ (7.36 \times 10^{4})^{0.8} \approx 6{,}038 $$

$$ (0.682)^{0.4} \approx 0.862 $$

$$ \mathrm{Nu} = 0.023 \times 6{,}038 \times 0.862 = 119.7 $$

**Step 5 — Convective Heat Transfer Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$ h = \frac{\mathrm{Nu} \cdot k}{D} = \frac{119.7 \times 0.038}{0.2} $$

$$ \boxed{h = 22.74 \text{ W/(m}^{2}\text{·K)}} $$

**Step 6 — Two Post-Processing Outputs (½ mark):**
1. **Static Temperature Contours** on a longitudinal plane — to visualize thermal development.
2. **Velocity Vectors** at the inlet cross-section — to check uniform inflow.
3. *(Alternative)* **Streamlines** colored by temperature — to track air parcel heating.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize for KTU ESE):**

- ⭐ **GAMBIT** = pre-processor (geometry + mesh). Output = `.msh` file.
- ⭐ **FLUENT** = solver (governing PDEs + FVM). Output = contours, vectors, plots.
- ⭐ The complete workflow has **6 main steps**: Define → Geometry → Mesh → Setup → Solve → Post-process.
- ⭐ **Three governing equations** in food CFD: **Continuity, Navier–Stokes, Energy**.
- ⭐ For **incompressible** flow (milk, oil, juice): $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- ⭐ For **compressible** flow (steam, air in dryers): $\partial \rho/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
- ⭐ **Default solver settings** in KTU lab: Segregated, Pressure-based, **SIMPLE**, Steady, 2D/3D as required.
- ⭐ **Default turbulence model**: Standard k-ε (use Realizable for swirling flow in dryers/mixers).
- ⭐ **Flow regime rule of thumb**: $\mathrm{Re} < 2300$ → Laminar; $2300 < \mathrm{Re} < 4000$ → Transitional; $\mathrm{Re} > 4000$ → Turbulent.
- ⭐ **Residual targets**: Continuity & velocities $< 10^{-3}$; Energy $< 10^{-6}$.
- ⭐ **Dittus–Boelter** (heating): $\mathrm{Nu} = 0.023 \, \mathrm{Re}^{0.8} \, \mathrm{Pr}^{0.4}$.
- ⭐ **Sieder–Tate** (large viscosity variation): $\mathrm{Nu} = 0.027 \, \mathrm{Re}^{0.8} \, \mathrm{Pr}^{1/3} (\mu/\mu_w)^{0.14}$.
- ⭐ **Wall boundary conditions** in food CFD: usually **no-slip** with either fixed $T$ or fixed heat flux.
- ⭐ **Inflation layers** are mandatory near walls to resolve $y^{+} \approx 1$ in low-Re turbulence modeling.
- ⭐ Always **validate** CFD results with experimental data (e.g., pasteurization temperature measured at outlet) — KTU emphasizes this.
- ⭐ **Incompressible assumption** is valid for **Mach number < 0.3** — true for most liquid food flows.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "G-F-L-I-S-P":**
> **G**eometry → **F**low setup → **L**oad mesh → **I**terate → **S**ee residuals → **P**ost-process. This is the exact sequence on the FLUENT GUI ribbon.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is LabVIEW?

> [!NOTE]
> **LabVIEW** stands for **Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench**. It is a system-design platform and development environment developed by **National Instruments (NI)** that uses a graphical programming language (G-language) instead of text-based code.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, LabVIEW is classified as a **Graphical System Design (GSD) environment** that allows engineers and scientists to interface with real-world signals, perform data acquisition, implement control logic, and analyze results — all through **Virtual Instruments (VIs)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a traditional food processing plant where a technician manually:
1. Looks at a temperature gauge on a pasteurizer,
2. Records the value in a logbook every 10 minutes,
3. Adjusts a steam valve by hand to maintain target temperature.

Now imagine replacing that technician with a **software dashboard on a computer screen** that:
- Shows the temperature as a digital number AND a trend graph,
- Logs data automatically to a hard drive,
- Automatically adjusts the steam valve to maintain the correct temperature,
- Sends an SMS to the manager if temperature goes out of range.

**That dashboard IS LabVIEW.** It turns the computer into a "virtual instrument" — a software version of an oscilloscope, thermometer, or PID controller. Instead of buying separate physical instruments, you create them on-screen using graphical blocks (icons) connected by wires.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition (Board Exam Standard):**
> *LabVIEW is a graphical programming environment developed by National Instruments in 1986, used for data acquisition, instrument control, and industrial automation. It uses dataflow programming where execution order is determined by the flow of data through wires connecting functional nodes, rather than by sequential textual lines of code.*

---

## 1.2 What is Data Acquisition (DAQ)?

> [!NOTE]
> **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of **sampling real-world physical signals** (such as temperature, pressure, pH, flow rate) and converting them into a digital format that a computer can process, analyze, and store.

### The Three Pillars of any DAQ System

| Pillar | Function | Food Industry Example |
|--------|----------|----------------------|
| **Sensor / Transducer** | Converts physical quantity into electrical signal (voltage/current) | Thermocouple measuring milk temperature during pasteurization |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Amplifies, filters, and isolates the signal to make it suitable for the ADC | Cold-junction compensation for thermocouple |
| **DAQ Hardware (ADC)** | Converts analog electrical signal into digital number (binary) | NI-DAQ card with 16-bit ADC sampling at 1 kS/s |

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of DAQ as **teaching a computer to "hear" the physical world**:
- Microphones translate sound → voltage → digital audio (your PC plays music).
- Thermocouples translate heat → millivolts → digital temperature (your PC logs pasteurizer data).

The entire DAQ chain is essentially: **Physical World → Sensor → Electrical Signal → ADC → Digital Data → Software**.

---

## 1.3 LabVIEW in the Context of Food Industry (ICT Context)

In a B.Tech Food Technology / Food Engineering context, LabVIEW is used for:

- **Process Monitoring**: Continuous logging of temperature, pressure, humidity in ovens, fermenters, cold storage.
- **Quality Control Automation**: Automated inspection of color, viscosity, moisture content via sensor arrays.
- **Process Control**: Closed-loop control of pasteurization (HTST), sterilization (retort), fermentation pH.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Electronic batch records required by FSSAI, HACCP, ISO 22000.
- **R&D Lab Automation**: Sensory analysis panels, texture analyzer integration, gas chromatography data capture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric Highlighted for KTU Board:**
> - ADC Resolution: **16-bit** (most common NI DAQ cards)
> - Quantization Levels: $2^{16} = \mathbf{65{,}536}$ discrete levels
> - Common Sample Rate: **1 kS/s to 100 kS/s** (kilo-Samples per second)
> - Thermocouple Range (Type K): **-200 °C to +1372 °C**

---

## 1.4 Virtual Instrument (VI) — The Fundamental Building Block

The fundamental program unit in LabVIEW is the **VI (Virtual Instrument)**. Every VI consists of three core components:

1. **Front Panel** — The User Interface (knobs, dials, graphs, numeric displays).
2. **Block Diagram** — The Graphical Source Code (icons, wires, structures).
3. **Icon / Connector** — The graphical representation allowing the VI to be used as a sub-VI (subroutine).

This is analogous to a function in C: a VI has inputs (controls), outputs (indicators), and executable code (block diagram).

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a LabVIEW DAQ System — Layered Model

The complete DAQ-and-Control stack in LabVIEW can be understood as **five functional layers**, each interacting with the layer above and below it:

### Layer 1: Physical / Sensor Layer
- Real-world phenomena: temperature, pressure, flow, pH, conductivity, optical density.
- Sensors convert these into analog electrical signals (mV, mA, V).
- **Standard signal ranges**: 0–5 V, 0–10 V, 4–20 mA (current loop is preferred in food plants due to noise immunity).

### Layer 2: Signal Conditioning Layer
- **Functions performed**:
  - Amplification (raising low-amplitude sensor signals to ADC range).
  - Filtering (removing 50/60 Hz mains interference using notch filters).
  - Isolation (protecting the DAQ card from voltage spikes).
  - Linearization (converting non-linear sensor response to linear output).
  - Cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples).
- **Standard Example**: NI SCXI chassis, NI SCC modules.

### Layer 3: DAQ Hardware Layer
- Houses the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**.
- Common NI cards: **NI USB-6008, NI USB-6009, NI PCIe-6321, NI cDAQ-9174**.
- Specifications governed by:
  - **Resolution** (bits).
  - **Sampling Rate** (samples per second).
  - **Number of Channels** (single-ended vs. differential).
  - **Input Range** (e.g., ±10 V).

### Layer 4: Driver Layer (NI-DAQmx)
- Software driver that exposes low-level hardware registers as high-level functions.
- Provides API for: **DAQ Assistant, Task Configuration, Read/Write operations, Timing, Triggering**.
- This is the bridge between hardware and the LabVIEW graphical code.

### Layer 5: Application Layer (LabVIEW VIs)
- The VI (Virtual Instrument) the user creates.
- Implements: data logging, real-time display, alarm logic, control algorithms (PID), file I/O, network communication.

---

## 2.2 Data Flow Programming Paradigm (Why this matters for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LabVIEW executes code based on the principle of DATA FLOW, not sequential text reading.** A node executes only when ALL its input terminals have received data. This is fundamentally different from C, Python, or MATLAB.

### Why this matters in DAQ/control:
- Naturally supports **parallel execution** (e.g., reading temperature and pressure simultaneously).
- Eliminates race conditions in real-time control because output cannot be produced before input arrives.
- Mirrors the physical reality of a control system: a valve cannot be opened before the setpoint is defined.

---

## 2.3 Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC) — Core Mathematics

The ADC is the heart of DAQ. Understanding its math is essential for KTU board problems.

### Key Quantities:

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|------|
| ADC Resolution | n | Number of bits in the digital output | bits |
| Number of Quantization Levels | L | $L = 2^n$ | levels |
| Input Voltage Range | $V_{FSR}$ | Full-scale range (max − min) | Volts |
| Least Significant Bit (LSB) size | $\Delta V$ | Smallest detectable voltage change | Volts |
| Sampling Rate | $f_s$ | Number of samples acquired per second | Hz (or S/s) |
| Nyquist Frequency | $f_N$ | $f_N = f_s / 2$ | Hz |

### KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $L = 2^n$ | Number of discrete quantization levels |
| 2 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$ | Voltage represented by 1 LSB (quantization step) |
| 3 | $f_N = \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Nyquist frequency (max signal frequency acquirable without aliasing) |
| 4 | $V_{in,digital} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{FSR}} \cdot (2^n - 1)$ | Decimal count output by ADC |
| 5 | $e_{q,max} = \pm \dfrac{\Delta V}{2}$ | Maximum quantization error |
| 6 | $\text{SNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ (dB) | Theoretical signal-to-noise ratio (for ideal ADC) |
| 7 | $u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output (continuous form) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Vertical Bar Usage in Tables**: For absolute value notation in tables, use `\vert V_{in} \vert` — never the literal pipe symbol `|`, as it breaks markdown table syntax.

### Real-World Utility in Food Engineering
- A **Type-K thermocouple** with NI USB-6009 (14-bit ADC, ±10 V range) yields $\Delta V \approx 1.22$ mV per LSB.
- For a pasteurizer where temperature must be controlled to ±0.5 °C (≈ 20 µV from thermocouple), **14-bit resolution is insufficient** — 16-bit or higher is needed. This kind of calculation is a **favourite KTU board question**.

---

## 2.4 PID Control in LabVIEW (Process Control Layer)

The most common control algorithm implemented in LabVIEW VIs for food process control is the **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative)** controller.

### The Three Terms Explained:

- **Proportional (P)**: Output reacts proportionally to current error. Quick response but causes steady-state offset.
- **Integral (I)**: Accumulates past errors. Eliminates steady-state offset, but can cause overshoot.
- **Derivative (D)**: Reacts to rate of change. Damps oscillations, improves stability.

### Discrete (Digital) Form Used in LabVIEW:

Since LabVIEW operates on **discrete samples**, the continuous integral and derivative are approximated:

$$u[k] = K_p \cdot e[k] + K_i \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] \cdot \Delta t + K_d \cdot \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

where:
- $u[k]$ = controller output at sample $k$ (e.g., valve opening %).
- $e[k]$ = error at sample $k$ = setpoint − measured value.
- $\Delta t$ = sampling interval = $1/f_s$.
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$ and $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ are alternative forms.

### Application in Food Industry
- **HTST Pasteurization**: Maintaining milk at exactly 72 °C for 15 seconds. PID controls steam valve.
- **Fermentation pH Control**: Maintaining pH 4.5 in yogurt fermentation. PID controls acid/base pump speed.
- **Cold Storage Humidity**: Maintaining 85% RH in cheese ripening rooms.

---

## 2.5 DAQmx — The Modern Driver Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **NI-DAQmx** is the current-generation driver set for all modern NI DAQ hardware. KTU students must know the four core concepts:
> 1. **Task** — A collection of one or more channels with timing, triggering, and other properties.
> 2. **Channel** — A physical or virtual input/output (e.g., "AI0 = thermocouple on fermenter 1").
> 3. **Timing** — Specifies sample rate, sample mode (finite, continuous, hardware-timed).
> 4. **Triggering** — Specifies start condition (digital edge, analog threshold, software).

A typical LabVIEW DAQmx sequence: **Create Task → Configure Channel → Configure Timing → Start Task → Read Data → Stop & Clear Task**.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Quantization Step (LSB Voltage) of a DAQ Card

> **Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark style):**
> An NI DAQ card has a 16-bit ADC and an input voltage range of −10 V to +10 V. Calculate the quantization step size (LSB voltage) and the maximum quantization error.

### Given:
- Resolution: $n = 16$ bits
- Input range: $V_{min} = -10$ V, $V_{max} = +10$ V
- Full-scale range: $V_{FSR} = V_{max} - V_{min}$

### Step 1 — Compute Full-Scale Range

$$V_{FSR} = V_{max} - V_{min}$$

$$V_{FSR} = (+10) - (-10) = 20 \text{ V}$$

This is the total voltage span that the ADC can digitize. Note that the **span** (20 V) is not the same as the **maximum measurable voltage** (10 V).

### Step 2 — Compute Number of Quantization Levels

$$L = 2^n$$

$$L = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ levels}$$

This means the ADC can produce **65,536 distinct digital codes**, from 0 to 65,535.

### Step 3 — Compute the Quantization Step Size (LSB)

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{20 \text{ V}}{65{,}536}$$

$$\Delta V = 3.05176 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 305.18 \text{ μV} = 0.305 \text{ mV}$$

This is the **smallest distinguishable voltage change** that the ADC can resolve.

### Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Quantization Error

The actual input can be anywhere within ±½ LSB of the nearest digital code:

$$e_{q,max} = \pm \frac{\Delta V}{2}$$

$$e_{q,max} = \pm \frac{0.305 \text{ mV}}{2}$$

$$e_{q,max} = \pm 0.1526 \text{ mV}$$

### Step 5 — Interpret in Food Engineering Terms

A Type-K thermocouple produces roughly **41 μV per °C**. The ±0.1526 mV error therefore translates to:

$$\Delta T_{error} = \frac{0.1526 \text{ mV}}{0.041 \text{ mV/°C}} \approx \pm 3.72 \text{ °C}$$

This is **unacceptable** for pasteurization (where ±0.5 °C is needed). Conclusion: **16-bit ADC at ±10 V range is borderline** for thermocouple-based food temperature control. Either:
- Use a higher-resolution ADC (18-bit or 24-bit), OR
- Use signal conditioning with amplifier gain (e.g., gain = 100) to map 0–100 °C onto the full ADC range.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Sampling Rate (Nyquist Criterion)

> **Problem (KTU 3-mark style):**
> A food packaging line uses a vibration sensor on a conveyor motor. The motor has a maximum rotational vibration frequency of 380 Hz. What is the minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing?

### Step 1 — Identify Nyquist Criterion

The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states that to reconstruct a signal without aliasing, the sampling frequency must be at least **twice the highest frequency component** present in the signal:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Known Maximum Frequency

$$f_s \geq 2 \times 380 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_s \geq 760 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Engineering Safety Factor

In practice, KTU/industry standard uses a safety factor of **2.5× to 10×** the Nyquist rate to account for real-world issues (filter roll-off, non-ideal anti-aliasing filter):

$$f_s^{practical} = k \cdot 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

Taking $k = 5$:

$$f_s^{practical} = 5 \times 760 \text{ Hz} = 3{,}800 \text{ Hz} \approx 4 \text{ kHz}$$

So a DAQ card capable of at least **4 kS/s** on that channel is required.

### Step 4 — Verify with the Nyquist Frequency

$$f_N = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{3{,}800}{2} = 1{,}900 \text{ Hz}$$

Since $1{,}900 \text{ Hz} > 380 \text{ Hz}$, the vibration signal will be captured without aliasing. ✅

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: 4–20 mA Current Loop Signal Conversion

> **Problem (KTU 4-mark style):**
> A pressure transmitter in a retort sterilizer outputs a 4–20 mA current proportional to pressure 0–4 bar. The signal is passed through a 250 Ω precision resistor before reaching the ADC with input range 0–10 V. Determine the voltage range at the ADC, and the digital code when the pressure is 2.5 bar. (Assume 12-bit ADC.)

### Step 1 — Convert Current Range to Voltage Range Across Resistor

Using Ohm's law, $V = I \cdot R$:

$$V_{min} = 4 \text{ mA} \times 250 \text{ Ω} = 1.0 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{max} = 20 \text{ mA} \times 250 \text{ Ω} = 5.0 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Full-Scale Range and Quantization Step

$$V_{FSR} = V_{max} - V_{min} = 5.0 - 1.0 = 4.0 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{4.0}{2^{12}} = \frac{4.0}{4{,}096} = 9.766 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 0.977 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Voltage at 2.5 bar

Since 4 mA ↔ 0 bar and 20 mA ↔ 4 bar, the pressure-to-current relationship is **linear**:

$$I = 4 \text{ mA} + \left(\frac{P}{4 \text{ bar}}\right) \times (20 - 4) \text{ mA}$$

For $P = 2.5$ bar:

$$I = 4 + \left(\frac{2.5}{4}\right) \times 16 = 4 + 10 = 14 \text{ mA}$$

Voltage across resistor:

$$V = 14 \text{ mA} \times 250 \text{ Ω} = 3.5 \text{ V}$$

### Step 4 — Compute the Digital Code

The ADC outputs digital code $D$ according to:

$$D = \frac{V_{in} - V_{min}}{\Delta V}$$

$$D = \frac{3.5 - 1.0}{0.000977} = \frac{2.5}{0.000977}$$

$$D \approx 2{,}560 \text{ counts}$$

Out of 4,096 maximum counts, this represents **62.5%** of full scale — which corresponds correctly to 2.5 bar / 4 bar = 62.5%. ✅

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why 4–20 mA and not 0–20 mA?**
> Because 4 mA is the "live zero". If the wire breaks, current drops to 0 mA — which is **distinguishable from a legitimate 0 bar reading**. This is a critical safety feature in food plants (HACCP, FSSAI compliance).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Discrete PID Step Response Tuning (Ziegler-Nichols)

> **Problem (KTU 5-mark style):**
> A temperature control loop in a milk pasteurizer has ultimate gain $K_u = 8$ and ultimate period $T_u = 60$ s. Using Ziegler-Nichols tuning rules for a PID controller, calculate $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$.

### Step 1 — Recall Ziegler-Nichols PID Formula (Closed-Loop Tuning)

$$K_p = 0.6 \cdot K_u$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \cdot T_u$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \cdot T_u$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Values

$$K_p = 0.6 \times 8 = 4.8$$

$$T_i = 0.5 \times 60 = 30 \text{ s}$$

$$T_d = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5 \text{ s}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Discrete PID Constants Used in LabVIEW

In LabVIEW's discrete PID implementation:

$$K_i^{discrete} = \frac{K_p \cdot \Delta t}{T_i}$$

$$K_d^{discrete} = \frac{K_p \cdot T_d}{\Delta t}$$

Assuming sampling interval $\Delta t = 1$ s (typical for thermal processes):

$$K_i^{discrete} = \frac{4.8 \times 1}{30} = 0.16 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

$$K_d^{discrete} = \frac{4.8 \times 7.5}{1} = 36.0 \text{ s}$$

### Step 4 — Final PID Equation in Discrete Form

$$u[k] = 4.8 \cdot e[k] + 0.16 \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + 36.0 \cdot \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{1}$$

This is the controller equation that gets implemented in the LabVIEW block diagram inside a While Loop with shift registers (to remember previous error $e[k-1]$).

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: LabVIEW DAQ + Control System Architecture for a Food Process

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph phys["PHYSICAL PROCESS LAYER"]
        fermenter["Fermenter / Pasteurizer / Retort Vessel"]
        product["Food Product inside Vessel"]
    end

    subgraph sensor["SENSOR LAYER"]
        tc["Thermocouple Type-K"]
        pt["Pressure Transmitter 4-20mA"]
        ph["pH Probe"]
        flow["Flow Meter"]
    end

    subgraph cond["SIGNAL CONDITIONING LAYER"]
        amp["Amplifier + Filter Module"]
        iso["Galvanic Isolation"]
        cjc["Cold-Junction Compensation"]
    end

    subgraph hw["DAQ HARDWARE LAYER"]
        adc["Analog-to-Digital Converter 16-bit"]
        dio["Digital I/O for Valve Control"]
        ao["Analog Output for Variable Pump"]
    end

    subgraph drv["DRIVER LAYER"]
        daqmx["NI-DAQmx Driver"]
        task["DAQmx Task Configuration"]
    end

    subgraph lv["LABVIEW APPLICATION LAYER"]
        fp["Front Panel UI Dials Graphs Alarms"]
        bd["Block Diagram Data Flow Code"]
        pid["PID Controller VI"]
        log["Data Logging to TDMS File"]
        net["Network Publish to HMI SCADA"]
    end

    subgraph act["ACTUATOR LAYER"]
        valve["Steam Valve Positioner"]
        pump["Peristaltic Pump for Acid"]
        motor["Conveyor Motor VFD"]
    end

    fermenter --> tc
    fermenter --> pt
    fermenter --> ph
    fermenter --> flow

    tc --> cjc
    pt --> iso
    ph --> amp
    flow --> amp

    cjc --> adc
    iso --> adc
    amp --> adc

    adc --> daqmx
    daqmx --> task
    task --> bd

    dio --> valve
    ao --> pump

    bd --> pid
    pid --> dio
    pid --> ao
    bd --> fp
    bd --> log
    bd --> net

    valve --> fermenter
    pump --> fermenter
    motor --> fermenter

    product -.Temperature Pressure pH.-> fermenter
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **physical process** (left) generates real-world variables (temperature, pH, pressure, flow).
- These are sensed by transducers → conditioned → digitized by ADC.
- The NI-DAQmx driver exposes this data to LabVIEW.
- The LabVIEW VI executes the PID control algorithm and writes outputs to actuators.
- The actuators (valves, pumps, motors) act back on the physical process — closing the loop.
- Simultaneously, the VI displays data on the front panel, logs it, and publishes it to the plant SCADA system.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: LabVIEW VI Internal Architecture (Front Panel + Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph fp["FRONT PANEL — User Interface"]
        ctrl_setpt["Numeric Control: Setpoint Temperature"]
        ctrl_kp["Knob: Kp Gain"]
        ctrl_ki["Knob: Ki Gain"]
        ctrl_kd["Knob: Kd Gain"]
        ind_temp["Numeric Indicator: Measured Temp"]
        ind_out["Gauge: Valve Output %"]
        graph_trend["Waveform Chart: Live Trend"]
        led_alarm["LED: High Temperature Alarm"]
    end

    subgraph bd["BLOCK DIAGRAM — Graphical Code"]
        whileLoop["While Loop with Stop Button"]
        daqmxRead["DAQmx Read VI"]
        subtract["Subtract Function Setpoint minus Measured"]
        pidvi["PID VI Subroutine"]
        daqmxWrite["DAQmx Write VI"]
        shiftReg["Shift Register for Previous Error"]
        fileWrite["Write to Measurement File TDMS"]
    end

    subgraph icon["ICON AND CONNECTOR Pane"]
        iconRep["Graphical Icon representing the PID VI"]
        connPins["Connector Terminals for Setpoint Measurement Output Kp Ki Kd"]
    end

    ctrl_setpt -.wired to.-> bd
    ind_temp -.wired from.-> bd
    graph_trend -.displays.-> bd

    whileLoop --> daqmxRead
    daqmxRead --> subtract
    ctrl_setpt --> subtract
    subtract --> pidvi
    pidvi --> daqmxWrite
    pidvi --> shiftReg
    shiftReg --> pidvi
    daqmxRead --> fileWrite

    ctrl_kp --> pidvi
    ctrl_ki --> pidvi
    ctrl_kd --> pidvi

    daqmxWrite --> valve[Physical Actuator]
    pidvi --> ind_out
    pidvi --> led_alarm

    bd --- iconRep
    iconRep --- connPins
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Front Panel** has user-adjustable controls (setpoint, gains) and read-out indicators (temperature, valve %, trend graph, alarm LED).
- The **Block Diagram** contains the actual logic: a While Loop repeatedly reads a sample from DAQ, computes error, runs PID, writes to actuator, logs data, and updates indicators.
- The **Shift Register** is a LabVIEW feature for storing the previous error $e[k-1]$ needed by the derivative term.
- The **Icon/Connector Pane** allows this VI to be reused as a sub-VI inside larger programs.

---

## 4.3 Flow Diagram: Data Flow Execution Model in LabVIEW

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["VI Execution Starts"]) --> nodeA["Node A: DAQmx Read"]
    nodeA -->|"Data Available"| nodeB["Node B: Compute Error e setpoint minus measured"]
    nodeB -->|"Error Available"| nodeC["Node C: PID Algorithm"]
    nodeC -->|"Output Available"| nodeD["Node D: DAQmx Write to Actuator"]
    nodeC -->|"Output Available"| nodeE["Node E: Update Front Panel Indicators"]
    nodeE -->|"Display Updated"| nodeF["Node F: Wait Until Next Sample ms"]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF -->|"Loop Condition Check"| decision{"Stop Button Pressed"}
    decision -->|"No"| nodeA
    decision -->|"Yes"| stop(["VI Execution Ends"])

    note["NOTE: Nodes B and E cannot execute until A finishes. But if data were available in parallel branches, they would execute simultaneously — LabVIEW is inherently parallel."] -.-> nodeB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- This illustrates the **data flow paradigm**. A node only executes when all its inputs are available.
- Node A (DAQmx Read) produces data → Node B (subtraction) executes → Node C (PID) executes → Nodes D and E (write + update indicators) execute in **parallel** because both have data simultaneously.
- The Wait function enforces a constant sampling period (e.g., 1 second for thermal control).

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define Data Acquisition System (DAS). List its main functional blocks with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is the process of sampling real-world physical signals and converting them into a digital form for processing, analysis, and storage by a computer.

Main functional blocks:
1. **Sensor / Transducer** — Converts physical quantity (temperature, pressure, pH) into an electrical signal (mV, V, mA).
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplifies weak signals, filters noise, and linearizes the sensor output.
3. **DAQ Hardware (ADC)** — Converts the conditioned analog signal into a digital number.
4. **Driver Software (e.g., NI-DAQmx)** — Provides an interface between hardware and application software.
5. **Application Software (e.g., LabVIEW VI)** — Performs measurement, display, logging, and control.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**What is a Virtual Instrument (VI) in LabVIEW? Explain its three main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** is the fundamental program unit in LabVIEW that emulates the function of a physical instrument (oscilloscope, multimeter, controller) through software.

Three main components:
1. **Front Panel** — The interactive user interface containing controls (inputs like knobs, switches) and indicators (outputs like graphs, LEDs, numeric displays).
2. **Block Diagram** — The graphical source code containing nodes (functions, sub-VIs), terminals, and wires that define the data flow logic.
3. **Icon / Connector** — A graphical symbol (icon) and a connector-pane pattern that allow the VI to be plugged into other VIs as a sub-VI, analogous to a function call in textual languages.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students are given internal choice between Question A and Question B. Below, **both alternatives are provided with full model solutions.**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper aligned to 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a LabVIEW-based Data Acquisition and Control system for a milk pasteurization plant. Identify each layer and describe its function.

**(b)** A thermocouple-based temperature measurement system uses a 16-bit DAQ card with input range 0–5 V. The thermocouple output is amplified by a gain of 50× before reaching the ADC. Calculate:
   (i) The quantization step size (LSB voltage).
   (ii) The temperature resolution in °C, assuming the thermocouple sensitivity is 41 μV/°C.
   (iii) Whether this system is suitable for HTST pasteurization which requires ±0.5 °C accuracy.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

A LabVIEW-based DAQ and Control system for **HTST (High-Temperature Short-Time) pasteurization** of milk has the following layers:

1. **Physical Process Layer**: The pasteurizer vessel where milk is heated to 72 °C and held for 15 seconds.

2. **Sensor Layer**: A **Type-K thermocouple** immersed in the milk flow measures the temperature continuously.

3. **Signal Conditioning Layer**: A **thermocouple amplifier module** with cold-junction compensation and a gain stage amplifies the ~3 mV signal to a 0–5 V range suitable for the ADC.

4. **DAQ Hardware Layer**: A **16-bit NI DAQ card** (e.g., NI USB-6009 or similar) digitizes the amplified voltage into 65,536 discrete levels.

5. **Driver Layer**: **NI-DAQmx** software driver creates a task, configures timing (continuous sampling at 10 Hz), and exposes read/write functions to LabVIEW.

6. **LabVIEW Application Layer**: A **VI running on a PC** with:
   - Front panel showing live temperature trend, setpoint, and PID gains.
   - Block diagram implementing a PID controller in a While Loop with a 100 ms Wait function.
   - Logging all samples to a TDMS file for FSSAI compliance records.

7. **Actuator Layer**: The PID output drives a **current-to-pressure (I/P) converter** connected to a **pneumatic steam control valve** that regulates steam flow into the heat exchanger.

8. **Communication Layer**: The VI also publishes data over Ethernet to the plant **SCADA system** for supervisory monitoring.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.1 above.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**(i) Quantization Step Size (LSB):**

Given: $n = 16$, $V_{FSR} = 5 - 0 = 5$ V.

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{FSR}}{2^n} = \frac{5}{2^{16}} = \frac{5}{65{,}536}$$

$$\Delta V = 7.629 \times 10^{-5} \text{ V} = 76.29 \text{ μV}$$

**(ii) Temperature Resolution:**

The amplified thermocouple voltage per °C is:

$$S_{amp} = 41 \, \mu\text{V/°C} \times 50 = 2050 \, \mu\text{V/°C} = 2.05 \text{ mV/°C}$$

Temperature resolution is:

$$\Delta T = \frac{\Delta V}{S_{amp}} = \frac{76.29 \, \mu\text{V}}{2050 \, \mu\text{V/°C}}$$

$$\Delta T = 0.0372 \text{ °C per LSB}$$

**(iii) Suitability for HTST:**

The temperature resolution is ±0.037 °C, which is **well within** the ±0.5 °C accuracy required for HTST pasteurization. **Therefore, the system is suitable.** However, in practice, system accuracy is also affected by thermocouple calibration error, amplifier offset drift, and noise — so the actual achievable accuracy is typically ±0.1 °C to ±0.2 °C, still acceptable.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Define the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. A vibration sensor on a food conveyor outputs a signal with maximum frequency component of 250 Hz. Determine (i) the minimum sampling rate, and (ii) the practical sampling rate using a 4× safety factor.

**(b)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the PID control algorithm as implemented in a LabVIEW VI for pH control in a yogurt fermentation tank. Derive the discrete-time PID equation used by LabVIEW from the continuous-time form.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Definition of Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:**

> The Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem states that a band-limited continuous-time signal containing no frequency components higher than $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. The minimum sampling rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**, and $f_{max}$ is the **Nyquist frequency**.

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate:**

$$f_{s,min} = 2 \times f_{max} = 2 \times 250 = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Practical Sampling Rate (with 4× safety factor):**

$$f_{s,practical} = 4 \times f_{s,min} = 4 \times 500 = 2{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

This practical rate ensures that the anti-aliasing filter (typically a low-pass Butterworth with finite roll-off) has enough margin to attenuate frequencies above 250 Hz before they fold back into the baseband.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**PID Control for Yogurt Fermentation:**

In yogurt manufacturing, *Lactobacillus bulgaricus* and *Streptococcus thermophilus* convert lactose to lactic acid, lowering the pH. The target pH is **4.5** for proper gel formation. A pH probe (sensor) continuously monitors the pH, and a PID controller in LabVIEW adjusts the speed of a peristaltic pump dosing a base (or acid-correcting agent) to maintain the setpoint.

**Block Diagram Reference:** See SECTION 4.2 above (substitute "pH" for "Temperature" and "base pump" for "valve").

**Continuous-Time PID Equation:**

$$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = controller output (pump speed, 0–100%).
- $e(t) = pH_{setpoint} - pH_{measured}$.
- $K_p$ = proportional gain.
- $K_i = K_p / T_i$ = integral gain.
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ = derivative gain.

**Conversion to Discrete-Time Form for LabVIEW:**

LabVIEW samples at discrete intervals $\Delta t$. The integral is approximated by the **rectangular (Euler) rule**:

$$\int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\, d\tau \approx \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] \cdot \Delta t$$

The derivative is approximated by the **backward difference**:

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e[k] - e[k-1]}{\Delta t}$$

Substituting these into the continuous equation yields the **discrete PID equation** implemented in the LabVIEW block diagram:
