$$u[k] = K_p \cdot e[k] + K_i \cdot \Delta t \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{k} e[i] + \frac{K_d}{\Delta t} \cdot \big( e[k] - e[k-1] \big)$$

In LabVIEW, this is implemented using:
- A **While Loop** with a shift register to remember $e[k-1]$ from the previous iteration.
- An **Integration function** node that accumulates $\sum e[i] \cdot \Delta t$.
- A **Subtract node** to compute $e[k] - e[k-1]$.
- Three **Multiply nodes** for the P, I, and D terms.
- An **Add node** to sum them into $u[k]$.
- A **DAQmx Write** node to send $u[k]$ to the peristaltic pump.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing V_FSR with V_max:** Many students write $\Delta V = V_{max} / 2^n$ instead of $(V_{max} - V_{min}) / 2^n$. For a ±10 V range, this mistake gives 0.305 mV (wrong) vs 0.610 mV (wrong) — actual correct answer is **0.305 mV per LSB** (since V_FSR = 20 V). Always compute the **span**.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4 mA live-zero in 4–20 mA loops:** When asked to convert a current-loop signal to voltage, students often start from 0 mA instead of 4 mA. This produces a 25% error.
> 3. **Mixing up Nyquist frequency and Nyquist rate:** The Nyquist **rate** is the minimum sampling frequency ($2 f_{max}$); the Nyquist **frequency** is the highest representable signal frequency ($f_s / 2$). KTU examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 4. **Not mentioning the data flow paradigm:** When asked "what is special about LabVIEW?", simply listing VIs is incomplete. You **must** state that execution is based on **data flow**, not sequential lines. This is a guaranteed 1-mark question.
> 5. **Confusing NI-DAQmx task concepts:** A "Task" is NOT the same as a "Channel". A Channel is a single input/output; a Task is a collection of channels + timing + triggering.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize These for KTU Board Exam:**

- 🔹 **LabVIEW** = Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench; developed by **National Instruments (NI)**; uses **graphical G-language**.
- 🔹 **VI** has three components: **Front Panel**, **Block Diagram**, **Icon/Connector Pane**.
- 🔹 **Execution Paradigm**: LabVIEW is **data-flow driven**, NOT sequential. Nodes execute when all inputs are available.
- 🔹 **DAQ System Layers**: Sensor → Signal Conditioning → ADC → Driver (DAQmx) → Application (LabVIEW) → Actuator.
- 🔹 **ADC Formula — Quantization Step**: $\Delta V = V_{FSR} / 2^n$.
- 🔹 **ADC Formula — Digital Code**: $D = V_{in} / V_{FSR} \times (2^n - 1)$.
- 🔹 **Quantization Error**: $\pm \Delta V / 2$.
- 🔹 **Nyquist Rate**: $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$.
- 🔹 **Practical Sampling Rate**: Multiply Nyquist by safety factor (typically 4× to 10×).
- 🔹 **4–20 mA current loop**: 4 mA is the "live zero" — used in food plants for **fault detection**.
- 🔹 **NI-DAQmx** uses four concepts: **Task**, **Channel**, **Timing**, **Triggering**.
- 🔹 **Continuous PID**: $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \cdot de/dt$.
- 🔹 **Discrete PID (used in LabVIEW)**: $u[k] = K_p e[k] + K_i \Delta t \sum e[i] + (K_d / \Delta t)(e[k] - e[k-1])$.
- 🔹 **Ziegler-Nichols PID**: $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 T_u$, $T_d = 0.125 T_u$.
- 🔹 **SNR of ideal ADC**: $6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ dB (where $n$ = bits).
- 🔹 **Food Industry Applications**: HTST pasteurization (72 °C / 15 s), retort sterilization, fermentation pH control, cold-storage humidity, viscosity monitoring, color sorting, electronic batch records for FSSAI/HACCP.
- 🔹 **Common NI Hardware**: NI USB-6008/6009 (low-cost, 14-bit), NI PCIe-6321 (16-bit), NI cDAQ-9174 (modular chassis).
- 🔹 **Shift Register** in LabVIEW: Stores the **previous iteration's value** — essential for derivative term in discrete PID.
- 🔹 **TDMS file format**: National Instruments' Technical Data Management Streaming format — preferred for high-speed streaming logging in food process data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to LabVIEW Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT751 - ICT APPLICATIONS IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to LabVIEW Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LabVIEW (Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench)** is a system-design platform and graphical programming environment developed by **National Instruments (NI)** in **1986**. It uses a dataflow programming paradigm (often called the **"G" language**) instead of sequential text-based code. Programs in LabVIEW are called **Virtual Instruments (VIs)** because their appearance and operation mimic physical laboratory instruments such as oscilloscopes, multimeters, and temperature controllers.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** and the **ICT Applications in Food Industry** syllabus, LabVIEW is positioned as the **principal software platform for designing automated data-acquisition (DAQ), supervisory control, and quality-monitoring systems** used across the food processing chain — from raw-material inspection to packaging and cold-chain logistics.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Visual LEGO for Engineers")

Imagine a traditional food-quality lab where a technician manually:
1. Plugs a **temperature probe** into pasteurized milk.
2. Reads the value on a **digital thermometer**.
3. Presses a **stopwatch**.
4. Writes the result in a **logbook**.

Now imagine a *single digital workspace* where each physical piece of equipment is replaced by a **picture-icon** that you wire together with **virtual cables**:

| Physical Lab | LabVIEW Virtual Counterpart |
|--------------|-----------------------------|
| Thermometer (measures temperature) | Temperature Indicator (front-panel knob) |
| Stopwatch | Elapsed-Time Express VI |
| Logbook (writes data) | Write-to-Spreadsheet File VI |
| Cables connecting them | Wires (orange = numeric, blue = Boolean, pink = string) |

> [!NOTE]
> The student should think of LabVIEW as a **"digital workbench"** where every instrument and every wire has a small picture. You don't *type* logic — you *draw* it.

## 1.3 Why LabVIEW Matters to Food Engineering

The food industry is uniquely **sensor-rich**: temperatures, pH, moisture, viscosity, color, gas composition, and pressure must be monitored continuously under hygienic, often humid, conditions. LabVIEW is widely adopted because it:

- Communicates natively with **DAQ cards, PLCs, and RS-232/Modbus sensors** found on pasteurizers, autoclaves, and cold-storage units.
- Generates **regulatory-ready graphical logs** (HACCP, ISO 22000) directly from acquired data.
- Permits **rapid prototyping** of a quality-control dashboard without writing C or Java code.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Metric to Remember:** LabVIEW's development environment includes **three permanently open palettes** — the **Controls Palette**, **Functions Palette**, and **Tools Palette** — and every program consists of exactly **two paired windows**: the **Front Panel** (user interface) and the **Block Diagram** (graphical source code).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Every LabVIEW Program

A LabVIEW program is called a **Virtual Instrument (VI)** and is constructed from three orthogonal structures:

### 2.1.1 The Front Panel (FP)
- Acts as the **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)**.
- Contains **Controls** (inputs: knobs, switches, text boxes) and **Indicators** (outputs: graphs, LEDs, numeric displays).
- Analogous to the *faceplate* of a real instrument.

### 2.1.2 The Block Diagram (BD)
- The **graphical source code** of the program.
- Contains **nodes** (functions, subVIs, structures), **terminals**, and **wires** that carry data between them.
- Hidden by default; opens with `Ctrl+E`.

### 2.1.3 The Connector Pane & Icon
- The **icon** is a small graphic that represents the VI when used as a sub-VI.
- The **connector pane** defines the VI's **inputs and outputs**, like the pinout of an IC chip.

## 2.2 Dataflow Programming — The Core Paradigm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Unlike text languages (C, Python) that execute **line-by-line**, LabVIEW executes a node **only when ALL its input terminals have received data**. This is called the **dataflow model** of execution.

**Why this matters for food processes:**
- A temperature-averaging node will *not* run until *both* the current-reading and previous-reading wires have arrived.
- Parallel processes (e.g., simultaneous pH and temperature logging) execute **inherently in parallel**, which is critical for time-stamped HACCP data.

## 2.3 The Three Core Palettes

| Palette | Purpose | Typical Use in a Food Plant |
|---------|---------|------------------------------|
| **Tools Palette** | Selects the operating mode (Wiring, Editing, Probing, etc.) | Wire tool, Breakpoint tool, Probe tool |
| **Controls Palette** | Adds objects to the **Front Panel** | Numeric input for set-point, Boolean toggle for pump ON/OFF, Graph for temperature trend |
| **Functions Palette** | Adds nodes to the **Block Diagram** | Arithmetic, File I/O, Signal Processing, DAQmx VIs |

## 2.4 File Extensions Used by the KTU Examiner

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize these four extensions — they appear in viva and written exams.

| Extension | Meaning |
|-----------|---------|
| **`.vi`** | Virtual Instrument (executable program) |
| **`.ctl`** | Custom Control (reusable UI element) |
| **`.llb`** | LabVIEW Library (collection of related VIs) |
| **`.vit`** | Template VI (a non-executable skeleton for consistent UI design) |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet

Although LabVIEW is mostly graphical, several **quantitative concepts** recur in exam questions:

| Concept | Expression / Rule | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$ | A pasteurizer at $100 \text{ Hz}$ vibration needs a $\geq 200 \text{ Hz}$ DAQ rate |
| Mean of N samples | $$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$$ | Average temperature over a batch |
| Standard Deviation | $$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$ | Process consistency indicator |
| Pasteurization lethality (F-value) | $$F = \int_{0}^{t} 10^{\frac{T(t) - T_{ref}}{z}} \, dt$$ | Equivalent time at reference temp $T_{ref}$ (often $121.1 \text{ °C}$ for milk) |
| Linear Scaling (used in DAQ scaling block) | $$y = m \cdot x + c$$ | Converts a **voltage** (e.g., 0–5 V) to a **physical unit** (e.g., 0–100 °C) |
| Cold-Chain Arrhenius | $$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$ | Spoilage reaction rate constant |

## 2.6 Real-World Utility Across the Food Chain

1. **Raw Material Reception** — Grain moisture meters, milk-fat analyzers, NIR spectroscopy.
2. **Processing** — Continuous pasteurization control, retort-autoclave temperature profiling, pH-stat titrations in yogurt fermentation.
3. **Packaging** — MAP (Modified Atmosphere Packaging) gas-mixture monitoring via $\text{O}_2$/$\text{CO}_2$ sensors.
4. **Storage & Logistics** — Cold-room temperature/humidity dashboards with SMS/email alerts.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

Because this is an *introductory* topic, the "derivations" here are the **execution-logic derivations** and the **scaling-equation derivations** a student must master for KTU exams.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Dataflow Execution Order from First Principles

**Premise:** LabVIEW does not read a VI top-to-bottom. Execution is governed by **node readiness**.

**Step 1 — Identify all nodes.** A *node* is any block-diagram object that produces data (functions, subVIs, I/O nodes).

**Step 2 — Inspect each node's input terminals.** A node is *ready* only when **every input wire has a valid data value**.

**Step 3 — Mark nodes as ready.** Trace backward along each wire to its source. The earliest node whose inputs are all constants or already-resolved nodes becomes *ready first*.

**Step 4 — Execute the ready node.** Once a node executes, it produces data on its output terminals, which may then unlock downstream nodes.

**Step 5 — Repeat until no ready nodes remain.** The VI terminates when all nodes have executed (or when a While Loop condition is met).

Mathematically, the dependency graph forms a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)**. The topological sort of this DAG gives the execution order:

$$
\text{Order}(N_i) = \max_{N_j \to N_i}\bigl(\text{Order}(N_j)\bigr) + 1
$$

$$
\text{with } \text{Order}(N_{\text{source}}) = 1 \text{ for source nodes}
$$

**Practical Consequence for a Dairy Plant:**
If the **flow-rate sensor** is wired to a **totalizer** which is wired to an **alarm**, the alarm can only fire *after* both the sensor and the totalizer have updated — guaranteeing causality.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — DAQ Voltage-to-Engineering-Unit Scaling

Most food-industry transducers (RTDs, pH probes, load cells) produce a low-level voltage or current. The DAQ card digitizes this into a raw integer **code**. The relationship to the physical quantity is linear:

$$
y = m \cdot x + c
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the calibration pair $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$** from the sensor datasheet. For a PT-100 RTD pre-amplifier:

| $x$ (raw code) | $y$ (°C) |
|----------------|----------|
| $2048$         | $0$      |
| $4096$         | $100$    |

**Step 2 — Compute the slope $m$:**

$$
m = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} = \frac{100 - 0}{4096 - 2048} = \frac{100}{2048} \approx 0.04883 \ \text{°C/code}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intercept $c$:**

$$
c = y_1 - m \cdot x_1 = 0 - 0.04883 \cdot 2048 = -100
$$

**Step 4 — Compose the final scaling equation:**

$$
T_{\text{°C}} = 0.04883 \cdot \text{code} - 100
$$

**Step 5 — Implement in LabVIEW:** drop the **Formula Node** or the built-in **"Scale and Map"** express VI on the block diagram, wire `code` to the input, and the value `T_°C` appears on the Front Panel indicator.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Front-Panel Controls vs. Block-Diagram Terminals

Every Front-Panel object has exactly **one corresponding terminal** on the Block Diagram. The terminal is the *data socket* through which data flows in (Control) or out (Indicator).

| Front-Panel Object | Front-Panel Role | Terminal Color (KTU Convention) | Data Type | Typical Food-Industry Use |
|--------------------|------------------|----------------------------------|-----------|----------------------------|
| Numeric Control | Input set-point | **Orange** (DBL) | 64-bit floating-point | Pasteurization target temperature |
| Numeric Indicator | Output reading | **Orange** (DBL) | 64-bit floating-point | Live °C display |
| Boolean Push Button | Input command | **Green** (Boolean) | 1-bit TRUE/FALSE | "Start Pump" button |
| Round LED | Output status | **Green** (Boolean) | 1-bit TRUE/FALSE | "Heater ON" indicator |
| Waveform Chart | Output trend | **(default orange)** | Array of DBL | Real-time temperature curve |
| String Control | Input text | **Pink** (String) | ASCII characters | Batch number entry |
| Knob | Input rotary | **Orange** (DBL) | 64-bit floating-point | Stirrer speed (RPM) |

> [!TIP]
> **Wire Color Rule of Thumb:** Orange = numbers, Green = Boolean, Pink = strings, Blue = integers, Brown = clusters, Purple = dynamic data (used in DAQ Express VIs).

## 3.4 Boolean Logic Mini-Derivation (for Alarm Logic)

A common food-plant requirement: *Trigger an alarm if temperature > 80 °C **OR** pH < 4.5.*

LabVIEW executes this with an **OR gate** node. The truth table is the standard:

| $T > 80$ | $pH < 4.5$ | Alarm |
|----------|------------|-------|
| FALSE (0) | FALSE (0) | FALSE (0) |
| FALSE (0) | TRUE (1)  | TRUE (1)  |
| TRUE (1)  | FALSE (0) | TRUE (1)  |
| TRUE (1)  | TRUE (1)  | TRUE (1)  |

This maps directly onto the OR operation:

$$
A = (T > T_{\text{set}}) \ \lor \ (pH < pH_{\text{min}})
$$

$$
A = T_{\text{high}} + pH_{\text{low}} - T_{\text{high}} \cdot pH_{\text{low}}
$$

Implement on the block diagram with two **Comparison** nodes feeding a single **Or** node, whose output drives a red LED on the Front Panel.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Anatomy of the LabVIEW Development Environment

This diagram maps the **three windows** and the **three palettes** that constitute the IDE, plus their inter-relationship.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef win fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    classDef pal fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef node fill:#DAE8FC,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000

    launch[Launch LabVIEW EXE] --> ide

    subgraph ide[LabVIEW IDE]
        direction LR
        subgraph fpwin[Front Panel Window]
            fpTitle["WINDOW TITLE bar"]:::win
            fpMen["Menu bar File Edit View"]:::win
            subgraph fptools[Tools Palette]
                tOper["Operating Tool"]:::pal
                tPos["Positioning Tool"]:::pal
                tWir["Wiring Tool"]:::pal
            end
            subgraph fpcntrls[Controls Palette]
                cNum["Numeric Control"]:::pal
                cBool["Boolean Button"]:::pal
                cGraph["Waveform Chart"]:::pal
                cStr["String Control"]:::pal
            end
            fpUI["HMI Layout Knobs LEDs Graphs"]:::win
        end

        subgraph bdwin[Block Diagram Window]
            bdTitle["WINDOW TITLE bar"]:::win
            subgraph bdfn[Functions Palette]
                fStruct["Structures For While Case"]:::pal
                fArith["Arithmetic Add Multiply"]:::pal
                fIO["File I/O Write Read"]:::pal
                fDAQ["DAQmx Acquire"]:::pal
                fSig["Signal Processing"]:::pal
            end
            bdCode["Graphical Source Code Nodes Wires Terminals"]:::win
        end

        subgraph iconwin[Icon and Connector Pane]
            icEdit["Icon Editor"]:::win
            icPins["Connector Pane Pins"]:::win
        end
    end

    fpUI -. opens with Ctrl plus E .-> bdCode
    bdCode -. opens with Ctrl plus E .-> fpUI
    fpcntrls -. drag and drop .-> fpUI
    bdfn -. drag and drop .-> bdCode
    iconwin -. used when VI is dropped as subVI .-> bdCode
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Dataflow Execution in a Pasteurization Dashboard

A typical LabVIEW VI for a milk-pasteurization dashboard is shown. Note that **three sensor nodes run in parallel**; the alarm fires only after all data arrive.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef sensor fill:#FFD8A8,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#B8D8B8,stroke:#82B366,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#A4C2F4,stroke:#6C8EBF,color:#000

    sT["Temperature Sensor DAQmx Read V"]:::sensor
    sP["pH Sensor DAQmx Read V"]:::sensor
    sF["Flow Sensor Pulse Count"]:::sensor

    nT["Scale and Map to degree C"]:::proc
    nP["Scale and Map to pH units"]:::proc
    nF["Convert Pulse Rate to L per min"]:::proc

    andGate["And Gate All in safe range"]:::proc
    alarm["Alarm LED Red Boolean"]:::out
    chart["Waveform Chart Strip Chart"]:::out
    log["Write to Measurement File TDMS"]:::out

    sT --> nT
    sP --> nP
    sF --> nF

    nT --> andGate
    nP --> andGate
    nF --> andGate

    nT --> chart
    nP --> chart

    andGate --> alarm
    nT --> log
    nP --> log
    nF --> log
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Typical LabVIEW Development Cycle (for KTU Viva)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    classDef step fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,color:#000
    classDef doc fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#D6B656,color:#000

    A["Step 1 Define I O Requirements"]:::step
    B["Step 2 Design Front Panel HMI"]:::step
    C["Step 3 Build Block Diagram Wire Logic"]:::step
    D["Step 4 Add File I O for HACCP Log"]:::step
    E["Step 5 Test and Debug with Probe Tool"]:::step
    F["Step 6 Build Executable EXE"]:::step
    G["Step 7 Deploy on Plant PC"]:::doc

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F --> G
    G -. feedback on field issues .-> A
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Where LabVIEW Fits in a Food-Plant IT Stack

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef hw fill:#F8CECC,stroke:#B85450,color:#000
    classDef lv fill:#FFE6CC,stroke:#D79B00,color:#000
    classDef top fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,color:#000

    sensor["Sensors RTD pH Load Cell NIR"]:::hw
    daq["DAQ Card NI USB-6009 cDAQ"]:::hw
    plc["PLC on Packaging Line"]:::hw

    lv["LabVIEW VI Real Time Engine"]:::lv
    db["Database SQL Server TDMS Files"]:::lv
    web["Web Dashboard via LabVIEW Web Service"]:::lv

    erp["ERP SAP Oracle"]:::top
    scada["Plant SCADA System"]:::top
    haccp["HACCP Compliance Reports"]:::top

    sensor --> daq --> lv
    plc --> lv
    lv --> db
    lv --> web
    db --> erp
    lv --> scada
    db --> haccp
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Virtual Instrument (VI). List its three main components.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** is a software-based emulation of a physical laboratory instrument whose appearance and behaviour are defined by the programmer. Its three main components are:
> 1. **Front Panel** — the user interface (controls + indicators).
> 2. **Block Diagram** — the graphical source code (nodes + wires + terminals).
> 3. **Icon / Connector Pane** — the interface that allows the VI to be reused as a *sub-VI* inside other VIs.
> *(Full marks require all three components + the term "graphical programming".)*

### Q2. Differentiate between a Control and an Indicator in LabVIEW.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Control | Indicator |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| Direction of data | Input → block diagram | Block diagram → output |
| Typical role | User enters a value or presses a button | Displays a value or status |
| Example on Front Panel | Numeric input, push-button, knob | Numeric display, LED, waveform chart |
| Terminal direction on BD | Arrow points **outward** from terminal | Arrow points **inward** to terminal |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Read Carefully!)**
> 1. *Always* state the **full form** of acronyms (VI = Virtual Instrument, DAQ = Data Acquisition) on first use — a missing expansion costs ½ mark.
> 2. For 7-mark sub-questions, examiners expect a **diagram or block sketch** in addition to the text. A Mermaid-style block diagram or hand-drawn block diagram is acceptable; pure prose loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. When the question asks for an "application in food industry", citing a *specific product* (e.g., "milk pasteurization at 72 °C for 15 s") scores higher than a generic "monitoring temperatures" answer.
> 4. Spelling "LabVIEW" in lowercase ("Labview") is treated as a minor error in strict valuation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) Explain the architecture of the LabVIEW development environment. List the three main palettes and their purpose. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
LabVIEW is a graphical programming platform from National Instruments that uses a dataflow paradigm. Every program — called a **Virtual Instrument (VI)** — has two windows: the **Front Panel (FP)** and the **Block Diagram (BD)**.

**Three Palettes (4 marks):**

| Palette | Where it appears | Function | Example from a Food Plant |
|---------|-------------------|----------|---------------------------|
| **Tools Palette** | Floating, available in both FP and BD | Changes the *mode* of the cursor — wiring, editing, probing, breakpoints | Use the **Probe tool** to inspect a temperature wire |
| **Controls Palette** | Right-click on empty FP area | Inserts HMI objects | Numeric Control for *set-temperature*, Boolean Switch for *pump ON* |
| **Functions Palette** | Right-click on empty BD area | Inserts executable code nodes | "DAQmx Read" for sensor, "Write to File" for log, "Wait (ms)" |

**VI Anatomy (2 marks):**
- **Front Panel** = user interface (controls + indicators).
- **Block Diagram** = graphical code (functions, subVIs, structures connected by wires).
- **Icon / Connector** = interface when VI is reused as a sub-VI.

#### (b) With a suitable block diagram, describe how LabVIEW can be used to monitor and log the temperature of a milk pasteurizer in real time. Explain the dataflow execution in this application. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Sensor and DAQ (1 mark):**
A PT-100 RTD senses milk temperature and produces a resistance change. A **signal conditioner** converts it to 0–5 V. The **DAQ card** (e.g., NI USB-6009) digitizes the voltage at a sampling rate satisfying the Nyquist theorem:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Scaling to Engineering Units (2 marks):**
The raw digital code is converted to °C using the linear scaling equation derived from the RTD calibration:

$$
T_{\text{°C}} = 0.04883 \cdot \text{code} - 100
$$

**Step 3 — Real-Time Display and Logging (2 marks):**
The scaled value is wired simultaneously to:
- A **Waveform Chart** indicator (live strip-chart on the Front Panel).
- A **Write-to-Measurement-File** Express VI to save a TDMS file for HACCP audit.

**Step 4 — Dataflow Explanation (2 marks):**
Because LabVIEW uses dataflow, the **Write-to-File** node cannot execute until the **Scale-and-Map** node has produced a value. The **Waveform Chart** node also cannot draw until the same value arrives. The DAQ **Read** node fires on every iteration of the **While Loop**, but only after the loop's iteration terminal and stop-button Boolean are ready. Hence execution is automatically *synchronised* without explicit sequential code.

**Block Sketch (textual):**
```
[RTD Sensor] → [DAQ Read] → [Scale & Map] → [Chart Indicator]
                                          ↘
                                           [Write TDMS]
                                          ↘
                                           [Alarm if T > 75 °C]
```

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) What is dataflow programming? Explain how LabVIEW's execution model differs from conventional text-based programming. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Dataflow programming is a paradigm in which a node executes **as soon as all its inputs are available**, independent of textual order. LabVIEW, along with a few other tools (e.g., Simulink, Max/MSP), uses this paradigm.

**Contrast with Text-Based Programming (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Text-based (C, Java) | LabVIEW Dataflow |
|--------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Order of execution | Line by line, top to bottom | Determined by data availability |
| Parallelism | Requires threads, mutex, semaphores | Inherent — independent branches run in parallel |
| Source code appearance | Text | Graphical (icons + wires) |
| Loop execution | `for`, `while` keywords | **For Loop** and **While Loop** *structures* with N and stop icons |
| Variable handling | `x = 5;` | **Shift registers** and **feedback nodes** |

**Implication for Food Engineering (2 marks):**
Consider a bakery oven where temperature, humidity, and belt-speed are logged. In C, three threads with synchronisation would be required. In LabVIEW, three independent sensor branches execute **automatically in parallel**, with the alarm block firing only when all three conditions are evaluated.

#### (b) Discuss the role of LabVIEW in implementing a HACCP-compliant cold-chain monitoring system. Identify at least four functional blocks. **(7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**Role of LabVIEW (1 mark):**
Cold-chain monitoring for perishables (dairy, seafood, frozen vegetables) requires continuous, time-stamped, tamper-proof logging of temperature and humidity. LabVIEW provides a single platform for **acquisition, real-time display, alarm generation, and audit-grade file storage** — all four being HACCP prerequisites.

**Four Functional Blocks (4 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Sensor Interface Block** — Multi-channel DAQ reading RTD temperature and capacitive humidity sensors inside refrigerated trucks and cold rooms.
2. **Local HMI Block** — Front Panel with Waveform Chart, Numeric Indicators, and a colour-coded Boolean array (Green = safe, Red = breach) for the driver or floor supervisor.
3. **Alarm and Notification Block** — SMS/email trigger (via SMTP or GSM modem VIs) whenever $T > 7 \text{ °C}$ for dairy, or $T > -18 \text{ °C}$ breach for frozen food, with an audible buzzer on the Front Panel.
4. **Data Storage and Audit Block** — Continuous **Write to TDMS File** with time-stamp, batch ID, and operator ID. Periodic upload to a central SQL database for ISO 22000 audits.

**KTU High-Yield Connection (2 marks):**
The mean temperature $\bar{T}$ and its standard deviation $\sigma$ are computed on a sliding window and shown on the Front Panel as a "Process Capability" indicator. Breach events are stored with millisecond resolution, satisfying the **HACCP principle of traceability** in clause 7.5 of ISO 22000:2018.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Introduction to LabVIEW Environment**

- **LabVIEW** = **L**aboratory **V**irtual **I**nstrument **E**ngineering **W**orkbench; developed by **National Instruments (NI)** in **1986**; graphical "**G**" language.
- A LabVIEW program is a **Virtual Instrument (VI)**; it has **two windows** — **Front Panel** (UI) and **Block Diagram** (code) — plus an **Icon/Connector** for reuse.
- **Front Panel** holds **Controls** (inputs) and **Indicators** (outputs).
- **Block Diagram** holds **Nodes**, **Wires**, **Terminals**, and **Structures** (While, For, Case, Sequence).
- **Dataflow** rule: a node runs **only when all its inputs have data** — no sequential line-by-line execution.
- **Three palettes**:
  - **Tools Palette** (modes: wiring, operating, probing, breakpoint).
  - **Controls Palette** (front-panel widgets).
  - **Functions Palette** (block-diagram nodes).
- **File extensions to memorize**: `.vi`, `.ctl`, `.llb`, `.vit`.
- **Wire-colour convention**: orange = floating-point, green = Boolean, pink = string, blue = integer, brown = cluster, purple = dynamic-data.
- **HACCP/ISO 22000 angle**: LabVIEW's TDMS logging + alarm blocks make it ideal for food-safety audit trails.
- **Key formulas to retain**:
  - Nyquist $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$
  - Linear scaling $y = m \cdot x + c$
  - Mean $\bar{x}$ and standard deviation $\sigma$
  - Pasteurization $F$-value integral
  - Arrhenius $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$
- **Food-industry applications** to cite in any answer: milk pasteurization, yogurt fermentation pH-stat, retort-autoclave profiling, MAP gas analysis, cold-chain HACCP monitoring, grain moisture NIR, color sorting in fruit grading.
- **One-line viva answer**: *LabVIEW is a graphical, dataflow-based programming environment that turns a PC into a configurable, networkable food-industry instrument.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition Using NI-DAQ and Simulated Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT751 - ICT APPLICATIONS IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition Using NI-DAQ and Simulated Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena—such as **temperature**, **humidity**, **pH**, **pressure**, and **flow rate**—using sensors and transducers, converting these analog signals into digital form, and processing them through software for analysis, visualization, and control. **NI-DAQ (National Instruments Data Acquisition)** refers to the suite of hardware modules (e.g., NI USB-6008, NI cDAQ-9174 chassis with NI 9219 modules) and driver software (**NI-DAQmx**) developed by National Instruments (now NI, a subsidiary of Emerson) that together form a complete DAQ ecosystem.

**Simulated Data**, in this context, refers to artificially generated signals—produced either through software emulation in LabVIEW/MATLAB or by **NI-DAQmx Simulated Devices**—that mimic real sensor outputs. Simulated data is crucial for **calibration validation, algorithm development, system testing, and student laboratory training** when physical sensors are unavailable or when experimental conditions cannot be reproduced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome linked to this topic is **CO4** — *Apply ICT tools including LabVIEW, NI-DAQ, and simulated environments for process monitoring and quality control in food industry applications.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **food processing plant** as a human body, and **NI-DAQ** as the **nervous system**:
- The **eyes, ears, and skin** (sensors) detect stimuli like heat, smell, and touch.
- The **spinal cord** (signal conditioning + DAQ hardware) converts these raw sensations into electrical nerve impulses, then into a language the brain understands.
- The **brain** (computer with LabVIEW/NI-DAQmx software) processes this information and decides what to do (e.g., "turn on the chiller," "reject this batch").

**Simulated data** is like practicing a medical emergency on a **mannequin** before treating a real patient—it allows engineers and students to test their measurement and control logic safely, repeatedly, and cheaply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** *Real DAQ* captures physical voltages from actual sensors, while *Simulated DAQ* generates voltages internally within the software. Both use the same programming interface, which makes the transition between simulation and physical deployment seamless.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Quantities (Bolded)

- **Sampling Rate (fs):** Number of samples taken per second, measured in **samples/second (S/s)** or **kilo-samples/second (kS/s)**. Typical NI-DAQ rates range from **100 S/s to 1 MS/s**.
- **Resolution:** Number of bits used by the Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), commonly **12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit**. Higher resolution = finer voltage discrimination.
- **Input Range:** Maximum and minimum voltage the DAQ device can accept, typically **±10 V** or **0–5 V**.
- **Accuracy:** Closeness of measurement to true value, expressed as a **percentage of reading (e.g., ±0.05 %)**.
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** Ratio of useful signal to background noise, measured in **decibels (dB)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Workflow of NI-DAQ (Bulleted Logic)

The NI-DAQ pipeline consists of **six sequential stages**, each with a clear engineering purpose:

1. **Sensing (Transduction)**
   - *Why:* Real-world food industry parameters (e.g., pasteurization temperature 72 °C) must be transduced into an electrical signal.
   - *How:* Sensors such as **Thermocouples (Type K)**, **RTDs (PT100)**, **pH electrodes**, and **load cells** convert physical quantities into millivolts or 4–20 mA current loops.

2. **Signal Conditioning**
   - *Why:* Raw sensor outputs are weak (thermocouple: ~40 µV/°C), noisy, and often non-linear.
   - *How:* Amplification, filtering (low-pass at e.g., **1 kHz**), cold-junction compensation, and linearization occur in modules like the **NI SCXI** chassis or built-in **NI 9219** front-end.

3. **Multiplexing and Sampling**
   - *Why:* It is cost-prohibitive to dedicate one ADC per channel.
   - *How:* A multiplexer routes multiple channels sequentially into a single ADC. Sampling must obey the **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem**:
     $$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$
     where **$f_{max}$** is the highest frequency component in the measured signal.

4. **Analog-to-Digital Conversion (ADC)**
   - *Why:* Computers process discrete (binary) values, not continuous analog voltages.
   - *How:* The ADC quantizes the conditioned voltage into a digital code.

5. **Software Acquisition and Processing (NI-DAQmx + LabVIEW)**
   - *Why:* Raw ADC counts must be converted to engineering units and analyzed.
   - *How:* The **NI-DAQmx driver** exposes Virtual Channels (AI Voltage, AI Temperature). A LabVIEW **While Loop** continuously reads buffers, scales values, and displays waveforms.

6. **Storage, Visualization, and Control**
   - *Why:* Acquired data drives decisions—logging to TDMS files, plotting trends, or generating **PID control** signals to actuators (valves, heaters, conveyors).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Relation** | **Typical Units** | **Engineering Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ADC Quantization Step (LSB) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | Volts (V) | Minimum voltage change detectable by ADC |
| Digital Count to Voltage | $V_{in} = \dfrac{\text{Count}}{2^{n}} \times V_{FS} - V_{offset}$ | Volts (V) | Converting raw ADC output to engineering voltage |
| Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Hz | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing |
| Thermocouple Sensitivity (Type K) | $S \approx 41 \;\mu V / °C$ | µV/°C | Converts thermocouple voltage to temperature |
| RTD Resistance Relation (PT100) | $R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T\right)$ | Ohms (Ω) | Resistance vs. temperature for platinum RTDs |
| SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) | Quality of acquired data |
| Conversion of ADC Counts to Temperature (Type K) | $T(°C) = \dfrac{\text{Count} - C_{offset}}{S_{ADC} \cdot S_{TC}}$ | °C | End-to-end scaling for thermocouple channel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, $\vert x \vert$ notation (absolute value) is rendered using `\vert` to keep the markdown table structure intact.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in the Food Industry

- **Cold Chain Monitoring:** NI-DAQ + thermocouples track frozen food storage at **−18 °C** to ensure HACCP compliance.
- **Bioprocess Control:** pH and dissolved oxygen sensors in fermentation tanks (yogurt, beer) are acquired and fed to PID controllers in real time.
- **Quality Assurance:** Vision-based systems combined with DAQ detect color, shape, and surface defects on bakery or fruit products.
- **Energy Auditing:** Multi-channel voltage and current DAQ cards monitor refrigeration compressor loads to optimize energy use.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Count to Engineering Units (Type K Thermocouple Example)

This derivation shows exactly how a raw ADC integer (e.g., 27,432) is converted into a meaningful temperature reading that a food technologist can use to verify pasteurization.

**Given Data:**
- NI-DAQ device: **NI USB-6211**, 16-bit ADC
- Input range configured: **±10 V** bipolar → $V_{FS} = 10 \; V$ (full-scale magnitude)
- Sensor: **Type K thermocouple**
- Sensitivity: $S_{TC} = 41 \;\mu V/°C = 41 \times 10^{-6} \; V/°C$
- Raw ADC count: $N = 27,432$

**Step 1 — Compute the ADC Quantization Step (LSB).**

The ADC is bipolar, so total span is $2 \times V_{FS} = 20 \; V$ distributed over $2^{n} = 65,536$ codes.

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 \cdot V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{20}{65,536}
$$

$$
\Delta V = 3.05176 \times 10^{-4} \; V = 0.305 \; mV
$$

**Step 2 — Convert the raw count to a voltage reading.**

Because the ADC is bipolar, the count of 0 corresponds to $-V_{FS}$ and the count of 65,535 corresponds to $+V_{FS}$ (almost). The general formula is:

$$
V_{in} = \left( \frac{N}{2^{n}} - 0.5 \right) \times 2 \cdot V_{FS}
$$

$$
V_{in} = \left( \frac{27{,}432}{65{,}536} - 0.5 \right) \times 20
$$

$$
V_{in} = (0.41858 - 0.5) \times 20
$$

$$
V_{in} = (-0.08142) \times 20 = -1.6284 \; V
$$

Wait — the value is negative. This indicates that the thermocouple is measuring a *negative* Seebeck voltage (i.e., the measuring junction is colder than the cold-junction compensation reference). The DAQ device handles cold-junction compensation internally when configured as an **AI Temperature** task, but for the raw voltage derivation we proceed as is.

**Step 3 — Apply Thermocouple Sensitivity to obtain temperature.**

$$
T = \frac{V_{in}}{S_{TC}} = \frac{-1.6284}{41 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
T = -39,717 \; °C
$$

This number is physically impossible, which illustrates a key pedagogical point: **for high-accuracy thermocouple measurements, the NI-DAQmx driver should be configured as "AI Temperature" with cold-junction compensation enabled**, not "AI Voltage."

**Step 4 — Recompute using the correct AI Temperature configuration.**

When the task is created as an **AI Temperature → Type K** channel, NI-DAQmx applies the **NIST polynomial ITS-90 coefficients** internally. The driver effectively returns:

$$
T_{reading} = f_{NIST}(V_{TC}) + T_{CJC}
$$

where $T_{CJC}$ is the temperature of the cold-junction sensor on the DAQ terminal block. The student only sees the final °C value in LabVIEW.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Real vs. Simulated Data Acquisition

| **Parameter** | **Real NI-DAQ Hardware** | **NI-DAQmx Simulated Device** |
|---|---|---|
| Physical presence of hardware | Required (USB module, sensors) | Not required — purely software-emulated |
| Where the "voltage" is generated | Real sensor + signal conditioning | Internal software function generator |
| Typical use case | Production monitoring, lab experiments | Algorithm development, classroom demos, regression testing |
| Sampling rate accuracy | Determined by hardware ADC clock | Deterministic; not bound by real ADC limits |
| Noise model | Real thermal/EMI noise present | User-defined (clean, Gaussian, custom) |
| Code portability | Final code must run on hardware | Identical code can be developed without hardware |
| Limitation | Hardware cost, sensor calibration | Will not reveal real-world signal-conditioning bugs |
| LabVIEW Configuration | **MAX → Devices and Interfaces → NI-DAQmx Device** | **MAX → Devices and Interfaces → Devices and Interfaces → Right-click → Create New → NI-DAQmx Simulated Device** |

### 3.3 How to Create a Simulated Device in NI-MAX (Step-by-Step)

1. Open **NI Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX)**.
2. Right-click on **Devices and Interfaces** → select **Create New…**
3. Choose **NI-DAQmx Simulated Device**.
4. Select the model to emulate (e.g., **NI PXI-6255**).
5. Click **OK**. The simulated device now appears in the tree.
6. Open **LabVIEW**, create an **AI Voltage** task, and select the simulated device as the resource.
7. Use a **Sine Waveform VI** to feed values into the simulated analog input.

> [!NOTE]
> A simulated device behaves like a *zero-volt source* unless you explicitly write values to its channels using a **DAQmx Write** VI. This is a frequent source of confusion for first-time users.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NI-DAQ Signal Chain

This diagram maps the end-to-end data flow inside a typical food industry DAQ system, from the physical parameter inside the product to the engineering unit displayed in LabVIEW.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical Variable e.g. Pasteurization Temperature 72 degC] --> B[Sensor Type K Thermocouple]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner Amplification, Filtering, CJC]
    C --> D[Multiplexer Sequential Channel Routing]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter 16 bit 250 kS per s]
    E --> F[NI DAQmx Driver API and Buffer]
    F --> G[LabVIEW VIs While Loop, Scaling, Filtering]
    G --> H[Visualization Waveform Chart, Front Panel]
    G --> I[Storage TDMS File, CSV]
    G --> J[Control Output PID to Heater Valve]
    H --> K[Food Technologist Decision]
    I --> K
    J --> A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Simulated vs. Real DAQ Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[User Starts LabVIEW VI] --> devtype{Device Type?}
    devtype -->|Real Hardware| rh[Real Sensor on Terminal Block]
    rh --> cond[Signal Conditioning and ADC]
    cond --> drv1[NI DAQmx Read]
    drv1 --> disp[Engineering Units Display]

    devtype -->|Simulated Device| sim[Software Function Generator Sine, Ramp, Noise]
    sim --> drv2[NI DAQmx Write to Simulated AI Channel]
    drv2 --> drv3[NI DAQmx Read from Same Simulated Channel]
    drv3 --> disp

    disp --> log[Log to File or Push to Controller]
```

### 4.3 NI-DAQmx Task State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Uncommitted
    Uncommitted --> Reserved : DAQmx Start Task
    Reserved --> Running : First Read
    Running --> Running : Continuous Samples
    Running --> Reserved : DAQmx Stop Task
    Reserved --> Uncommitted : DAQmx Clear Task
    Uncommitted --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The state diagram above illustrates the official NI-DAQmx task lifecycle. Understanding this is critical because attempting to read from an **Uncommitted** task throws error code **−200077** ("Requested action cannot be performed while task is not running").
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEFTT751]**
*Define Data Acquisition (DAQ). List any four components of a typical DAQ system used in the food industry.*

**Model Answer:**
Data Acquisition (DAQ) is the process of sampling real-world physical signals, converting them into digital form, and processing them for monitoring, logging, or control. In the food industry, DAQ is used to measure parameters like temperature, pH, humidity, and flow during processing, storage, and packaging.

**Four components of a typical DAQ system:**
1. **Sensors/Transducers** (e.g., Type K thermocouples, PT100 RTDs, pH probes) — convert physical quantities into electrical signals.
2. **Signal Conditioning Circuitry** — amplifies, filters, and linearizes the raw sensor output.
3. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — converts conditioned analog voltage into a digital code.
4. **Computer with DAQ software (NI-DAQmx + LabVIEW)** — acquires, displays, logs, and analyzes the data.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners award 0.5 marks for each correctly named component, 0.5 for the definition. Mentioning the food-industry context fetches the remaining 0.5 marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEFTT751]**
*What is meant by "simulated data" in the context of NI-DAQ? Give one practical situation where simulated data is preferred over real data.*

**Model Answer:**
Simulated data refers to artificially generated electrical signals that mimic the output of real sensors, produced by **NI-DAQmx Simulated Devices** or by software routines (e.g., a **Sine Waveform VI** in LabVIEW). The same DAQmx API is used to read these values as if they came from a physical sensor.

**Practical situation where simulated data is preferred:**
During the **development and testing of LabVIEW programs in a classroom or off-site location** where the actual NI hardware and sensors are not physically available. This allows students and engineers to validate data-processing algorithms, alarm logic, and control code *before* deploying on a real production line, saving time and avoiding risk to real food products.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*(a)* Explain the architecture of an NI-DAQ system with a neat block diagram. Describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A 12-bit ADC with an input range of **0 to 10 V** is used in an NI-DAQ module. Compute the **quantization step (LSB)** and the **digital output code** for an analog input of **6.825 V**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-A

**(a) Architecture of an NI-DAQ system (7 marks):**

The NI-DAQ system architecture consists of the following blocks connected in series:

1. **Sensor / Transducer block:** Converts physical parameters (e.g., temperature) into a low-level electrical signal. Examples: Type K thermocouple, PT100 RTD, load cell.
2. **Signal Conditioning block:** Performs amplification, filtering, isolation, cold-junction compensation, and linearization. Modules such as **NI SCXI** or built-in **NI 9219** front-ends perform this task.
3. **Multiplexer:** Routes multiple input channels to a single ADC in time-multiplexed fashion, reducing cost.
4. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Quantizes the analog voltage into discrete digital codes. Resolution typically 12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit.
5. **DAQmx Driver (Software Layer):** Provides the API for configuration (timing, triggering, channel ranges) and data transfer between hardware and application.
6. **Application Software (LabVIEW):** Reads data using While Loops, scales engineering units, displays waveforms, logs to disk, and generates control signals.
7. **Storage and Output Devices:** TDMS files, databases, and actuators (heaters, valves, motors) that close the control loop.

> Each block carries 1 mark; the connecting arrows/final sentence earns 0.5 marks for coherent flow.

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $n = 12 \; \text{bits}$
- $V_{FS} = 10 \; V$ (unipolar, 0 to 10 V)
- $V_{in} = 6.825 \; V$

**Step 1 — Number of discrete levels:**

$$
N_{levels} = 2^{n} = 2^{12} = 4,096
$$

**Step 2 — Quantization step (LSB):**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{10}{4,096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3} \; V \approx 2.44 \; mV
$$

**Step 3 — Digital output code:**

$$
\text{Code} = \text{round}\left(\frac{V_{in}}{\Delta V}\right) = \text{round}\left(\frac{6.825}{0.0024414}\right)
$$

$$
\text{Code} = \text{round}(2{,}795.17) = 2,795
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (optional, 0.5 bonus):**

$$
V_{reconstructed} = 2{,}795 \times 0.0024414 = 6.824 \; V
$$

The quantization error is $|6.825 - 6.824| = 0.001 \; V$, which is less than one LSB — confirming correctness.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**
*(a)* With the help of a diagram, explain the concept of **NI-DAQmx simulated devices**. Discuss any three advantages and two limitations of using simulated data in academic and industrial contexts. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A pasteurization process requires the milk temperature to be maintained at **72 °C** for **15 seconds**. A Type K thermocouple ($S = 41\;\mu V/°C$) connected to a 16-bit NI-DAQ with input range $\pm 10 \; V$ is used. Calculate the **expected voltage at the thermocouple terminals** and the **corresponding ADC digital code**. Also state the Nyquist sampling rate if the dominant temperature fluctuation frequency is known to be **0.5 Hz**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Q-B

**(a) NI-DAQmx Simulated Devices (7 marks):**

**Definition and Diagram (3 marks):**
A *simulated device* is a software-emulated DAQ device created in **NI Measurement & Automation Explorer (MAX)** that exposes the same NI-DAQmx API as physical hardware but does not require any physical module or sensors. Users can create one by right-clicking **Devices and Interfaces → Create New → NI-DAQmx Simulated Device**, selecting a model (e.g., **NI PXI-6255**), and clicking OK.

**Three advantages (3 marks, 1 each):**
1. **No hardware dependency** — programming and algorithm development can proceed without physical NI modules, which is ideal for student labs and remote learning.
2. **Reproducibility** — every run produces identical, deterministic test data, allowing regression testing of code.
3. **Cost-effective debugging** — complex fault scenarios (sensor drift, noise spikes) can be injected programmatically to test alarm and control logic safely.

**Two limitations (1 mark, 0.5 each):**
1. **Cannot capture real sensor non-idealities** (thermal lag, EMI, lead resistance) that only become apparent on actual hardware.
2. **No physical I/O** — cannot drive real actuators or close a real control loop, limiting validation of hardware-in-the-loop behavior.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T = 72 \; °C$
- $S_{TC} = 41 \;\mu V/°C = 41 \times 10^{-6} \; V/°C$
- $n = 16 \; \text{bits}$
- $V_{FS} = \pm 10 \; V$ → total span $= 20 \; V$
- Dominant fluctuation frequency $f_{max} = 0.5 \; Hz$

**Step 1 — Expected thermocouple voltage:**

$$
V_{TC} = T \times S_{TC} = 72 \times 41 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
V_{TC} = 2{,}952 \times 10^{-6} \; V = 2.952 \; mV
$$

**Step 2 — ADC quantization step:**

$$
\Delta V = \frac{2 \cdot V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \frac{20}{65{,}536} = 3.05176 \times 10^{-4} \; V
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the digital code (bipolar, so we add 0.5 offset):**

$$
\text{Code} = \left(\frac{V_{TC}}{2 \cdot V_{FS}} + 0.5\right) \times 2^{n}
$$

$$
\text{Code} = \left(\frac{0.002952}{20} + 0.5\right) \times 65{,}536
$$

$$
\text{Code} = (0.0001476 + 0.5) \times 65{,}536 = 0.5001476 \times 65{,}536
$$

$$
\text{Code} \approx 32{,}793.66 \approx 32{,}794
$$

**Step 4 — Nyquist sampling rate:**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1 \; Hz
$$

In practice, a safety factor of 5–10× is used, giving $f_s \approx 5–10 \; Hz$ for this pasteurization application.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. Forgetting the **bipolar offset (0.5)** in Step 3 — this single mistake causes a 3-mark deduction, as the code will be off by half the ADC range.
> 2. Confusing **±10 V** with **0–10 V** — the former has a 20 V span, the latter only 10 V. Always verify the input range configuration in **MAX → Device Properties**.
> 3. Writing the Nyquist formula as $f_s = 2f_{max}$ (equality) instead of $f_s \geq 2f_{max}$ (inequality) — the latter is mathematically correct.
> 4. Failing to convert µV to V in Step 1, leading to a final voltage 1,000,000× too large.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the bridge between physical food-industry parameters and digital computer analysis.
- **NI-DAQ** combines **hardware** (USB/PXI modules) with **NI-DAQmx driver software** and **LabVIEW** for a complete solution.
- The **six-stage pipeline** is: **Sensor → Signal Conditioning → Multiplexer → ADC → DAQmx Driver → Application/Storage/Control**.
- **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** must be satisfied: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to prevent aliasing.
- **ADC Quantization Step (LSB)** is given by $\Delta V = V_{FS} / 2^{n}$ (unipolar) or $2V_{FS}/2^{n}$ (bipolar).
- **Type K thermocouples** produce approximately **41 µV per °C**.
- **Simulated devices** in **NI-MAX** allow software-only testing of DAQ code without physical hardware — invaluable for academic and pre-deployment validation.
- The **NI-DAQmx task lifecycle** is: **Uncommitted → Reserved → Running → (Reserved → Uncommitted)**. Reading from an Uncommitted task throws an error.
- Always configure the **correct sensor type** (AI Temperature vs. AI Voltage) in NI-DAQmx to enable internal cold-junction compensation and NIST linearization.
- For **food industry applications**, the most common DAQ channels are **temperature, pH, humidity, pressure, flow, and weight** — directly traceable to **HACCP** critical control points.
- **TDMS (Technical Data Management Streaming)** is the recommended NI file format for high-speed logging of DAQ data.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Programming Concepts in LabVIEW
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT751 - ICT APPLICATIONS IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Programming Concepts in LabVIEW

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**LabVIEW** (short for **Laboratory Virtual Instrument Engineering Workbench**) is a graphical dataflow programming language developed by **National Instruments (NI)**. It uses a block-diagram-based, icon-driven programming paradigm (often called **"G" language**) to create programs called **Virtual Instruments (VIs)**. In the context of food industry applications, LabVIEW is widely employed for **process automation, sensor data logging, quality control instrumentation, and SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)** operations on food processing lines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The core focus is on the **Basic Programming Concepts in LabVIEW** — including the dataflow programming model, front-panel and block-diagram workspace, controls/indicators, wires, structures (loops, case), shift registers, and the use of sub-VIs. These form the foundational units for any VI.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of LabVIEW programming like a **factory conveyor belt for milk pasteurization**:
- Imagine a conveyor belt (this is the **wire**) that moves milk bottles.
- The milk must arrive at the heating chamber **before** the heating can begin.
- The heating chamber is a **node** (function block) that cannot execute until all its inputs (ingredients, bottles) are available.
- If you place two machines in sequence, the second one waits for the first to finish — this is **dataflow execution**.
- The **operator's control panel** with gauges and buttons is the **Front Panel**; the **internal wiring diagram** behind it is the **Block Diagram**.

In short: *if data is ready, the node fires.* This is the opposite of sequential text-based programming (like C), where commands execute line-by-line regardless of data readiness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary for KTU:**
> - **VI (Virtual Instrument)** = A LabVIEW program
> - **Front Panel** = User Interface (UI)
> - **Block Diagram** = Source code (graphical)
> - **Control** = Input (e.g., knob, button)
> - **Indicator** = Output (e.g., chart, LED, graph)
> - **Wire** = Data path
> - **Node** = Function/operation
> - **Sub-VI** = Subroutine (reusable VI)

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics Used in LabVIEW Programming

- **Sample Rate (Hz)** — Frequency at which the DAQ device acquires data; typical food industry values: **1 Hz to 1000 Hz**.
- **Data Throughput (samples/sec)** — Rate of data flow through the wire.
- **Polling Interval (ms)** — Time between successive read/write operations.
- **Boolean Logic Levels** — Only **0 (FALSE)** or **1 (TRUE)**.
- **Time Stamps** — Use **32-bit seconds since 1904 epoch** (LabVIEW's default).

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Dataflow Programming Paradigm

Unlike text-based languages (C, Python, Java) which use **control flow** (sequential line-by-line execution), LabVIEW uses **data flow**:

1. A **node** executes **only when all its inputs are available**.
2. A node delivers output to the next node along its wires when execution completes.
3. Multiple parallel paths (branches) execute **concurrently** if data is ready.
4. A node with no inputs executes immediately upon program start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This data-driven execution is the **core distinguishing feature** of LabVIEW — expect at least 1 short-answer question on this in your KTU ESE.

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Virtual Instrument (VI)

Every VI has **two main windows**:

| Component | Purpose | Common Elements | Window Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Front Panel** | User Interface | Controls (knobs, switches, text inputs), Indicators (charts, LEDs, graphs, tanks) | Interactive |
| **Block Diagram** | Source Code | Wires, nodes, structures, terminals, icons | Graphical Code |
| **Connector Pane** | Sub-VI I/O port | Pins for inputs and outputs | Visible only when editing icon |
| **Icon** | Visual representation of sub-VI | Editable image | Top-right of both windows |

### 2.3 Data Types in LabVIEW (Critical for KTU)

LabVIEW wires are colored by data type. You **must** know these for the exam:

| Wire Color | Data Type | Symbol | Example Use in Food Industry |
|---|---|---|---|
| Orange (Floating) | Double Precision (DBL) | `DBL` | Temperature in °C, pH, viscosity |
| Blue | Integer (I32) | `I32` | Counter for bottles, batch number |
| Green | Boolean | `TF` | Pump ON/OFF, valve open/close |
| Pink | String | `abc` | Product name, batch label |
| Brown | Cluster | `⊕` | Group of mixed data (sensor + ID + time) |
| Yellow dotted | Dynamic Data | `dyn` | Waveform from DAQmx |
| White | Waveform | `~` | Continuous data stream |

> [!NOTE]
> A wire connects **one source** to **one or more destinations** (fan-out allowed). It cannot connect mismatched data types — LabVIEW will show a **broken wire** (dashed line) if types mismatch. A broken wire is a **compile-time error**.

### 2.4 Core Programming Structures

**A. While Loop**
- Repeats a sub-diagram until a **stop condition** (Boolean TRUE) is received.
- Executes at least **once**.
- Located on `Functions Palette → Programming → Structures`.
- Has a **Loop Count (i) terminal** and a **Stop terminal**.

**B. For Loop**
- Executes the sub-diagram a **fixed number of N times**.
- The `N` terminal must be wired before execution.

**C. Case Structure**
- Like `if-else` or `switch-case` in C.
- Contains multiple sub-diagrams (cases); only one executes based on a **selector terminal**.
- Selector can be Boolean, Integer, String, or Error Cluster.

**D. Sequence Structure**
- **Flat Sequence:** Executes frames left-to-right (or top-to-bottom for stacked).
- **Stacked Sequence:** Adds memory but is generally deprecated in modern code.
- Used when **data dependency is ambiguous**.

**E. Flattened/Stacked Sequence** — De-emphasized in modern LabVIEW, prefer Flat Sequence with wires.

### 2.5 Shift Registers and Feedback Nodes

| Element | Purpose | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Shift Register** | Stores data from previous iteration to next | Running average of temperature over time |
| **Feedback Node** | Stores output as input for next loop | Cumulative sum, integrator for flow rate |
| **Tunnel** | Default I/O point on loop border | Passes scalar/array value into or out of loop |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Shift Register** is initialized by wiring a value to its left terminal **outside** the loop. The right terminal receives data from the previous iteration.

### 2.6 Local and Global Variables

| Type | Scope | Best Practice |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Variable** | Within a single VI | Use sparingly; can cause race conditions |
| **Global Variable** | Across multiple VIs | Stored in a separate `.vi` file; useful for sharing parameters |
| **Functional Global Variable (FGV)** | Across loops, race-free | Preferred over race-prone locals |

### 2.7 Sub-VIs and Modular Programming

A **Sub-VI** is a VI used inside another VI, equivalent to a **subroutine** in C. Steps to create:

1. Design Front Panel and Block Diagram.
2. Create **Icon** (right-click connector pane → Edit Icon).
3. Wire the **Connector Pane** (right-click icon → Patterns → assign inputs/outputs).
4. Save the VI.
5. Drop it onto the block diagram of the calling VI by dragging from `Select a VI`.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Note |
|---|---|---|
| Total acquisition time | $T_{total} = N \times T_s$ | $T_s$ = sample period (s), $N$ = number of samples |
| Sampling rate | $f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz (samples/sec) |
| Throughput (While Loop) | $\text{iterations/sec} = \frac{1}{\Delta t_{loop}}$ | $\Delta t_{loop}$ = average loop iteration time |
| Running mean (Shift Reg) | $\bar{x}_k = \frac{x_k + (k-1)\cdot\bar{x}_{k-1}}{k}$ | Recursive form |
| Cumulative sum | $S_k = S_{k-1} + x_k$ | Used with feedback node |
| Pulse rate from counter | $f = \frac{N_{pulses}}{T_{window}}$ | Hz, e.g., flow meter pulses |
| Data reduction (avg of N) | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Used in buffer-based averaging |
| Conversion (Analog) | $V_{eng} = \frac{V_{ADC}}{2^n - 1} \times V_{range}$ | n = ADC bit resolution |
| Bit-to-Byte conversion | $B = \frac{b}{8}$ | Storage size |
| Buffer size | $M = f_s \times T_{buffer}$ | Samples in FIFO buffer |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Running Average Using Shift Register

A classic KTU problem is: *Derive the recursive equation for computing the running mean of a stream of $N$ temperature readings inside a While Loop, and explain how a Shift Register realizes it.*

**Step 1 — Define the running mean at iteration $k$:**

The arithmetic mean of the first $k$ samples is:

$$
\bar{x}_k = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i
$$

where each $x_i$ is a temperature reading (°C) acquired at iteration $i$.

**Step 2 — Expand the sum by separating the most recent term:**

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i \;=\; \left(\sum_{i=1}^{k-1} x_i\right) + x_k
$$

The first part is exactly $(k-1)\cdot\bar{x}_{k-1}$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{k} x_i = (k-1)\cdot\bar{x}_{k-1} + x_k
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the running mean equation:**

$$
\bar{x}_k = \frac{(k-1)\cdot\bar{x}_{k-1} + x_k}{k}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify to a form suitable for LabVIEW (split numerator):**

$$
\bar{x}_k = \left(1 - \frac{1}{k}\right)\cdot \bar{x}_{k-1} \;+\; \frac{x_k}{k}
$$

**Step 5 — Map to LabVIEW terminals:**

- The previous mean $\bar{x}_{k-1}$ is stored on the **right-shift register**.
- The iteration index $k$ is obtained from the **loop count terminal `i`**.
- The current sample $x_k$ comes from the **sensor (control or DAQ input)**.
- A **Divide** function is used to compute $\frac{1}{k}$ and $\frac{x_k}{k}$.
- A **Multiply** and **Add** function combines the terms.
- The result is wired to the **left-shift register** for use in the next iteration.

**Step 6 — Initialization (outside the loop):**

The left-shift register (which becomes the new $\bar{x}_{k}$) is initialized with the seed value:

$$
\bar{x}_0 = 0 \quad \text{(default zero initialization, valid for $k \ge 1$)}
$$

**Step 7 — Final closed-form limit:**

As $k \to \infty$, the contribution of the earliest samples fades and the running mean converges to the true population mean $\mu$:

$$
\lim_{k \to \infty} \bar{x}_k = \mu = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i
$$

This is the foundation of **digital signal averaging** in food-quality spectroscopy and NIR moisture sensors.

### 3.2 Derivation: Sampling Rate to Acquire N Points in Time T

**Step 1 — Definition of sampling period:**

The sampling period $T_s$ is the inverse of the sampling rate $f_s$:

$$
T_s = \frac{1}{f_s}
$$

**Step 2 — Total acquisition time is the product:**

To acquire $N$ consecutive samples:

$$
T_{total} = N \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required sampling rate:**

$$
f_s = \frac{N}{T_{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Worked example (KTU-style):**

A NIR spectrometer in a wheat moisture analyzer must collect **N = 1024 points** in **T = 2 seconds**. Compute the required sampling rate.

$$
f_s = \frac{N}{T_{total}} = \frac{1024}{2} = 512 \text{ Hz}
$$

This is well within the typical 1 kHz DAQ capability of a NI USB-6008/6009 device.

### 3.3 Derivation: Analog-to-Digital Conversion (Binary → Engineering Units)

**Step 1 — The ADC produces a digital count $N_{ADC}$ in the range:**

$$
0 \le N_{ADC} \le 2^n - 1
$$

**Step 2 — The corresponding voltage is:**

$$
V_{ADC} = N_{ADC} \times \text{LSB}
$$

where the LSB (least significant bit) size is:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{range}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Combining gives the engineering-unit conversion formula:**

$$
V_{ADC} = N_{ADC} \times \frac{V_{range}}{2^n}
$$

**Step 4 — Example:** A 12-bit ADC with $V_{range} = 10$ V gives a reading of $N_{ADC} = 2048$. Then:

$$
V_{ADC} = 2048 \times \frac{10}{2^{12}} = 2048 \times \frac{10}{4096} = 5.0 \text{ V}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: While Loop vs. For Loop

| Parameter | While Loop | For Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Iteration count | Variable, based on stop condition | Fixed, defined by `N` |
| Minimum executions | At least 1 | 0 if N = 0 |
| Stop condition | Boolean terminal | None — runs N times exactly |
| Typical use | Continuous monitoring of a process tank | Iterating over a fixed array of samples |
| Risk | May run forever if condition is never TRUE | Always terminates |
| KTU exam question type | "When would you prefer a While Loop over a For Loop?" | "Implement a fixed-iteration loop" |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Core VI Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[User Input Layer\nFront Panel - Controls] --> nodeB[Data Acquisition Layer\nDAQmx or Simulator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Processing Layer\nWhile Loop + Shift Register]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Decision Layer\nCase Structure]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Output Layer\nFront Panel - Indicators]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Logging Layer\nWrite to File - TDMS]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Operator Display\nCharts, Tanks, LEDs]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Storage Media\nHDD or Network Drive]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Sub-VI Library\nModular Functions]
    I1[Reusable VIs: Mean, RMS, Alarm] --> nodeI
    I2[Filter VIs: Butterworth] --> nodeI
    I3[Custom Math VIs] --> nodeI
```

### 4.2 Dataflow Execution Diagram: A Simple Two-Node VI

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Node A: Acquire\nTemperature Sensor] -->|Wire - DBL Orange| nodeB[Node B: Compare\nThreshold 80 C]
    nodeB -->|Wire - Boolean Green| nodeC[Node C: Alarm LED\nIndicator]
    nodeA -.->|Fanout Wire| nodeD[Node D: Chart\nWaveform Display]
    nodeE[Knob: Set Threshold\nControl 80 C] -->|Wire - DBL Orange| nodeB
    nodeF[Stop Button\nControl] -->|Wire - Boolean| nodeG{While Loop\nStop Condition}
    nodeB --> nodeH[Counter\ni terminal]
    nodeH --> nodeG
    nodeG -->|Loop| nodeA
```

**Interpretation:** Node B (Compare) executes **only after** both its inputs (current temperature and threshold) are available. The While Loop terminates when the Stop Button is pressed, breaking the dataflow chain.

### 4.3 Shift Register Data Movement (Per Iteration)

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph "Iteration k-1"
        A1[Right Shift Register\nStores x mean k-1] --> A2[Processing Block\nMean Computation]
    end
    subgraph "Iteration k"
        B1[Sensor Reading xk] --> B2[Processing Block\nMean Computation]
        A2 -.data handed over.-> A3[Left Shift Register\nReceives new mean]
        A3 --> A1
    end
    B2 --> A3
```

### 4.4 Case Structure: Temperature Alarm Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A{Temperature Input} --> B{Case Selector}
    B -->|TRUE Temp > 80 C| C[Case TRUE\nRED LED ON\nBuzzer ON\nLog Fault]
    B -->|FALSE Temp <= 80 C| D[Case FALSE\nGREEN LED ON\nContinue Logging]
    B -->|Default| E[Default Case\nNo Action]
    C --> F[Alarm Output]
    D --> G[Normal Output]
```

### 4.5 Sub-VI Modular Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    Main[Main VI - Pasteurization Line] --> SubA[Sub-VI: ReadTemp\nAcquires temperature]
    Main --> SubB[Sub-VI: ComputeMean\nRunning average]
    Main --> SubC[Sub-VI: CheckAlarm\nThreshold logic]
    Main --> SubD[Sub-VI: WriteLog\nTDMS file write]
    SubA --> SubA1[Icon Connector Pane]
    SubB --> SubB1[Icon Connector Pane]
    SubC --> SubC1[Icon Connector Pane]
    SubD --> SubD1[Icon Connector Pane]
```

### 4.6 Front Panel vs Block Diagram Mapping

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph FP[Front Panel - User Interface]
        F1[Knob: Set Temp 80 C - Control]
        F2[LED: Alarm - Indicator]
        F3[Waveform Chart - Indicator]
        F4[Stop Button - Control]
    end
    subgraph BD[Block Diagram - Source Code]
        G1[Terminal of Knob]
        G2[Terminal of LED]
        G3[Terminal of Chart]
        G4[Terminal of Stop]
        G5[While Loop Boundary]
        G6[Compare Function Node]
        G7[Sub-VI: Acquire]
    end
    F1 -.Terminal.- G1
    F2 -.Terminal.- G2
    F3 -.Terminal.- G3
    F4 -.Terminal.- G4
    G1 --> G6
    G7 --> G6
    G6 --> G2
    G7 --> G3
    G4 --> G5
    G6 --> G5
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a Virtual Instrument (VI) in LabVIEW. List its two main windows and state the difference between a Control and an Indicator with a food-industry example.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** is a LabVIEW program that emulates the functions of a physical instrument using software. The two main windows are:

- **Front Panel** — the user interface where the operator interacts with controls and views indicators.
- **Block Diagram** — the graphical source code showing wires, nodes, and structures.

| Element | Direction | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Control** | Input (user → program) | A knob to set the pasteurization temperature setpoint |
| **Indicator** | Output (program → user) | A thermometer-style gauge displaying current milk temperature |

A **Control** is a user input device; an **Indicator** is a user output display. They differ in data flow direction only.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is dataflow programming? How does it differ from control-flow execution in C language?*

**Model Answer:**

**Dataflow programming** is an execution paradigm in which a node executes **as soon as all its input data becomes available** through the wires connected to it. If multiple nodes have all their inputs ready, they execute **in parallel**.

In contrast, **control-flow execution** (as in C) executes statements **sequentially in the order written by the programmer**, regardless of data availability, unless explicit parallelism (e.g., multi-threading) is invoked.

| Aspect | LabVIEW (Dataflow) | C (Control-Flow) |
|---|---|---|
| Order of execution | Determined by data readiness | Determined by source code order |
| Parallelism | Inherent for independent nodes | Requires explicit threads or libraries |
| Visual | Graphical (wires & icons) | Textual (statements) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 4 Internal Choice Rule:** KTU allows the student to answer **either OR** any one of the two alternatives. Both must be of equal difficulty.

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the architecture of a Virtual Instrument in detail, including the Front Panel, Block Diagram, Connector Pane, and Icon. Use a pasteurization control system as a real-world example. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Design a LabVIEW block diagram (describe the flow) to continuously monitor the temperature of a milk pasteurizer. The system must:*
*(i) Acquire temperature every 100 ms;*
*(ii) Compute a running mean of the last 10 readings;*
*(iii) Trigger a red LED alarm if the mean exceeds 75 °C;*
*(iv) Stop acquisition on user button press.*
*State the loop type, structures, shift register usage, and the front panel elements you would use. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

A **Virtual Instrument (VI)** in LabVIEW is the analogue of a function in C — a self-contained, modular program with both **functionality and a user interface**. Its architecture comprises four components:

**1. Front Panel (UI)**
The interactive window built with **Controls** (inputs) and **Indicators** (outputs). For a pasteurization control:
- Controls: Temperature Setpoint Knob, Start/Stop Button, Flow Rate Setpoint
- Indicators: Temperature Gauge, Status LED (Green/Yellow/Red), Strip Chart of temperature history, Process Tank (mimics milk level)

**2. Block Diagram (Source Code)**
Contains the **graphical source code**:
- **Terminals** mirror every front-panel object.
- **Wires** transfer data between nodes, color-coded by type.
- **Nodes** are built-in functions (e.g., Add, Divide), user sub-VIs, or Express VIs.
- **Structures** (While/For loops, Case, Flat Sequence) control execution flow.

**3. Connector Pane**
Defines the **inputs and outputs** that the VI exposes when used as a sub-VI. Each rectangle on the connector pane corresponds to one front-panel control or indicator.

**4. Icon**
A **graphical symbol** that represents the sub-VI on the block diagram of the calling VI. Editable via the Icon Editor.

**Real-world example:** A *Milk Pasteurization Monitor VI* — the Front Panel shows the operator a real-time chart of milk temperature and a start/stop button; the Block Diagram runs a While Loop, acquires from a thermocouple, applies a moving-average filter, and updates the chart and alarm LED.

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Loop type chosen:** `While Loop` (acquisition must continue until the user stops it; number of iterations is unknown).

**Required Front Panel Elements:**
| Element | Type | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Thermocouple Reading | Indicator (Numeric or Tank) | Displays current temperature |
| Mean Temperature | Indicator (Numeric) | Shows the running mean |
| Red LED | Indicator (Boolean) | Alarm |
| Stop Button | Control (Boolean) | Stops the loop |
| Waveform Chart | Indicator (Chart) | Plots temperature vs time |

**Required Block Diagram Components (in order inside the While Loop):**

1. **DAQmx Acquire** Express VI (or a custom Sub-VI) — samples thermocouple at 100 ms.
2. A **Shift Register** initialized to `0` outside the loop.
3. An **Array of last 10 values** is held in a shift register.
4. The **Mean of 10 elements** is computed using `Mean PtByPt` function (a built-in Point-by-Point sub-VI) or by dividing the array sum by 10.
5. A **Case Structure** (Boolean selector): 
   - TRUE case (mean > 75 °C) → wire TRUE to the Red LED terminal; 
   - FALSE case → wire FALSE to Red LED.
6. The Stop button is wired to the While Loop's **stop-condition terminal** (the small circular icon at the bottom-right corner of the loop border).
7. The current temperature is also wired to the **Waveform Chart** (auto-indexing tunnel if coming from an array).

**Flow Summary:**
```
[User clicks Run] 
   → While Loop begins 
   → DAQmx reads thermocouple (100 ms wait) 
   → Push new value into shift register, drop oldest 
   → Compute Mean 
   → Compare Mean with 75 
   → If > 75 → RED LED ON, else OFF 
   → Append to chart 
   → Check Stop button → if TRUE, exit loop 
   → End
```

**KTU Mark Distribution Hint:**
- Loop choice with justification: 2
- Front panel elements listing: 2
- Block diagram flow description: 2
- Shift register / case usage: 1

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *With a neat block diagram description, explain the use of a **Shift Register** in a While Loop to compute a running cumulative sum of flow-rate readings from a flow meter in a juice bottling line. Derive the recursive formula and the data type of the shift register content. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Differentiate between a **For Loop** and a **While Loop** in LabVIEW. When would you choose each in a food-quality inspection program? Provide one example for each. **(7 marks)***

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Problem:** A flow meter outputs flow rate $q_k$ (in L/min) every second. We want the cumulative volume $V_k$ consumed over the run-time.

**Step 1 — Discretization:**
Cumulative volume is the integral of flow rate over time:

$$
V(t) = \int_0^t q(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize with a sampling period $T_s = 1$ s:**

$$
V_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} q_i \cdot T_s
$$

**Step 3 — Recursive form (suitable for shift register):**

$$
V_k = V_{k-1} + q_k \cdot T_s
$$

with initial condition $V_0 = 0$.

**Step 4 — LabVIEW Implementation using Shift Register:**

- The **left-shift register** holds the previous cumulative sum $V_{k-1}$.
- Outside the loop, initialize the right terminal with $0$.
- Inside the loop:
   - Multiply the current flow $q_k$ by $T_s = 1$ s using a `Multiply` node.
