# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression (Board-Level Walkthrough)

This is the most-tested derivation in KTU 2024 Scheme Machine Learning modules. We derive the **closed-form update rule** for the weights $w_j$ of a linear regression model.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Given a dataset with $m$ training examples, each having $n$ features:

$$ \mathcal{D} = \{(x^{(1)}, y^{(1)}), (x^{(2)}, y^{(2)}), \ldots, (x^{(m)}, y^{(m)})\} $$

The hypothesis (model) is:

$$ h_\mathbf{w}(x) = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n $$

The cost function (Mean Squared Error) is:

$$ J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right)^2 $$

> **Note:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is added for mathematical convenience so that the exponent cancels during differentiation.

### 3.1.2 Derivation of the Partial Derivative

We need $\frac{\partial J(\mathbf{w})}{\partial w_j}$. We use the **chain rule** of differentiation.

Let $f_i = h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$. Then:

$$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} \left[ f_i^2 \right] $$

Applying chain rule:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} \left[ f_i^2 \right] = 2 \cdot f_i \cdot \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial w_j} $$

Since $f_i = h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}$ and $\frac{\partial y^{(i)}}{\partial w_j} = 0$:

$$ \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial w_j} = \frac{\partial}{\partial w_j} h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) = x_j^{(i)} $$

Substituting back:

$$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} 2 \cdot \left( h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)} $$

Simplifying the $2$'s:

$$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)} $$

### 3.1.3 Final Update Rule

The gradient descent update rule becomes:

$$ \boxed{ w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) \cdot x_j^{(i)} } $$

This rule is applied **simultaneously** for all $j = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$ in every iteration until $J(\mathbf{w})$ converges to a minimum.

### 3.1.4 Convergence Criterion

The algorithm converges when:

$$ \left\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \right\vert < \epsilon $$

where $\epsilon$ is a small tolerance (typically $10^{-6}$) and $t$ is the iteration number.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Confusion Matrix Metrics from First Principles

### 3.2.1 Accuracy Derivation

By definition, accuracy is the fraction of correct predictions over total predictions.

Total predictions = $TP + TN + FP + FN$
Correct predictions = $TP + TN$

Therefore:

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{Correct}}{\text{Total}} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} $$

### 3.2.2 Precision Derivation

Precision answers: *"Of all the items the model labelled as positive, how many were actually positive?"*

$$ \text{Predicted Positives} = TP + FP $$
$$ \text{Actually Positive (in predicted set)} = TP $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} $$

### 3.2.3 Recall Derivation

Recall answers: *"Of all the actually positive items, how many did the model catch?"*

$$ \text{Actual Positives} = TP + FN $$
$$ \text{Caught by Model} = TP $$

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$

### 3.2.4 F1-Score Derivation

F1 is the **harmonic mean** of Precision ($P$) and Recall ($R$). The harmonic mean of two numbers $a$ and $b$ is:

$$ H = \frac{2}{\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}} = \frac{2ab}{a+b} $$

Substituting $a = P$, $b = R$:

$$ \text{F1} = \frac{2PR}{P + R} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Harmonic Mean and Not Arithmetic Mean?**
> The harmonic mean **penalizes extreme values**. If either $P$ or $R$ is very low, F1 becomes low. This is critical in food safety where missing a contaminated batch (low recall) is far more costly than a false alarm (low precision).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU Board-Style Problem

> **Problem:** A ML model classifies 1000 food packets as "Safe" or "Contaminated". The confusion matrix is:
>
> | | Predicted Safe | Predicted Contaminated |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Actual Safe** | 850 | 50 |
> | **Actual Contaminated** | 30 | 70 |
>
> Compute Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score for the "Contaminated" class.

### 3.3.1 Step 1 — Extract Values

For "Contaminated" (positive class):
- $TP = 70$ (correctly identified contaminated)
- $FP = 50$ (safe packets wrongly flagged as contaminated)
- $FN = 30$ (contaminated packets missed)
- $TN = 850$ (correctly identified safe)

### 3.3.2 Step 2 — Compute Accuracy

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{70 + 850}{70 + 850 + 50 + 30} = \frac{920}{1000} = 0.92 $$

### 3.3.3 Step 3 — Compute Precision

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{70}{70 + 50} = \frac{70}{120} \approx 0.583 $$

### 3.3.4 Step 4 — Compute Recall

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{70}{70 + 30} = \frac{70}{100} = 0.70 $$

### 3.3.5 Step 5 — Compute F1-Score

$$ \text{F1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.583 \cdot 0.70}{0.583 + 0.70} = \frac{0.8162}{1.283} \approx 0.636 $$

### 3.3.6 Interpretation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The model has high overall accuracy (92%), but **low precision (58.3%)** and **moderate recall (70%)** for contaminated packets. In food safety, this is **dangerous** — out of every 100 packets flagged as contaminated, ~42 are actually safe (waste), but more critically, 30 contaminated packets slipped through. The company should retrain with more contaminated samples (class balancing) or lower the classification threshold.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Big Data vs. Traditional Data

| Parameter | Traditional RDBMS | Big Data (Hadoop) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Data Volume** | GB | TB to PB |
| **Data Type** | Structured (tables) | Structured + Semi + Unstructured |
| **Schema** | Schema-on-write | Schema-on-read |
| **Processing** | Vertical scaling (bigger server) | Horizontal scaling (more nodes) |
| **Cost Model** | High CAPEX | Low CAPEX, OPEX-based |
| **Throughput** | Low for huge data | High (parallel) |
| **Fault Tolerance** | RAID-based | Replication (factor = 3) |
| **Latency** | Real-time queries | Batch (minutes) or streaming (ms) |
| **Example Tools** | MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server | Hadoop, Spark, NoSQL |
| **Food Industry Use** | Recipe database, inventory | IoT sensor stream, supply chain analytics |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse IaaS/PaaS/SaaS responsibilities** — the most common 2-mark loss is writing "Cloud provides hardware" (too vague). Be specific: "IaaS provides virtualized compute, storage, and network resources".
> 2. **In ML questions, always specify the positive class** when computing Precision/Recall. A 0.5-mark deduction per missing specification.
> 3. **For Big Data, mention all 5 V's if asked for "characteristics"** — students often forget Veracity and Value.
> 4. **MapReduce questions require both Map and Reduce phases explicitly** — writing only "Map phase" gets only 50% marks.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Big Data. List and explain any three characteristics of Big Data.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Big Data refers to extremely large and complex datasets that cannot be processed using traditional database management tools. It demands cost-effective, innovative forms of information processing to extract insight and enable better decision-making.

**Three Characteristics (2 marks — 1 mark each for any two, or 0.67 for each of three):**

1. **Volume:** Refers to the massive amount of data generated, ranging from terabytes to petabytes. In the food industry, this could be the daily sensor data from thousands of refrigerated trucks.

2. **Velocity:** Refers to the speed at which data is generated and must be processed. For example, real-time temperature data from a cold-chain needs to be processed within seconds to prevent spoilage.

3. **Variety:** Refers to the different types and formats of data — structured (sensor tables), semi-structured (JSON logs), and unstructured (images of food products, social media reviews).

*(Other acceptable V's: Veracity — data trustworthiness; Value — actionable business insight.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Supervised and Unsupervised Learning with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Supervised Learning | Unsupervised Learning |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Data Type** | Labelled data (input-output pairs given) | Unlabelled data (only inputs given) |
| **Goal** | Learn a mapping function from input to output | Discover hidden patterns/structure |
| **Algorithms** | Linear Regression, SVM, Decision Tree, KNN | K-Means, DBSCAN, PCA, Apriori |
| **Feedback** | Yes — error is computed against known labels | No — no feedback signal |
| **Example (Food)** | Classify mangoes as Grade A/B/C using labelled training images | Segment customers into groups based on purchase history |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each, 0.5 mark for example each, 0.5 mark for any one differentiating criterion.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, two full Part B questions are provided. Students answer **ONE**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Big Data + Cloud Computing Focus**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the Hadoop architecture in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the role of HDFS and MapReduce in big data processing. Mention the default block size and replication factor.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hadoop Architecture (3 marks):**

Hadoop is an open-source framework that allows distributed storage and processing of large datasets across clusters of commodity computers. It has two major components:

1. **HDFS (Hadoop Distributed File System)** — handles storage.
2. **MapReduce** — handles processing.
3. **YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)** — handles resource management and job scheduling.

**HDFS Architecture (2 marks):**

HDFS follows a master-slave architecture:
- **NameNode (Master):** Stores metadata (file names, permissions, block locations). Runs on a high-memory machine.
- **DataNodes (Slaves):** Store the actual data blocks. Multiple DataNodes run on commodity hardware.
- **Secondary NameNode:** Periodically merges the edit log with the fsimage to prevent the NameNode's memory from overflowing.

When a file is uploaded, it is split into blocks of **128 MB** (default), and each block is replicated to **3** DataNodes (default replication factor) for fault tolerance.

**MapReduce (2 marks):**

MapReduce is a programming model for parallel processing:
- **Map Phase:** Each input record is converted into intermediate key-value pairs.
- **Shuffle & Sort Phase:** Pairs are grouped by key and sorted.
- **Reduce Phase:** All values for a key are aggregated to produce the final output.

**Diagram (use Mermaid or hand-drawn equivalent — full marks for any clear block diagram showing HDFS + MapReduce flow).**

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**Discuss the three service models of cloud computing — IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS — with suitable food industry examples. Compare them on the basis of user control and provider responsibility.**

**Model Answer:**

| Service Model | Full Form | Provider Manages | User Manages | Food Industry Example |
|---------------|-----------|-------------------|--------------|----------------------|
| **IaaS** | Infrastructure as a Service | Virtualization, Storage, Servers | OS, Middleware, Apps, Data | A food company rents virtual servers from AWS EC2 to run its ERP |
| **PaaS** | Platform as a Service | OS, Middleware, Runtime, Dev Tools | Apps and Data | Using Google App Engine to deploy a recipe-recommendation web app |
| **SaaS** | Software as a Service | Entire stack including application | Only user data and configuration | Using Salesforce CRM to manage food distributor relationships |

**Explanation (4 marks — 1.5 for IaaS, 1.5 for PaaS, 1 for SaaS or similar distribution):**

- **IaaS** offers the most flexibility and control. The user can install any OS and software. Best for companies that want to migrate existing applications to the cloud with minimal change.
- **PaaS** abstracts away the infrastructure, allowing developers to focus only on writing code. Ideal for food-tech startups building novel applications like AI-based quality inspection.
- **SaaS** is the most user-friendly. The user just logs in via a browser. Used for off-the-shelf applications like email, CRM, ERP.

**Comparison (2 marks):** As control moves from user to provider (IaaS → PaaS → SaaS), flexibility decreases but ease-of-use increases. Food companies should choose based on in-house technical expertise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Machine Learning Focus**

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]**
**Explain the difference between classification and regression in machine learning. Derive the gradient descent update rule for linear regression. State the convergence criterion.**

**Model Answer:**

**Classification vs. Regression (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Classification | Regression |
|--------|----------------|------------|
| **Output** | Discrete category/label | Continuous numerical value |
| **Examples** | "Fresh" vs. "Spoiled" | Predicting moisture content in % |
| **Algorithms** | Logistic Regression, SVM, KNN | Linear Regression, Ridge, Lasso |
| **Evaluation** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1 | MSE, MAE, RMSE, R² |

**Linear Regression Setup (1 mark):**

Hypothesis: $h_\mathbf{w}(x) = w_0 + w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + \cdots + w_n x_n$

Cost function: $J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{1}{2m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})^2$

**Derivation of Gradient Descent Update (3 marks):**

Differentiating $J$ with respect to $w_j$:

$$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)} $$

Applying the gradient descent rule:

$$ \boxed{ w_j := w_j - \alpha \cdot \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} (h_\mathbf{w}(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)}) \cdot x_j^{(i)} } $$

**Convergence Criterion (1 mark):**

$$ \left\vert J^{(t)} - J^{(t-1)} \right\vert < \epsilon $$

where $\epsilon$ is a small threshold (typically $10^{-6}$). When the change in cost between consecutive iterations is below this threshold, the algorithm is said to have converged.

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]**
**For a binary classification problem in food quality detection, the confusion matrix is as follows:**
- True Positives (TP) = 80
- False Positives (FP) = 20
- False Negatives (FN) = 40
- True Negatives (TN) = 60

**Compute the Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score. Which metric is most important in a food safety application, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

**Total samples:** $80 + 20 + 40 + 60 = 200$

**Step 1 — Accuracy (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} = \frac{80 + 60}{200} = \frac{140}{200} = 0.70 $$

**Step 2 — Precision (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} = \frac{80}{80 + 20} = \frac{80}{100} = 0.80 $$

**Step 3 — Recall (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} = \frac{80}{80 + 40} = \frac{80}{120} \approx 0.667 $$

**Step 4 — F1-Score (1.5 marks):**

$$ \text{F1} = \frac{2 \cdot P \cdot R}{P + R} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.80 \cdot 0.667}{0.80 + 0.667} = \frac{1.0672}{1.467} \approx 0.728 $$

**Most Important Metric in Food Safety (1 mark):**

**Recall is the most important metric** in a food safety application. Recall measures the proportion of actually contaminated items that the model successfully detects. A low recall means many contaminated items are missed (False Negatives), which can lead to public health hazards, product recalls, legal liabilities, and brand damage. In food safety, **missing a contaminated batch is far more costly than raising a false alarm** — hence, the model should be tuned to maximize recall, even at the cost of some precision.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE**

- **Big Data** = high-Volume, high-Velocity, high-Variety data (5 V's: Volume, Velocity, Variety, Veracity, Value).
- **Hadoop** = HDFS (storage) + MapReduce (processing) + YARN (resource management).
- **HDFS block size = 128 MB**; **replication factor = 3**.
- **MapReduce** has two phases: **Map** and **Reduce**, with an intermediate **Shuffle & Sort** phase.
- **Machine Learning** = learning from data without explicit programming.
- **Three types of ML:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Reinforcement.
- **Linear Regression model:** $y = w_0 + \sum w_i x_i$.
- **MSE cost function:** $J = \frac{1}{2m} \sum (h_w(x) - y)^2$.
- **Gradient Descent:** $w_j := w_j - \alpha \frac{\partial J}{\partial w_j}$.
- **Confusion Matrix:** TP, FP, FN, TN.
- **Accuracy = (TP+TN)/Total**; **Precision = TP/(TP+FP)**; **Recall = TP/(TP+FN)**; **F1 = 2PR/(P+R)**.
- **K-Means** is an unsupervised clustering algorithm; iteratively assigns points to nearest centroid and recomputes centroids.
- **Cloud Computing** = on-demand, pay-as-you-go, scalable IT resources over the Internet.
- **5 Characteristics of Cloud (NIST):** On-demand self-service, Broad network access, Resource pooling, Rapid elasticity, Measured service.
- **Service Models:** IaaS (raw infra), PaaS (platform), SaaS (ready-to-use app).
- **Deployment Models:** Public, Private, Hybrid, Community.
- **Cloud examples:** AWS, Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud Platform (GCP), IBM Cloud.
- **Food Industry Applications:**
  - **Big Data:** Cold-chain monitoring, supply chain analytics, demand forecasting.
  - **ML:** Automated food quality grading, predictive maintenance, spoilage prediction.
  - **Cloud:** Recipe databases, traceability platforms, IoT sensor data lakes, ERP for food companies.
- **Common KTU Pitfalls:** Forgetting 5th V (Veracity/Value), confusing IaaS/PaaS/SaaS responsibilities, omitting the Shuffle phase in MapReduce, not specifying the positive class in confusion matrix problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Machine Learning and Image Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT751 - ICT APPLICATIONS IN FOOD INDUSTRY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Machine Learning and Image Processing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Machine Learning (ML) — Formal Definition

**Machine Learning (ML)** is a sub-discipline of **Artificial Intelligence (AI)** that enables computational systems to learn patterns, relationships, and decision boundaries directly from empirical data — *without being explicitly programmed* for every rule.

In the context of the **food industry**, ML algorithms are trained on historical datasets of food products (e.g., moisture content, pH, color values, microbial counts) to build **predictive models** for quality grading, shelf-life estimation, adulteration detection, and process optimization.

$$ \text{ML} \subseteq \text{AI} \subseteq \text{Computer Science} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per the **PEFTT751 – ICT Applications in Food Industry (2024 Scheme)** syllabus, Module 4 specifically requires students to understand:
> - **Supervised vs. Unsupervised Learning**
> - **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** basics
> - **Digital Image Processing** fundamentals
> - **Computer Vision** applications in food quality assessment

---

## 1.2 Image Processing (IP) — Formal Definition

**Image Processing (IP)** is the discipline of performing mathematical operations on **digitized images** (typically represented as 2D or 3D matrices of pixel intensity values) to enhance, transform, extract features, or classify visual information.

In food technology, a **digital food image** is a matrix $I(x,y)$ where each pixel carries intensity values — usually in **Red, Green, Blue (RGB)** channels, or in grayscale.

$$ I(x,y) \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots, 255\} \quad \text{for an 8-bit grayscale image} $$

---

## 1.3 💡 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sorting Master" Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Imagine a seasoned tea-taster in a tea factory 🍵**
>
> - **Traditional Programming** is like writing an explicit rulebook: *"If leaf color is X, then grade A."* You must predefine every condition.
> - **Machine Learning** is like a trainee who tastes 10,000 tea samples with the master saying *"This one is Grade A, this is Grade B."* Over time, the trainee *learns the pattern* and can grade new, unseen tea leaves by himself.
> - **Image Processing** is the "trainee's eyes" — a camera captures the leaf image, filters the noise (e.g., shadows), extracts the color and shape features, and feeds them to the ML brain.
>
> Together, **IP acts as the senses** and **ML acts as the decision-making brain** of a smart food inspection system.

---

## 1.4 Why ML + IP in Food Industry? — Real-World Motivation

The modern food industry generates **massive, high-dimensional, multi-sensor data** — far beyond human capacity to analyze in real-time. Key drivers include:

- **Speed**: A computer vision line can inspect **thousands of apples per minute** on a sorting conveyor.
- **Objectivity**: ML removes *human bias* and *fatigue error* in grading.
- **Non-destructive testing**: Cameras and NIR sensors check food **without cutting or contaminating** it.
- **Traceability**: ML models can flag contaminated batches by pattern recognition in sensor logs.
- **Consistency**: A model trained once gives **identical decisions** every shift, every day.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry 4.0 Connection:** ML + IP is the *analytical backbone* of the **Smart Food Factory** — where IoT sensors stream data and AI dashboards make autonomous quality and process decisions.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Machine Learning Algorithms

ML algorithms are broadly classified into **three paradigms** based on the nature of the learning signal:

### 🔹 A. Supervised Learning
The algorithm is given **labeled training data** — i.e., input-output pairs $(X_i, Y_i)$.

**How it works — in 4 logical steps:**
1. Collect a labeled dataset (e.g., 1000 tomato images labeled "ripe" or "unripe").
2. Choose a model (e.g., Decision Tree, SVM, Neural Network).
3. Train: the model minimizes a **loss function** $\mathcal{L}$ between predicted and actual labels.
4. Test: deploy on new, unseen images to predict labels.

**Common algorithms used in food industry:**

| Algorithm | KTU-Standard Use Case in Food Industry |
|---|---|
| **k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)** | Classifying fruit varieties by shape/size features |
| **Decision Trees (DT)** | HACCP decision logic (Pass/Fail based on pH, temperature) |
| **Support Vector Machines (SVM)** | Detecting defective vs. acceptable biscuits on a conveyor |
| **Artificial Neural Networks (ANN)** | Predicting drying kinetics, fermentation endpoints |
| **Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN)** | End-to-end image classification of food products |

---

### 🔹 B. Unsupervised Learning
The algorithm receives **unlabeled data** and must discover structure on its own (clustering, dimensionality reduction).

- **Clustering** (e.g., **K-Means**) → Grouping milk samples by fatty acid profile into "premium" vs. "standard" tiers without pre-existing labels.
- **Dimensionality Reduction** (e.g., **PCA — Principal Component Analysis**) → Reducing 200 spectral wavelengths to 5 key features for downstream regression.

---

### 🔹 C. Reinforcement Learning (RL)
The agent learns a **policy** $\pi(a \mid s)$ by interacting with an environment and receiving **rewards** or **penalties**.

- **Food industry use**: Optimizing a bakery oven's temperature profile to minimize energy use while maintaining crust color — the agent "tries" heating schedules and gets rewarded for matching the target color.

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Pillars of Supervised Learning

A supervised model is a **function approximator** $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$, where $\theta$ represents the learnable parameters (weights).

### 🔸 The Loss Function
For regression, the most common loss is the **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y_i - \hat{y}_i \right)^2 $$

For classification, the **Cross-Entropy Loss** is standard:

$$ \mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{c=1}^{C} y_{i,c} \cdot \log \left( \hat{p}_{i,c} \right) $$

### 🔸 The Learning Rule — Gradient Descent
Parameters are updated iteratively to *minimize* the loss:

$$ \theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta_t) $$

where:
- $\eta$ = **learning rate** (a small positive scalar, typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$)
- $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ = gradient of the loss with respect to parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Analogy:** Think of $\mathcal{L}$ as a hilly landscape, and the model parameters as a ball rolling downhill. Gradient descent is the "gravity" — it always nudges the parameters toward the lowest valley (minimum loss).

---

## 2.3 Fundamentals of Digital Image Processing

### 🔸 Image as a Matrix
A grayscale image of size $M \times N$ is:

$$ I = \begin{bmatrix} I(1,1) & I(1,2) & \cdots & I(1,N) \\ I(2,1) & I(2,2) & \cdots & I(2,N) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ I(M,1) & I(M,2) & \cdots & I(M,N) \end{bmatrix} $$

For a **color image**, three such matrices exist — one each for **R, G, B** channels.

### 🔸 Key Image Processing Operations

| Operation | Mathematical Form | Food Industry Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Grayscale Conversion** | $I_{\text{gray}} = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | Simplifying apple ripeness analysis |
| **Thresholding (Binarization)** | $I_b(x,y) = 1$ if $I(x,y) > T$, else $0$ | Segmenting grains vs. background |
| **Histogram Equalization** | Spreads intensity to enhance contrast | Making bruises on fruit visible |
| **Edge Detection (Sobel, Canny)** | Computes spatial gradient $\nabla I$ | Detecting boundaries of chocolate chips |
| **Morphological Operations** | Erosion / Dilation of binary masks | Removing noise from segmented rice grains |
| **Filtering (Gaussian, Median)** | Convolution with kernel $K$ | Smoothing noisy NIR images |

---

## 2.4 Feature Extraction — The Bridge Between IP and ML

Before classical ML (non-deep) can classify a food image, **hand-crafted features** must be extracted:

### 🔸 Color Features
- **Mean RGB** of the segmented region
- **HSV histogram** (Hue, Saturation, Value) — robust to lighting changes

### 🔸 Texture Features
- **Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM)** features: contrast, correlation, energy, homogeneity
- **Local Binary Patterns (LBP)**

### 🔸 Shape Features
- **Area**, **Perimeter**, **Circularity** $= \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$
- **Eccentricity**, **Solidity**

> [!NOTE]
> These features are then fed into an ML classifier (SVM, Random Forest, ANN) to predict quality grades.

---

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Equations

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Image intensity (grayscale) | $I(x,y) \in [0, 255]$ | 8-bit pixel |
| 2 | Grayscale conversion | $I_{\text{gray}} = 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B$ | ITU-R BT.601 weights |
| 3 | Binary thresholding | $I_b = 1$ if $I > T$ else $0$ | $T$ = threshold intensity |
| 4 | Mean Squared Error | $\text{MSE} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ | Loss metric |
| 5 | Cross-Entropy Loss | $\text{CE} = -\sum y_c \log \hat{p}_c$ | Classification loss |
| 6 | Gradient Descent Update | $\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | $\eta$ = learning rate |
| 7 | Circularity (shape feature) | $C = \dfrac{4\pi A}{P^2}$ | $A$ = area, $P$ = perimeter |
| 8 | Image Histogram | $h(k) = $ count of pixels with intensity $k$ | $\sum_k h(k) = M \cdot N$ |
| 9 | Convolution (filtering) | $I'(x,y) = \sum_{u,v} K(u,v) \cdot I(x-u, y-v)$ | $K$ = kernel |
| 10 | Normalization (Min-Max) | $I_{\text{norm}} = \dfrac{I - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}$ | Scales to $[0,1]$ |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Working of a Single Artificial Neuron (Perceptron)

The **Perceptron** is the atomic unit of an Artificial Neural Network (ANN). It mimics a biological neuron by computing a **weighted sum of inputs**, adding a bias, and passing it through an **activation function**.

### 🔹 Step 1 — Define the inputs
Let the input feature vector from a food sample be:

$$ \mathbf{x} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \\ x_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Color score} \\ \text{Firmness (N)} \\ \text{pH} \end{bmatrix} $$

### 🔹 Step 2 — Define the weight vector and bias
The network learns parameters:

$$ \mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix} w_1 \\ w_2 \\ w_3 \end{bmatrix}, \quad b \in \mathbb{R} $$

### 🔹 Step 3 — Compute the pre-activation (linear combination)
This is the dot product of weights and inputs, plus bias:

$$ z = \mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b = w_1 x_1 + w_2 x_2 + w_3 x_3 + b $$

### 🔹 Step 4 — Apply a non-linear activation function
Common choices:
- **Sigmoid**: $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ — output in $(0, 1)$, used for probability.
- **ReLU**: $\phi(z) = \max(0, z)$ — used in deep networks.
- **Tanh**: $\tanh(z) = \dfrac{e^z - e^{-z}}{e^z + e^{-z}}$ — output in $(-1, 1)$.

### 🔹 Step 5 — Final output (predicted class)

$$ \hat{y} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\mathbf{w}^\top \mathbf{x} + b)}} $$

If $\hat{y} > 0.5$ → class **"Acceptable"**; else → class **"Reject"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why non-linearity?** Without an activation function, stacking multiple neurons collapses into a single linear equation — incapable of modeling complex food-quality boundaries (e.g., the non-linear relationship between moisture and microbial growth).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Min-Max Normalization for Image Preprocessing

Food images from different cameras have different lighting conditions. To standardize them:

### Step 1 — Identify the range of pixel intensities
For an 8-bit grayscale image, $I_{\min} = 0$ and $I_{\max} = 255$ in theory. But in practice, the *actual observed* range matters.

### Step 2 — Apply the linear scaling formula

$$ I_{\text{norm}}(x,y) = \frac{I(x,y) - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} $$

### Step 3 — Verify bounds
- When $I(x,y) = I_{\min}$: $I_{\text{norm}} = 0$ ✓
- When $I(x,y) = I_{\max}$: $I_{\text{norm}} = 1$ ✓

### Worked Numerical Example
Suppose a tomato image's red channel has:
- $I_{\min} = 32$, $I_{\max} = 230$, and a particular pixel $I(x,y) = 150$.

Then:

$$ I_{\text{norm}} = \frac{150 - 32}{230 - 32} = \frac{118}{198} \approx 0.596 $$

This normalized value is then fed to the ML model — making the pipeline **lighting-invariant**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Mapping ML Methods to Food Industry Tasks

| Food Industry Task | Input Data Type | Recommended ML Method | Output / KPI |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fruit ripeness grading | RGB image | CNN (e.g., ResNet) | Class: Unripe / Ripe / Overripe |
| Milk adulteration detection | NIR spectrum | PCA + SVM | Class: Pure / Adulterated |
| Bakery product quality | Image + weight | ANN (MLP) | Continuous score 0–100 |
| Shelf-life prediction | Time-series sensor data | LSTM (Recurrent NN) | Remaining days |
| Meat freshness | Hyperspectral image | Random Forest | TVB-N content estimate |
| Grain variety sorting | Image features (area, color) | k-NN | Variety class |
| Fermentation endpoint | pH, temperature, CO₂ | Reinforcement Learning | Optimal time to stop |
| Oil quality grading | Chemical sensors (e-smell) | Decision Tree | Grade A / B / C |
| Process fault detection | Conveyor camera stream | Autoencoder (anomaly) | Normal / Faulty |
| Recipe optimization | Ingredient matrix | Genetic Algorithm (evolutionary) | Optimized nutrition/cost |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Confusion Matrix and Key Classification Metrics

For a binary food classifier ("Defective" vs. "Acceptable"):

| | **Predicted Defective** | **Predicted Acceptable** |
|---|---|---|
| **Actual Defective** | TP (True Positive) | FN (False Negative) |
| **Actual Acceptable** | FP (False Positive) | TN (True Negative) |

From this, the four most important KTU-exam metrics are derived:

$$ \text{Accuracy} = \frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN} $$

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP} \quad \text{(of items flagged defective, how many really are?)} $$

$$ \text{Recall (Sensitivity)} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} \quad \text{(of all defective items, how many did we catch?)} $$

$$ F_1\text{-Score} = 2 \cdot \frac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In food safety contexts, **Recall (Sensitivity)** is the most critical metric — *missing a defective product* (FN) is far more dangerous than *over-flagging a good one* (FP). Examiners love to ask which metric matters most for food safety.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: End-to-End ML + IP Pipeline for Food Quality Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Product on Conveyor] --> B[Camera and NIR Sensor Capture]
    B --> C[Image Acquisition Stage]
    C --> D[Image Preprocessing Module]
```
